description: Upgrade cell-monitoring ASIC from 12-channel to 18-channel parts, reducing slave boards per pack from 8 to 6. Two fewer PCBAs removes connectors, isoSPI transceivers, and assembly, saving ~£12/pack and shrinking the harness. Higher channel count suits long LFP blade strings. Diagnostics and balancing current preserved. Benchmarks Li Auto pack BMS slave consolidation on its 800V platform.

ideaData.title: BMS slave board channel consolidation
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline uses 12-channel cell-monitoring ASICs, needing 8 slave PCBAs per pack. The change upgrades to 18-channel monitoring ASICs, cutting slave boards from 8 to 6. Two fewer PCBAs remove their connectors, isoSPI transceivers and assembly, saving ~£12/pack and shrinking the harness. The higher channel count suits long LFP blade strings with many series cells per board. Diagnostic coverage, balancing current and ASIL partitioning are preserved on the higher-density ASIC. Board layout is re-spun for the added channels and balancing FETs; thermal of the denser board handled by copper pour and placement.
ideaData.materialGrade: 18-channel cell-monitor ASIC vs 12-channel (slave board count 8->6)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Reduces slave PCBAs from 8 to 6, removing two boards, their connectors and isoSPI transceivers from assembly and shrinking the harness. Requires a board re-spin for higher channel density and balancing FET placement; fewer board-mount and connector-mate operations. Board re-validation and balancing-FET thermal verification added before release.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Higher-channel ASIC cuts slave board count

ideaData.riskNotes: Denser boards concentrate sensing/balancing — a single board failure now affects more cells, and balancing-FET heat rises; mitigate with copper-pour thermal, robust diagnostics and partitioning. Verify ASIL coverage and balancing-current adequacy for long blade strings. Re-spin validation needed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto 800V platform BMS slave consolidation, 2023
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 ASIL-D

title: Passive-balancing resistor uprate cuts balance time
description: Increase passive-balancing bleed from 60mA to 150mA by selecting lower-value power resistors on the slave board, avoiding a switch to costly active balancing ICs. Faster end-of-charge balancing reclaims usable capacity equivalent to ~1.5% pack energy, deferring cell oversizing. Board thermal handled by copper pour. Net saves ~£5.1/pack versus active balancing. Benchmarks Xpeng BMS balancing strategy on LFP packs.

ideaData.title: Passive-balancing resistor uprate cuts balance time
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline passive balancing bleeds ~60mA per cell through slave-board resistors. The change selects lower-value power resistors to raise bleed current to 150mA, speeding end-of-charge balancing and avoiding a switch to costly active-balancing ICs. Faster balancing reclaims usable capacity equivalent to ~1.5% pack energy, deferring cell oversizing. The higher bleed dissipates more heat, handled by board copper pour and resistor power-rating selection. Net saving ~£5.1/pack versus active balancing hardware. Change is a component-value swap plus thermal layout tweak on the existing slave board; balancing algorithm timing updated in firmware.
ideaData.materialGrade: 150mA passive-balancing power resistors vs 60mA / active-balancing ICs
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Component-value swap on the existing slave board (lower-ohm, higher-power resistors) plus copper-pour thermal tweak — no new stations. Avoids active-balancing IC supply and BOM. Firmware balancing timing updated. Minimal line impact; board thermal re-verified. Resistor power-rating and derating checks added; otherwise a near drop-in change.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Faster passive balancing avoids active-balancing ICs

ideaData.riskNotes: Higher bleed current raises board temperature and standby drain; mitigate with resistor power-rating margin, copper pour and duty-cycled balancing. Excess bleed risks over-discharge of weak cells — algorithm guards needed. Validate thermal derating and balancing accuracy.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng LFP-pack BMS balancing strategy, 2022

evidenceSources.title: Passive vs active balancing cost study

title: Integrated BMS master into VCU domain controller
description: Fold the standalone BMS master controller into the central power-domain controller, sharing the MCU, power supply, and CAN/Ethernet transceivers. Eliminates a separate ECU housing, connector set, and harness branch (~£18/vehicle) and reduces software-integration variants. Cell-monitoring slaves remain local. ASIL-D partitioning maintained in the consolidated SoC. Benchmarks Xpeng/Zeekr zonal architecture absorbing BMS master functions.

ideaData.title: Integrated BMS master into VCU domain controller
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline has a standalone BMS master ECU with its own housing, MCU, power supply and CAN/Ethernet transceivers. The change folds the BMS master function into the central power-domain (zonal) controller, sharing the MCU, power supply and communication transceivers. This eliminates a separate ECU housing, connector set and harness branch (~£18/vehicle) and reduces software-integration variants. Cell-monitoring slaves remain local in the pack. ASIL-D partitioning (freedom-from-interference) is maintained within the consolidated SoC via hardware isolation and a safety hypervisor. Requires a domain controller with spare compute and certified mixed-criticality software integration.
ideaData.materialGrade: Integrated BMS-master-on-domain-controller (ASIL-D SoC) vs standalone BMS ECU
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Removes a discrete ECU: its housing, connectors and harness branch, cutting assembly points and a part number. BMS-master function becomes software on the power-domain controller. Reduces ECU variants/logistics; shifts effort to mixed-criticality software integration and validation rather than hardware lines.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Absorb BMS master into domain controller

ideaData.riskNotes: Mixed-criticality consolidation risks fault propagation and complicates ASIL-D certification; mitigate with hardware isolation, safety hypervisor and freedom-from-interference analysis. Single controller is a larger single point of failure — needs safe-state design. Software integration and timing validation heavy.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng/Zeekr zonal architecture absorbing BMS master, 2023
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 ASIL-D

title: Stamped busbar HV distribution replacing cables
description: Replace flexible HV cables between cell array, BDU, and connectors with laser-cut, formed aluminium busbars on an insulated carrier. Busbars remove crimped lugs, heat-shrink, and manual routing, cutting ~£16/vehicle and 0.9kg versus shielded cable, with lower resistance for 500A continuous. Snap-fit carrier speeds install. Benchmarks BMW Gen5 BDU busbar-based HV distribution.

ideaData.title: Stamped busbar HV distribution replacing cables
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline HV distribution between the cell array, BDU and connectors uses flexible shielded HV cables with crimped lugs and heat-shrink, manually routed. The change uses laser-cut, formed aluminium busbars mounted on an insulated (PA/PC) carrier. Rigid busbars remove crimped lugs, heat-shrink and manual routing, cutting ~£16/vehicle and 0.9kg versus shielded cable, with lower resistance for 500A continuous. The snap-fit carrier locates and insulates the busbars and speeds install. Aluminium replaces copper where ampacity allows, with clad transitions at copper interfaces. Busbar forming tooling and carrier molding are added; creepage/clearance and EMC validated.
ideaData.materialGrade: Formed aluminium busbar on insulated carrier vs shielded HV cable
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces cut/crimp/route cable operations with drop-in busbar-on-carrier snap-fit assembly, removing crimping and heat-shrink stations and speeding install. Adds busbar laser-cut/form tooling and carrier molding. Rigid pre-formed routing improves repeatability; creepage/clearance fixed by design. Busbar-joint expansion features and creepage are designed in; EMC re-verified on the carrier.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Rigid busbars replace crimped HV cables

ideaData.riskNotes: Rigid busbars tolerate less misalignment/thermal expansion than flexible cable, risking joint stress; mitigate with expansion features, validated joints and tolerance design. Aluminium-copper interfaces need clad transitions to avoid galvanic corrosion. EMC shielding and creepage validated.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Gen5 BDU busbar-based HV distribution, 2020
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100; creepage/clearance LV123

title: Bistable contactor swap for economized coil type
description: Replace continuously-energized monostable main contactors with bistable (latching) gas-filled contactors that draw coil power only during switching. Eliminates ~6W standby per contactor (two per pack), saving parasitic energy and removing the economizer circuit on the driver board. Lower self-heating extends contact life. Net hardware and energy save ~£9.3/vehicle. Benchmarks Nio swap-station packs using latching HV contactors.

ideaData.title: Bistable contactor swap for economized coil type
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline main contactors are continuously-energized monostable types, holding closed via constant coil power (with an economizer circuit reducing hold current). The change uses bistable (latching) gas-filled contactors that draw coil power only during switching transitions and stay closed mechanically/magnetically. This eliminates ~6W standby per contactor (two per pack), saving parasitic energy and removing the economizer circuit on the driver board. Lower self-heating extends contact life and reduces thermal derating. Net hardware and energy saving ~£9.3/vehicle. Requires a dual-coil (set/reset) driver and a defined fail-safe open strategy on power loss; gas-filled arc-quench supports HV DC interruption.
ideaData.materialGrade: Bistable latching gas-filled HV contactor vs monostable economized contactor
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Swaps contactor type and replaces the economizer hold-circuit with a set/reset dual-coil driver on the BDU board. Lower self-heating eases thermal layout. Similar mounting/footprint; driver firmware updated for latch logic and fail-safe. Reduces standby load test scope. Latch-state feedback diagnostics and fail-safe verification added to the BDU test plan.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Latching coil removes standby power and economizer

ideaData.riskNotes: Latching contactors must reliably open on a power-loss/crash event — fail-safe logic is safety-critical; mitigate with energy-reserve reset drive and validated fail-open. Latch-state feedback diagnostics required. Validate HV-DC interruption capacity and mechanical latch endurance.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio swap-station packs latching HV contactors, 2022
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100; ISO 26262

title: Pyro-fuse to solid-state HV disconnect
description: Replace the single-shot pyrotechnic disconnect plus separate fuse with a resettable solid-state SiC power switch performing both overcurrent interruption and crash disconnect. Removes the pyro squib, its igniter harness, and the melt-fuse, and eliminates field replacement after a trip, saving ~£13/vehicle plus warranty. Microsecond interruption improves short-circuit protection. Benchmarks AVATR/Huawei DriveONE solid-state HV protection.

ideaData.title: Pyro-fuse to solid-state HV disconnect
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline crash/overcurrent protection uses a single-shot pyrotechnic disconnect (pyro-squib severs the busbar on crash signal) plus a separate melt-fuse for overcurrent. The change replaces both with a resettable solid-state SiC power switch performing overcurrent interruption and crash disconnect in one device. It removes the pyro squib, its igniter harness and the melt-fuse, and eliminates the field replacement required after any pyro trip, saving ~£13/vehicle plus warranty. Microsecond-scale interruption improves short-circuit protection versus a melting fuse. Requires SiC MOSFET stack with thermal management, gate-drive and current sensing, plus a safe conduction-loss budget at 500A continuous.
ideaData.materialGrade: SiC solid-state HV disconnect/fuse vs pyro-fuse + melt-fuse
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Consolidates two safety parts into one SiC switch module, removing the pyro squib, igniter harness and melt-fuse from assembly. Adds SiC power-module mounting, heatsinking and gate-drive/sensing electronics. Eliminates post-trip field-replacement service; adds power-electronics thermal validation. SiC module thermal-cycling and short-circuit abuse validation added to the qualification plan.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Resettable SiC switch replaces pyro-fuse and melt-fuse

ideaData.riskNotes: SiC switch conduction losses generate heat at 500A continuous and add a complex active device into a safety-critical path; mitigate with robust thermal design, redundancy and a fail-safe open default. Must match pyro interruption speed/energy and survive short-circuit di/dt. Extensive abuse validation.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AVATR/Huawei DriveONE solid-state HV protection, 2023
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100; ISO 26262 ASIL-D

title: Integrated BDU consolidating contactor and fuse
description: Consolidate main/precharge contactors, current sensor, MSD, and fuses into one molded battery disconnect unit with an integrated busbar frame. Single housing removes inter-component cabling, individual brackets, and 14 fasteners, cutting ~£20/vehicle and assembly time. Shared shunt simplifies HV measurement. Improves sealing to IP6K9K. Benchmarks Denza/BYD integrated BDU module. Single sealed unit also eases pack-level HV servicing and diagnostics.

ideaData.title: Integrated BDU consolidating contactor and fuse
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline has main/precharge contactors, a current sensor, manual service disconnect (MSD) and fuses as discrete bracketed components linked by inter-component cabling. The change consolidates them into one molded battery disconnect unit (BDU) with an integrated busbar frame. The single housing removes inter-component cabling, individual brackets and ~14 fasteners, cutting ~£20/vehicle and assembly time. A shared shunt simplifies HV current measurement. Sealing improves to IP6K9K, and the single sealed unit eases pack-level HV servicing and diagnostics. Requires an integrated busbar/molding design, thermal management of contactors/fuses in one enclosure, and EMC/creepage layout.
ideaData.materialGrade: Integrated molded BDU with busbar frame vs discrete contactor/fuse/MSD assembly
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces multiple bracketed HV components and their inter-cabling with one pre-assembled, tested BDU dropped into the pack, removing ~14 fasteners and cabling stations and cutting assembly time. Shifts content to a sub-supplier module; pack line gains a single sealed, testable unit. Improves IP6K9K sealing.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Single BDU consolidates contactor/fuse/sensor/MSD

ideaData.riskNotes: Packing contactors and fuses in one enclosure concentrates heat and a fault could affect neighbours; mitigate with thermal partitioning, derating and creepage layout. Single sealed unit limits component-level repair. Validate IP6K9K, EMC and HV isolation of the integrated frame.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Denza/BYD integrated BDU module, 2022
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100; IP6K9K

title: HVIL loop simplification via daisy-chained connectors
description: Re-architect the High-Voltage Interlock Loop from a star topology to a single series daisy-chain through connector-integrated HVIL pins, removing dedicated interlock wires and the standalone monitoring relay. Cuts ~8 low-voltage wires and a relay (~£6/vehicle) while keeping unplug detection. Diagnostics read loop continuity at the BMS. Benchmarks Audi PPE HVIL series-loop implementation.

ideaData.title: HVIL loop simplification via daisy-chained connectors
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline High-Voltage Interlock Loop (HVIL) uses a star topology: dedicated interlock wires run from a central monitor to each HV connector, plus a standalone monitoring relay. The change re-architects HVIL into a single series daisy-chain through connector-integrated HVIL pins, so unplugging any connector breaks the loop. This removes dedicated interlock wires and the standalone relay, cutting ~8 LV wires and a relay (~£6/vehicle) while preserving unplug detection. Loop continuity is read directly at the BMS, which also diagnoses open/short faults. Requires connectors with integrated short-pins and a defined detection threshold; loop integrity and fault-localization validated.
ideaData.materialGrade: Series daisy-chain HVIL via connector short-pins vs star-topology interlock wiring
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Simplifies wiring to a single series loop through connector-integrated pins, removing ~8 dedicated interlock wires and a monitoring relay from assembly. Fewer wire terminations and a deleted relay shorten harness build. Requires connectors specified with HVIL short-pins; BMS reads continuity directly.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Series HVIL loop deletes wires and monitor relay

ideaData.riskNotes: A series loop loses the ability to identify WHICH connector opened (only that one did); mitigate with segmented sensing or service diagnostics if localization is required. Single-loop break disables HV — ensure no nuisance trips from vibration. Validate detection threshold and contact reliability.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi PPE HVIL series-loop implementation, 2023
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100 HV interlock requirements

title: Aerogel inter-cell barrier downgauge
description: Optimize inter-cell thermal-runaway barriers from 3mm full silica aerogel to a 1.5mm aerogel-mica laminate. The hybrid retains <600°C cold-face under single-cell runaway while halving costly aerogel content (~£77/m²), saving ~£14/pack. Mica adds dielectric and mechanical robustness. Validated against no-propagation requirements. Benchmarks Zeekr/CATL Qilin no-propagation barrier design. Lower flammable mass also marginally reduces total pack thermal load during abuse events.

ideaData.title: Aerogel inter-cell barrier downgauge
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline inter-cell thermal-runaway barriers are 3mm full silica-aerogel pads. The change optimizes them to a 1.5mm aerogel-mica laminate: a thin silica-aerogel layer for low thermal conductivity bonded to muscovite mica for dielectric strength and mechanical/erosion robustness against vent ejecta. The hybrid keeps the cold-face below ~600°C during single-cell runaway while halving costly aerogel content (~£77/m²), saving ~£14/pack. Mica adds high-voltage insulation and structural integrity at the reduced thickness. Validated against no-propagation requirements (GB 38031). Die-cut laminate installs as a single part between cells; thickness reduction also reclaims a little pack volume.
ideaData.materialGrade: 1.5mm silica-aerogel/mica laminate vs 3mm silica aerogel
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces a thick aerogel pad with a thinner die-cut laminate, halving aerogel use and reclaiming inter-cell space. Single die-cut part installs as before, no new station. Mica improves handling robustness (less friable than pure aerogel). Supplier laminate sourcing and propagation re-validation required.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Halve aerogel via aerogel-mica hybrid barrier

ideaData.riskNotes: Thinner barrier reduces thermal margin — must still block propagation; mitigate with validated laminate construction and full GB 38031 no-propagation testing. Erosion of the thin layer by high-velocity ejecta and long-term compression set validated. Dielectric integrity confirmed at HV.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr/CATL Qilin no-propagation barrier design, 2022
ideaData.regulatoryContext: GB 38031 no-propagation; UN R100.03

title: Mica top-cover sheet replacing potting compound
description: Replace ceramic potting over the cell-vent zone with a die-cut muscovite mica sheet bonded to the underside of the pack cover. Mica withstands >1000°C vent ejecta and installs in one pick-place, eliminating the messy potting dispense and cure station, saving ~£10/pack and takt. Also electrically insulates the cover. Benchmarks BYD blade pack mica top-shield.

ideaData.title: Mica top-cover sheet replacing potting compound
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline protects the cell-vent zone with a dispensed-and-cured ceramic potting compound over the cover. The change replaces it with a die-cut muscovite mica sheet bonded to the underside of the pack cover. Mica withstands >1000°C vent ejecta and high-voltage flashover, installs in one pick-and-place, and eliminates the messy potting dispense and cure station, saving ~£10/pack and takt. It also electrically insulates the metallic cover from the cell-vent gases/plasma. Requires a die-cut mica part shaped to the cover, an adhesive bond, and validation that the sheet survives directional ejecta without dislodging. Cleaner process than potting with no cure dwell.
ideaData.materialGrade: Die-cut muscovite mica top-shield vs dispensed ceramic potting
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the ceramic-potting dispense and cure station (messy, slow) and replaces it with a single die-cut mica pick-and-place bond. Cuts takt and removes cure dwell and dispense maintenance. Adds mica die-cutting and an adhesive step; cleaner line with less rework.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Mica sheet replaces messy ceramic potting

ideaData.riskNotes: Mica sheet must stay bonded and cover all vent paths under directional high-velocity ejecta; mitigate with adequate adhesive, edge coverage and abuse-test validation. Mica can delaminate/crack if mishandled. Confirm HV flashover and >1000°C survival over service life.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD Blade pack mica top-shield, 2021

evidenceSources.title: BYD Blade pack cover mica shield teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: GB 38031; UN R100.03 venting

title: Directional venting channel cast into tray
description: Integrate a directional venting plenum into the HPDC tray casting so runaway gases route to a single explosion vent, removing the bolted-on sheet-metal venting duct and its gaskets. Cast channels delete ~9 parts and their sealing, saving ~£11/pack, and improve flow to keep internal pressure below cover-burst. Single membrane vent remains. Benchmarks Zeekr cast-in venting architecture.

ideaData.title: Directional venting channel cast into tray
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline routes thermal-runaway gases via a bolted-on sheet-metal venting duct with gaskets to a single explosion vent. The change integrates a directional venting plenum directly into the HPDC tray casting, so runaway gases route through cast channels to one explosion vent, removing the bolted duct and its gaskets. Cast channels delete ~9 parts and their sealing, saving ~£11/pack, and improve flow to keep internal pressure below the cover-burst threshold during a runaway event. A single membrane/explosion vent remains. Requires casting the plenum geometry (cores) into the tray, flow/CFD validation of gas routing, and ensuring the channels survive high-temperature ejecta without breaching the cell zone.
ideaData.materialGrade: Cast-in directional venting plenum (ADC12 HPDC tray) vs bolted sheet-metal duct
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Casts the venting plenum into the existing HPDC tray (added cores/geometry), deleting a bolted sheet-metal duct, its gaskets and ~9 parts plus their fastening/sealing. Fewer assembly operations and leak paths; adds casting complexity and CFD-validated channel geometry. One explosion vent remains.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Cast venting channels delete bolted duct and gaskets

ideaData.riskNotes: Cast channels add die complexity and must survive high-temperature, high-velocity ejecta without cracking into the cell zone; mitigate with CFD flow design, wall-thickness margin and abuse testing. Pressure must stay below cover-burst. Casting porosity in channel walls inspected.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr cast-in venting architecture, 2023

title: Single explosion vent valve replacing multi-vent
description: Consolidate four small pressure-equalization vents plus one burst vent into a single dual-function vent valve that breathes for altitude/temperature and bursts at 25kPa during runaway. Removes 4 vent assemblies and their seals (~£5.1/pack) and reduces IP-test leak points. Validated for water ingress and gas discharge. Benchmarks AITO/Luxeed pack venting consolidation.

ideaData.title: Single explosion vent valve replacing multi-vent
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline uses four small pressure-equalization vents (breathe for altitude/temperature, IP-rated membranes) plus one separate burst vent for runaway. The change consolidates these into a single dual-function vent valve that both breathes for normal pressure/altitude swings and bursts at ~25kPa during a thermal-runaway event. This removes 4 vent assemblies and their seals (~£5.1/pack) and reduces IP-test leak points. The valve uses a Gore-type ePTFE membrane for breathing with an integrated rupture element for emergency discharge. Validated for water ingress (IP67/IP6K9K) and runaway gas discharge rate. One mounting location simplifies sealing and assembly.
ideaData.materialGrade: Dual-function breathe/burst vent (ePTFE + rupture element) vs 4 equalization + 1 burst vent
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Reduces five vent installations to one, removing 4 vent assemblies, their seals and mounting/leak-test points, cutting assembly and IP-test scope. Single dual-function part simplifies the cover. Adds qualification of the combined breathe/burst valve; fewer sealing interfaces lowers leak rework. Combined breathe/burst valve qualification and IP/discharge testing added to release.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One dual-function vent replaces five vents

ideaData.riskNotes: A single vent must reliably do two jobs — slow breathing AND high-flow burst — and its failure removes both functions; mitigate with proven dual-membrane design and redundancy sizing. Validate burst pressure consistency, water ingress and discharge area for worst-case runaway gas rate.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AITO/Luxeed pack venting consolidation, 2023
ideaData.regulatoryContext: GB 38031; UN R100; IP67/IP6K9K

title: Intumescent fire barrier coating on pack cover
description: Replace a bonded ceramic-fiber fire blanket under the pack cover with a sprayed intumescent coating that chars and expands at 200°C. Spray application removes the cut-blanket part, its adhesive, and lay-up labour, saving ~£7.6/pack and 0.6kg while meeting the 5-minute no-flame-penetration requirement. Uniform coverage on complex covers. Benchmarks Mercedes pack fire-protection coating trials.

ideaData.title: Intumescent fire barrier coating on pack cover
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline fire protection under the pack cover is a bonded ceramic-fiber fire blanket (cut, adhesive-applied, laid up by hand). The change is a sprayed intumescent coating that chars and expands at ~200°C to form an insulating barrier. Spray application removes the cut-blanket part, its adhesive and the lay-up labour, saving ~£7.6/pack and 0.6kg, while meeting the 5-minute no-flame-penetration requirement. The coating gives uniform coverage on complex cover geometry that a flat blanket cannot conform to. Requires a spray booth/robot, cure oven and dry-film-thickness control; coating adhesion and expansion ratio validated to the flame-penetration spec.
ideaData.materialGrade: Sprayed intumescent fire coating vs bonded ceramic-fiber blanket
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces cut-blanket lay-up with robotic spray plus cure, removing a manual labour step and the blanket part/adhesive. Adds a spray booth, DFT measurement and cure dwell. Gives uniform coverage on complex covers and lowers mass; overspray/masking and cure time are new controls.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Sprayed intumescent replaces cut fire blanket

ideaData.riskNotes: Coating must achieve consistent dry-film thickness and expansion to pass the 5-minute flame test; thin spots risk burn-through — mitigate with DFT control and spray-pattern validation. Adhesion over service life and thermal cycling verified. Overspray handling and cure energy added.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes pack fire-protection coating trials, 2022
ideaData.regulatoryContext: GB 38031 5-min egress; UN R100.03

title: Structural cell-to-pack bonding for floor stiffness
description: Use structural adhesive to bond blade cells between tray floor and cover so the cell array carries shear load (honeycomb effect), allowing deletion of internal cross-beams and reducing tray wall thickness. Removes ~8 cross-members (~£26/vehicle, 11kg) while raising pack bending stiffness ~25%. Adhesive doubles as thermal path. Benchmarks CATL Qilin/BYD blade load-bearing cell integration.

ideaData.title: Structural cell-to-pack bonding for floor stiffness
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline blade cells sit in a tray with internal cross-beams carrying structural load and a thicker tray wall. The change uses structural adhesive to bond blade cells between the tray floor and the cover so the bonded cell array carries shear load like a honeycomb sandwich panel. This load-sharing allows deletion of ~8 internal cross-members and a thinner tray wall, removing ~£26/vehicle and 11kg while raising pack bending stiffness ~25%. The adhesive also serves as a thermal path to the floor cold plate. Requires a high-shear-modulus structural adhesive, controlled bond-line thickness, surface prep, and validation of fatigue/crash load transfer through the cell array.
ideaData.materialGrade: Structural adhesive cell-to-floor/cover bonding (load-bearing blade array)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds precise structural-adhesive dispense and cure with bond-line and surface-prep control, but deletes ~8 cross-members and downgauges the tray, cutting parts and mass. Bonded sandwich is built in fixture; cure time and bond verification (pull/peel) become key stations. Disassembly for service harder.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Load-bearing bonded cells delete cross-beams

ideaData.riskNotes: Cells now bear structural load — adhesive fatigue, crash load-path and bond integrity are safety-critical, and cell replacement is very hard; mitigate with validated structural adhesive, bond-line control, crash testing and a defined service strategy. Thermal cycling of the bond and recyclability assessed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: CATL Qilin / BYD Blade load-bearing cell integration, 2022
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100.03; crash R94/R95; GB 38031

title: Extruded crash rail replacing welded box section
description: Replace the multi-piece welded steel side crash structure with a single 6082-T6 aluminium multi-cavity extrusion. The extrusion delivers tuned crush in one profile, removing ~14 stampings and ~60 spot welds, cutting ~£22/vehicle and rework. Multi-cell cavities absorb side-pole intrusion protecting cells. Benchmarks Porsche/PPE pack side-extrusion crash member. Recyclable single-alloy profile further lowers end-of-life processing cost versus mixed steel.

ideaData.title: Extruded crash rail replacing welded box section
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline pack side crash structure is a multi-piece welded steel box section (stampings + spot welds). The change is a single 6082-T6 aluminium multi-cavity extrusion that delivers tuned crush behavior in one profile. The extrusion removes ~14 stampings and ~60 spot welds, cutting ~£22/vehicle and weld rework, while its multiple cavities progressively absorb side-pole intrusion energy to protect the cells. The single-alloy aluminium profile is more recyclable than mixed-gauge steel, lowering end-of-life processing cost. Requires extrusion dies, cut-to-length, and CNC of end features; crush performance and joint-to-tray integrity validated against side-impact load cases.
ideaData.materialGrade: 6082-T6 aluminium multi-cavity extrusion vs welded steel box section
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces ~14 stampings and ~60 spot welds with one extruded, cut-to-length, end-machined profile, removing weld stations and rework. Adds extrusion tooling and CNC end-prep. Single-alloy profile simplifies recycling. Joint to tray (weld/bolt/SPR) qualified for crash load transfer. Crash-load validation of the extrusion-to-tray joint added to the structural sign-off.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One extrusion replaces 14 stampings and 60 welds

ideaData.riskNotes: Aluminium extrusion crush tuning must match steel's energy absorption for side-pole protection of cells; mitigate with multi-cavity wall optimization and crash validation. Aluminium-to-tray joints and corrosion at dissimilar interfaces managed. Extrusion tolerance and weldability of 6082 confirmed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche/PPE pack side-extrusion crash member, 2021
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R95 side impact; R100

title: Constrained-layer damping patch reduction via tuned ribs
description: Remove bonded constrained-layer damping patches from the pack cover by casting tuned stiffening ribs that shift the first cover mode above 250Hz, away from road-noise excitation. Deletes ~1.2m² of damping material (~£6.8/vehicle) and the manual application step while meeting interior NVH targets. Modal validated by shaker test. Benchmarks Audi pack-cover NVH rib optimization.

ideaData.title: Constrained-layer damping patch reduction via tuned ribs
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline reduces pack-cover boom/resonance with bonded constrained-layer damping (CLD) patches (visco-elastic + constraining foil) applied by hand. The change deletes the CLD patches by casting tuned stiffening ribs into the cover that shift the first cover bending mode above 250Hz, away from dominant road-noise excitation. This removes ~1.2m² of damping material (~£6.8/vehicle) and the manual application step while meeting interior NVH targets. The ribbed cover is validated by modal/shaker test to confirm the frequency shift. Requires cover redesign (cast or stamped ribs) and FEA modal optimization; mass-neutral or lighter than the deleted damping material.
ideaData.materialGrade: Cast/stamped tuned stiffening ribs vs bonded constrained-layer damping patches
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Removes ~1.2m² of damping material and a manual patch-application step by integrating stiffening ribs into the cover tool (cast or stamp). No added station — the rib geometry is in the existing cover forming. Requires modal FEA and shaker validation; tooling revision for ribs.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Tuned ribs replace bonded damping patches

ideaData.riskNotes: Ribs raise modal frequency but add little broadband damping — if real road excitation is broadband, some boom may remain; mitigate with modal+road-noise validation and localized damping only where needed. Tooling change cost and cover stiffness/fit re-checked. Confirm interior NVH targets.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi pack-cover NVH rib optimization, 2022

evidenceSources.title: Pack-cover NVH rib vs CLD study

title: Cell terminal aluminium-clad-copper transition
description: Use ultrasonically clad aluminium-copper transition terminals so all-aluminium busbars can join copper cell tabs without bimetallic corrosion or costly all-copper busbars. Aluminium busbars cut conductor cost ~£2/kWh versus copper at equal ampacity. Clad joint avoids galvanic failure in humid conditions. Welds reliably to both metals. Benchmarks Zeekr busbar bimetallic terminal design.

ideaData.title: Cell terminal aluminium-clad-copper transition
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline interconnects use all-copper busbars to join copper cell tabs, or accept galvanic risk when mixing metals. The change uses ultrasonically clad aluminium-copper (Al-Cu) transition terminals: a solid-state-bonded bimetallic piece that lets all-aluminium busbars connect to copper cell tabs without a direct galvanic Al-Cu junction or costly all-copper busbars. Aluminium busbars cut conductor cost ~£2/kWh versus copper at equal ampacity (larger cross-section offsets lower conductivity). The clad joint, made by ultrasonic/explosion welding, avoids galvanic corrosion in humid conditions and welds reliably to both metals (Al-side to Al busbar, Cu-side to Cu tab). Requires clad-terminal supply and tuned welding per metal.
ideaData.materialGrade: Ultrasonically clad Al-Cu transition terminal enabling aluminium busbars
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Enables aluminium busbars (cheaper conductor) by adding a clad Al-Cu transition piece welded Al-to-Al and Cu-to-Cu, avoiding direct bimetallic joints. Welding parameters set per side; busbar stamping switches to aluminium. Adds a clad-terminal part but removes galvanic-corrosion field risk. Joint-resistance and humid-corrosion testing of the clad terminal added to validation.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Aluminium busbars via clad Al-Cu transition

ideaData.riskNotes: Clad interface quality is critical — a poor Al-Cu bond raises resistance and corrosion risk; mitigate with qualified ultrasonic/explosion-clad terminals and joint resistance testing. Aluminium's larger cross-section and weld parameters must meet ampacity and thermal cycling. Validate humid/salt durability.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr busbar bimetallic transition terminal design, 2022

title: Prismatic 173Ah cell upsizing to cut part count
description: Move from 100Ah to 173Ah prismatic LFP cells, reducing cell count for a 90kWh pack from ~280 to ~162. Fewer cells means ~42% fewer busbars, welds, sense lines, and holders, saving ~£41/vehicle in interconnect and assembly while raising volumetric efficiency. Larger format keeps £/kWh flat. Thermal managed by bottom cooling. Benchmarks CATL/Denza large-format prismatic cell adoption.

ideaData.title: Prismatic 173Ah cell upsizing to cut part count
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline uses 100Ah prismatic LFP cells — a 90kWh pack needs ~280 cells. The change moves to 173Ah prismatic LFP cells, cutting cell count to ~162 for the same 90kWh. Fewer, larger cells mean ~42% fewer busbars, laser welds, sense lines and holders, saving ~£41/vehicle in interconnect and assembly while raising volumetric efficiency (less inactive packaging per kWh). Larger format keeps £/kWh roughly flat (same chemistry, larger jelly-roll). Thermal is managed by bottom cooling sized for the bigger cell. Requires new cell-handling/welding fixtures for the larger format and revised BMS channel mapping for fewer, higher-capacity cells.
ideaData.materialGrade: 173Ah prismatic LFP cell vs 100Ah prismatic LFP
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Larger cells cut count ~42%, removing ~42% of busbars, welds, sense lines and holders and shortening interconnect takt. Requires new handling/welding fixtures sized for 173Ah cells and revised cell-stacking. BMS channel mapping updated. Higher per-cell energy raises thermal-management sizing. Large-format thermal-gradient and swelling-preload validation added before launch.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Fewer larger cells slash interconnect content

ideaData.riskNotes: Larger cells store more energy each, raising single-cell runaway severity and demanding robust propagation barriers and bottom cooling; mitigate with validated thermal design and inter-cell barriers. Fewer cells reduce BMS granularity. Validate large-format thermal gradients and swelling preload.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: CATL/Denza large-format prismatic cell adoption, 2022

title: Cell can material from steel to aluminium
description: Replace nickel-plated steel cylindrical cans with deep-drawn aluminium cans. Aluminium cuts cell mass ~12g per 4680 (~7 Wh/kg gain) and improves heat rejection from the can wall, reducing cooling demand. Avoids nickel plating line and its effluent treatment, netting ~£0.8/cell. Laser-weld closure validated. Benchmarks BMW Gen6 large-cylindrical aluminium-can cell. Single-alloy aluminium body also simplifies cell recycling and shredding recovery streams.

ideaData.title: Cell can material from steel to aluminium
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline cylindrical cells use nickel-plated steel cans (drawn steel, Ni plating for corrosion/weldability). The change uses deep-drawn aluminium cans for the 4680 format. Aluminium cuts cell mass ~12g per cell (~7 Wh/kg gain) and improves heat rejection through the higher-conductivity can wall, easing cooling demand. It avoids the nickel-plating line and its effluent treatment, netting ~£0.8/cell. Closure is by laser welding validated for aluminium. The single-alloy aluminium body simplifies cell recycling and shredding recovery versus plated steel. Requires aluminium deep-draw tooling, revised can-to-cap laser-weld parameters, and validation of can strength/internal-pressure containment for the large format.
ideaData.materialGrade: Deep-drawn aluminium can vs nickel-plated steel can (4680)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Switches can forming to aluminium deep-draw and eliminates the nickel-plating line and effluent treatment. Laser-weld closure parameters retuned for aluminium. Lighter cans improve handling; better heat rejection eases pack cooling. Validate can burst strength and weld hermeticity for the large format.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Aluminium can saves mass, plating line and aids recycling

ideaData.riskNotes: Aluminium cans have lower strength and different burst behavior than steel, and aluminium laser-weld hermeticity is harder; mitigate with wall-thickness design, validated weld process and pressure testing. Confirm vent/burst direction and abuse performance for the large 4680 format.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Gen6 large-cylindrical aluminium-can cell, 2025

evidenceSources.title: BMW Gen6 cylindrical cell can briefing

title: Single-piece stamped cooling-plate manifold
description: Integrate the coolant inlet/outlet manifold into the stamped cold-plate halves rather than welding on separate machined manifold blocks and barbed fittings. Forming the distribution gallery in-die removes 2 machined blocks, 4 brazed joints, and their leak risk per plate, saving ~£6.8/pack and a leak-test rework loop. Quick-connect ports overmolded. Benchmarks Li Auto integrated cold-plate manifold.

ideaData.title: Single-piece stamped cooling-plate manifold
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline cold plate has the coolant inlet/outlet manifold as separate machined blocks with barbed fittings welded onto the stamped plate. The change forms the distribution gallery directly into the stamped cold-plate halves in-die, integrating the manifold. This removes 2 machined manifold blocks, 4 brazed/welded joints and their leak risk per plate, saving ~£6.8/pack and a leak-test rework loop. Overmolded quick-connect ports replace barbed fittings. The in-die gallery is shaped during the existing stamping operation, so no added forming station. Requires die revision for the gallery geometry, flow-balance validation across cells, and proof-pressure testing of the integrated channels.
ideaData.materialGrade: In-die integrated cold-plate manifold (stamped aluminium) vs welded machined manifold blocks
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Forms the manifold gallery during existing cold-plate stamping, deleting 2 machined blocks and 4 braze/weld joints and their leak-test/rework. Adds die revision; overmolded quick-connect ports replace barb fittings. Fewer joints cut leak escapes and assembly time. CFD flow-balance and proof-pressure verification of the in-die gallery added to the plate line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: In-die manifold deletes machined blocks and welds

ideaData.riskNotes: In-die gallery must maintain flow balance to all cells and survive coolant pressure without the robustness of machined blocks; mitigate with CFD flow design, proof-pressure test and stamping-quality control. Overmolded ports' seal integrity validated. Die-revision cost amortised over volume.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto integrated cold-plate manifold, 2022

title: Module-less BMS sensing via flex-PCB cell connecting system
description: Replace discrete voltage/temperature sense wires and connectors with a single laser-structured flexible-PCB Cell Contacting System laminated to the busbar carrier. The FPC integrates sense traces, NTCs, and fusible links, deleting ~120 crimped wires per pack and their connectors, saving ~£15/vehicle and assembly faults. Suits CTP layouts. Benchmarks Mercedes/CATL flex-foil CCS in cell-to-pack designs.

ideaData.title: Module-less BMS sensing via flex-PCB cell connecting system
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline cell sensing uses discrete voltage/temperature wires and connectors crimped to each cell group. The change replaces them with a single laser-structured flexible-PCB Cell Contacting System (FPC CCS) laminated to the busbar carrier. The FPC integrates voltage sense traces, NTC temperature sensors and fusible links in one flat circuit, deleting ~120 crimped wires per pack and their connectors, saving ~£15/vehicle and reducing assembly faults. The flat, thin CCS suits cell-to-pack layouts with long cell rows. The FPC is laser-structured (additive/subtractive copper on polyimide) and bonded over the busbars; sense points are laser-welded or spring-contacted. Requires FPC supply, lamination tooling and validation of contact reliability and fuse calibration.
ideaData.materialGrade: Laser-structured flex-PCB Cell Contacting System (polyimide/Cu) vs discrete sense wires
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces ~120 crimped sense wires and connectors with one laminated FPC carrier placed in a single operation, removing crimping stations and their fault modes. Integrates NTCs and fusible links into the flex circuit. Adds FPC supply and lamination; sense-point welding/contact and fuse calibration validated. Ideal for CTP.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Single flex CCS replaces discrete sense wiring

ideaData.riskNotes: FPC contact and fusible-link reliability over vibration/thermal cycling is critical — a damaged flex can lose multiple sense channels; mitigate with robust lamination, strain relief and contact-weld inspection. Repair of a failed FPC is harder than one wire. Validate fuse calibration and EMC.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes/CATL flex-foil CCS in cell-to-pack designs, 2022
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 sensing integrity

title: Bonded composite pack underbody shield
description: Replace the bolted 3mm aluminium underbody impact shield with an adhesively-bonded glass-fiber SMC composite plate. SMC absorbs road-debris and ground-strike energy at lower mass (~4kg saved) and removes ~24 fasteners and their weld-nuts, cutting ~£13/vehicle and assembly time. Composite avoids galvanic corrosion at the tray interface. Benchmarks Porsche Taycan composite pack undershield.

ideaData.title: Bonded composite pack underbody shield
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline underbody impact shield is a bolted 3mm aluminium plate protecting the pack from road debris and ground strike. The change is an adhesively-bonded glass-fiber SMC (sheet-molding compound) composite plate. The SMC absorbs road-debris and ground-strike energy at lower mass (~4kg saved) and removes ~24 fasteners and their weld-nuts, cutting ~£13/vehicle and assembly time. Bonding avoids galvanic corrosion at the aluminium-tray interface (no dissimilar-metal contact). SMC is compression-molded to net shape with integrated ribs. Requires SMC tooling, a structural-adhesive bond process with surface prep, and validation of impact/penetration resistance and bond durability under road and thermal loads.
ideaData.materialGrade: Glass-fiber SMC composite undershield (bonded) vs bolted 3mm aluminium plate
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces a bolted aluminium plate with a compression-molded SMC plate bonded by structural adhesive, removing ~24 fasteners and weld-nuts and their install stations. Adds SMC molding tooling and an adhesive bond/cure with surface prep. Lighter, corrosion-isolated; bonded joint replaces mechanical fastening.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Bonded SMC shield deletes fasteners and saves mass

ideaData.riskNotes: Composite impact/penetration behavior differs from ductile aluminium — risk of cracking on hard ground strike; mitigate with glass-fiber content, rib design and impact validation. Bonded joint must survive road vibration, thermal cycling and moisture — surface prep and adhesive durability critical. Repair replaces whole panel.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Taycan composite pack undershield, 2020
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100 ground-strike/underbody integrity

title: 800V pack reconfiguration cutting conductor mass
description: Re-series the pack from 400V to 800V architecture, halving current for equal power and allowing busbar and HV-cable cross-section to drop ~50%. Lighter conductors save ~£18/vehicle in copper/aluminium and reduce I²R losses for ~1.5% range gain. Existing cells re-grouped, no chemistry change. Fast-charge to 5C enabled. Benchmarks Zeekr/AITO 800V platform conductor downsizing.

ideaData.title: 800V pack reconfiguration cutting conductor mass
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline pack is a 400V architecture. The change re-series the cells to 800V (more cells in series, fewer parallel), halving current for equal power, which lets busbar and HV-cable cross-sections drop ~50%. Lighter conductors save ~£18/vehicle in copper/aluminium and cut I²R losses for ~1.5% range gain; the cells are simply re-grouped (no chemistry change). 800V enables up to 5C fast charging and reuses the same cells. Requires 800V-rated busbars, contactors, connectors and insulation (creepage/clearance), an 800V-capable BDU, and DC-DC/OBC compatibility. Cell-group re-balancing and BMS series-string handling updated; HV safety isolation re-validated to the higher voltage.
ideaData.materialGrade: 800V series reconfiguration with downsized Cu/Al conductors vs 400V
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Re-groups existing cells to 800V series and halves conductor cross-section, cutting busbar/cable copper-aluminium content and mass. Requires 800V-rated contactors, connectors and insulation (larger creepage) and an 800V BDU. Same cells/chemistry; HV isolation and safety testing re-validated to higher voltage. Insulation, creepage and HVIL re-validation added across all 800V-rated HV components.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: 800V halves current, downsizing conductors

ideaData.riskNotes: 800V raises insulation, creepage/clearance and arc-flash requirements and needs 800V-rated HV components throughout; mitigate with qualified 800V parts, increased isolation and updated safety/service procedures. DC-DC/OBC and charging compatibility required. Re-validate HVIL and isolation monitoring.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr/AITO 800V platform conductor downsizing, 2023
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100 HV; LV123 creepage/clearance

title: Laser-notched fusible busbar replacing discrete cell fuses
description: Integrate per-cell overcurrent protection by laser-notching a fusible neck directly into the stamped busbar connecting each cylindrical cell, removing discrete wire-fuses or fuse-bonds. The calibrated notch clears a shorted cell at ~30A, deleting thousands of separate fuse parts and bond operations, saving ~£6/vehicle and takt. Notch geometry validated by abuse test. Benchmarks Tesla fusible-busbar concept used at Volvo.

ideaData.title: Laser-notched fusible busbar replacing discrete cell fuses
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline per-cell overcurrent protection uses discrete wire-fuses or fuse-bonds at each cylindrical cell. The change laser-notches a fusible neck directly into the stamped busbar tab connecting each cell, integrating the fuse into the busbar. The calibrated notch (reduced cross-section) clears a shorted cell at ~30A by melting, deleting thousands of separate fuse parts and their bonding operations, saving ~£6/vehicle and takt. The notch geometry is laser-cut into the busbar in-line and validated by abuse testing for consistent clearing current. Requires precise notch dimensional control (sets fuse rating), busbar material/thickness selection, and verification that clearing isolates a faulted cell without nuisance trips at normal current.
ideaData.materialGrade: Laser-notched fusible busbar neck (stamped Cu/Al) vs discrete wire-fuses
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Integrates the fuse into the busbar via in-line laser notching, deleting thousands of discrete fuse parts and fuse-bond operations and cutting interconnect takt. Adds a controlled laser-notch step with dimensional inspection (notch sets clearing current). Fewer parts and welds; abuse-test calibration required.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Fusible busbar neck replaces discrete cell fuses

ideaData.riskNotes: Notch dimension precisely sets clearing current — variation risks nuisance trips or failure to clear; mitigate with tight laser-notch control, vision inspection and abuse-test validation. Notch must not fatigue under vibration/thermal cycling at normal current. Validate fault isolation without arcing.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Tesla fusible-busbar concept used at Volvo, 2022

evidenceSources.title: Integrated fusible link in cell busbar

title: Reusable swap-pack standardized interface for scale
description: Standardize a swappable pack mechanical/electrical/coolant interface across vehicle lines so a single pack variant amortises tooling and qualification across higher volume. Shared latching busbar, blind-mate HV connector, and coolant quick-disconnect cut per-program engineering and enable battery-as-a-service. Standardization trims ~£47/vehicle via volume and reduced variants. Benchmarks Nio third-generation swap-station pack interface.

ideaData.title: Reusable swap-pack standardized interface for scale
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline packs are bespoke per vehicle line, fragmenting tooling and qualification volume. The change standardizes a swappable pack mechanical/electrical/coolant interface across multiple vehicle lines so a single pack variant amortises tooling and qualification across higher volume and enables battery-as-a-service (swap). A shared latching busbar (HV), blind-mate HV connector and coolant quick-disconnect form the standardized interface that mates in the swap station. Standardization cuts per-program engineering and reduces variants, trimming ~£47/vehicle via volume and reuse. Requires a rigorously defined interface spec (mechanical datums, blind-mate tolerances, sealing, HV/coolant sequencing), cross-line packaging compatibility, and swap-cycle durability validation of the connectors and latches.
ideaData.materialGrade: Standardized swap-pack interface (latching busbar, blind-mate HV connector, coolant QD)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Consolidates pack variants to a shared interface across lines, amortising tooling and qualification over higher volume and cutting per-program engineering. Standard blind-mate HV/coolant connectors and latching busbar enable automated swap. Adds interface-spec control and swap-cycle durability testing; reduces unique part numbers and validation scope.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Standardized swap interface amortises tooling across lines

ideaData.riskNotes: A frozen shared interface constrains future pack/vehicle design and the blind-mate HV/coolant connectors face thousands of swap cycles — wear, sealing and contact reliability are critical; mitigate with robust connector design, swap-cycle endurance testing and a governed interface spec. Cross-line packaging compromises managed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio third-generation swap-station pack interface, 2023

evidenceSources.title: Nio Gen3 swap-station pack interface briefing

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN R100; GB/T swap-interface standards

title: Continuous hairpin wave-winding to cut stator copper scrap
description: Replace discrete U-pin hairpin insertion with a continuous flat-wire wave-winding fed from a single coil, eliminating ~6 welding stations and cutting per-slot laser-weld joints from 96 to 8. Copper offcut scrap drops ~14%, raising slot fill from 62% to 73% and shaving ~0.18 kg copper per stator. At £7.6/kg copper plus reduced weld rework this saves ~£9.3/unit. Benchmarked against BMW Gen5 eDrive flat-wire stators.

ideaData.title: Continuous hairpin wave-winding to cut stator copper scrap
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 800V hairpin stator uses discrete U-pins of ~2.0×4.0 mm rectangular enamelled copper (PAI/PEEK insulation) inserted per slot, with 96 crown-side laser-welded joints across 48 slots. This idea feeds a single continuous flat-wire conductor formed into a wave winding by servo bending heads, so each phase is one length and crown joints fall to 8. Continuous forming raises slot fill from 62% to 73%, trims ~0.18 kg of Cu OF-grade copper per stator and removes ~6 fibre-laser weld cells. Fewer keyhole welds cut copper splatter scrap ~14% and eliminate per-pin laser cycle time on the M250-35A laminated core.
ideaData.materialGrade: OF copper flat-wire 2.0×4.0 mm, PAI/PEEK enamel; M250-35A core
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes ~6 fibre-laser weld stations and 88 of 96 per-slot weld cycles, cutting weld-cell takt and consumable shielding gas. Continuous wave forming on servo benders raises slot fill to 73%; fewer joints lift first-pass weld yield and reduce copper offcut scrap ~14%, lowering per-stator copper mass 0.18 kg.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Eliminating discrete-pin welds and offcut scrap while raising slot fill cuts copper and weld-cell cost.

ideaData.riskNotes: Continuous wave forming demands tight spring-back control on flat wire; insulation abrasion at bend radii must be PD-tested to 800V. Mitigate with calibrated bend dies, in-line enamel integrity scan, and DV on slot-fill thermal margin. Tooling change-over per pole count limits flexibility.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Gen5 eDrive flat-wire stator, 2021; benchmarked in 2022 teardown

evidenceSources.title: BMW iX Gen5 eDrive stator teardown
Hairpin vs continuous wave winding cost study

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100 HV insulation; IEC 60034 PD limits

title: Segmented stator core with bonded laminations vs interlocked
description: Switch from full-ring interlocked laminations to segmented (T-shaped) stator cores using thin 0.20 mm NO20 electrical steel with adhesive back-lack bonding. Segmentation lifts steel utilization from ~58% to ~88% on the press, recovering ~1.1 kg silicon steel per core, while back-lack reduces iron loss ~6%. Tooling cost drops on smaller progressive dies. Net ~£6/unit on material plus efficiency gain. Benchmarked against Mercedes eATS 2.0 segmented stator.

ideaData.title: Segmented stator core with bonded laminations vs interlocked
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline stator stamps full-ring laminations from 0.20 mm NO20 non-oriented silicon steel and interlocks them, with the inter-tooth web wasting strip material. This idea stamps T-shaped segments nested on the coil, lifting steel utilisation from ~58% to ~88% and recovering ~1.1 kg of NO20 per core. Segments are joined by adhesive back-lack (B-stage epoxy on the laminate) cured under press, replacing mechanical interlock dimples that bridge eddy paths; this drops iron loss ~6%. Smaller progressive dies on segments lower tool cost and allow pre-wound needle winding of each tooth for higher fill on the 800V machine.
ideaData.materialGrade: 0.20 mm NO20 non-oriented electrical steel, back-lack epoxy bonded
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Nested T-segment stamping raises strip utilisation to ~88%, cutting scrap skeleton. Smaller progressive dies reduce tooling spend versus full-ring tools. Back-lack bonding replaces interlock dimples, removing a stress-riser and enabling pre-wound segment assembly for higher slot fill and lower iron loss.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Segmentation slashes lamination scrap and tooling while bonding cuts iron loss.

ideaData.riskNotes: Segment joints can raise vibration/NVH and require robust back-lack bond shear strength at 180°C; ring concentricity and air-gap roundness must hold. Mitigate with bonded-stack pull tests, segment indexing fixtures, and modal validation. Adhesive cure adds process control burden.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes eATS 2.0 segmented stator, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Thinner NO grade electrical steel for 800V high-frequency stator
description: Adopt 0.25 mm NO25 non-oriented steel in place of 0.35 mm grade for the 800V stator running at higher electrical frequency. Eddy-current iron loss falls ~22% at 16 kHz fundamental, allowing ~3% length reduction of the active stack for equal torque, trimming ~0.9 kg steel and ~0.12 kg copper per machine. Material premium is offset by stack shortening for ~£5.1/unit net. Benchmarked against Porsche Taycan PSM 800V stator.

ideaData.title: Thinner NO grade electrical steel for 800V high-frequency stator
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 800V stator stack uses 0.35 mm (M330-35A-class) non-oriented steel; at a 16 kHz fundamental the classical eddy-current loss scales with thickness squared. This idea adopts 0.25 mm NO25 high-silicon non-oriented steel, cutting eddy iron loss ~22%. The recovered efficiency lets the active stack shorten ~3% for equal torque, trimming ~0.9 kg steel and ~0.12 kg copper end-turns per machine. The thinner gauge raises stamping stroke count and burr control demands, but net material savings plus efficiency offset the per-kg premium of NO25, netting ~£5.1/unit on the high-frequency PSM stator.
ideaData.materialGrade: 0.25 mm NO25 non-oriented silicon steel (vs 0.35 mm M330-35A)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Thinner 0.25 mm gauge increases lamination count and press strokes per stack and tightens burr/flatness control; carbide die edges and tighter clearances needed. Offset by ~3% shorter stack reducing total laminations and shorter copper end-turns, lowering machining and winding material.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Lower-loss thin gauge enables stack shortening that recovers steel and copper mass.

ideaData.riskNotes: Thinner gauge premium and higher stamping cost can erode savings if stroke rate drops; burr height must stay <15 µm to avoid inter-lamination shorts. Mitigate with high-speed carbide tooling, surface-insulation coating QA, and loss-budget validation at 16 kHz. Supply of NO25 must scale.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Taycan PSM 800V stator, 2020; benchmarked in 2021 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Porsche Taycan rear PSM stator teardown

title: Laser-skinned hairpin enamel removal replacing mechanical stripping
description: Replace mechanical/abrasive enamel stripping of flat-wire hairpin ends with galvo laser ablation, removing PEEK/PAI insulation in <0.4 s per pin with zero conductor nicking. Scrap from over-stripped pins drops from ~1.8% to ~0.3%, improving weld yield and removing one abrasive-media consumable line. Saves ~£2.7/stator plus reduced warranty on weld voids. Benchmarked against Audi PPE 800V hairpin stator lines.

ideaData.title: Laser-skinned hairpin enamel removal replacing mechanical stripping
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline flat-wire hairpin ends are stripped of PAI/PEEK enamel by abrasive wheels or mechanical scraping before crown laser welding, occasionally nicking the OF copper and creating weak joints. This idea uses galvo-scanned pulsed fibre-laser ablation to remove insulation in <0.4 s/pin with no conductor contact, leaving a clean, oxide-controlled weld land. Over-strip scrap drops from ~1.8% to ~0.3%, improving downstream weld yield and removing the abrasive-media consumable line and dust extraction. Saves ~£2.7/stator plus warranty reduction on weld voids in the 800V hairpin stator.
ideaData.materialGrade: OF copper flat-wire, PAI/PEEK enamel; galvo fibre-laser ablation
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces abrasive stripping cell and its media/dust-extraction consumables with a contactless galvo laser station, cutting conductor-nick scrap from 1.8% to 0.3% and stabilising weld-land prep. Faster <0.4 s/pin cycle and cleaner surface raise downstream laser-weld first-pass yield. Dust-free enclosure and reduced housekeeping further lower line-maintenance overhead.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Contactless laser stripping removes abrasive scrap and nick-induced weld defects.

ideaData.riskNotes: Laser parameter drift can leave residual enamel or over-ablate, weakening joints; copper oxidation between strip and weld must be controlled. Mitigate with closed-loop power monitoring, inert shroud, and inline weld inspection. IP/process licensing on ablation recipes to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi PPE 800V hairpin stator line, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 line study

evidenceSources.title: Audi PPE (Q6 e-tron) stator hairpin teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: IEC 60034 winding insulation integrity

title: Higher slot-fill via in-slot compaction for copper mass reduction
description: Apply post-insertion radial compaction of flat-wire conductors to lift slot fill from 68% to 78%, reducing DC winding resistance ~11% and copper loss at rated current. For equal efficiency the conductor cross-section is trimmed, cutting ~0.21 kg copper per stator (~£1.6/unit) and lowering cooling demand. Combined material plus efficiency credit ~£7.6/unit. Benchmarked against BYD e-Platform 3.0 flat-wire motor.

ideaData.title: Higher slot-fill via in-slot compaction for copper mass reduction
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline flat-wire stator achieves ~68% slot fill after insertion. This idea applies post-insertion radial compaction—servo-driven blade tooling pressing conductors toward the slot opening—raising fill to ~78%. Higher fill lowers DC winding resistance ~11% and copper loss at rated current. Holding efficiency constant, the conductor cross-section is reduced, removing ~0.21 kg copper per stator (~£1.6/unit) and easing cooling demand on the oil-cooled stator. Combined material plus efficiency credit reaches ~£7.6/unit. Compaction must respect PAI/PEEK enamel integrity and slot-liner PD margin on the 800V machine.
ideaData.materialGrade: OF copper flat-wire, PAI/PEEK enamel; M270-35A core
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds a servo radial-compaction station after insertion to densify conductors to 78% fill; enables thinner conductor section, cutting copper 0.21 kg/stator. Lower I²R reduces oil-cooling duty. Requires compaction tooling tuned to avoid enamel damage and maintain slot-liner clearance. The added compaction station fits within existing winding-line takt and removes no downstream operations.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Higher slot fill lets copper section shrink, cutting mass and loss together.

ideaData.riskNotes: Compaction can abrade enamel or crush slot liners, risking PD breakdown at 800V; conductor section reduction must keep thermal margin. Mitigate with force-controlled tooling, PD/hi-pot screening, and thermal DV. Repeatability across slot-fill variation needs SPC.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD e-Platform 3.0 flat-wire motor, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: IEC 60034-18 PD insulation classification

title: Single-component slot liner replacing multi-layer insulation
description: Consolidate the three-layer Nomex-Mylar-Nomex slot liner into a single calendered aramid-PET composite liner rated to 800V partial-discharge limits. Removes two insertion operations and one bill-of-material line, cutting insulation cost ~£1.8/stator and reducing slot wall thickness 0.15 mm to recover slot area for copper. Assembly cycle time drops ~4%. Benchmarked against Volvo/Volvo Cars EX90 dual-motor stator.

ideaData.title: Single-component slot liner replacing multi-layer insulation
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline slot insulation is a three-layer Nomex(aramid)-Mylar(PET)-Nomex laminate inserted in two operations. This idea consolidates it into a single calendered aramid-PET composite slot liner qualified to 800V partial-discharge limits (Class H, 180°C). Removing two insertion steps and one BOM line cuts insulation cost ~£1.8/stator; the thinner 0.15 mm-reduced wall recovers slot area for copper, lifting fill. Assembly cycle time drops ~4%. The single-piece liner is pre-formed and cuffed, simplifying automated insertion on the EX90 dual-motor stator line. The cuffed single-piece liner also tolerates faster automated insertion without the alignment fussiness of stacking three films.
ideaData.materialGrade: Calendered aramid-PET composite slot liner, Class H 180°C
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Collapses three-layer liner into one pre-formed insert, deleting two insertion operations and one BOM line; reduces slot-wall thickness 0.15 mm to free copper area. Cuts winding-line cycle time ~4% and simplifies automated cuffing/insertion. The single-piece liner also reduces winding-line jams and rework from misaligned multi-layer inserts.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Single-piece liner removes insertion steps, BOM lines, and slot-wall thickness.

ideaData.riskNotes: Single-layer must hold 800V PD margin without the redundancy of three layers; edge tear during insertion risks shorts. Mitigate with PD-qualified composite, cuff reinforcement, and 100% hi-pot. Supplier qualification of composite to Class H required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Volvo EX90 dual-motor stator, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: IEC 60034-18 PD; UN/ECE R100

title: Ce-substituted NdFeB magnets to cut rare-earth content
description: Substitute ~20% of Nd/Pr with cerium in the rotor's NdFeB magnets (Ce-blended N48 grade) with grain-boundary diffusion of Dy only at pole edges. Reduces heavy/light rare-earth cost ~25% at ~£6/unit magnet-mass equivalent, with <3% remanence loss recovered via a 4% thicker pole geometry. Removes broad Dy dip-coating. Benchmarked against Nio 180/300 kW IPM rotor magnet strategy.

ideaData.title: Ce-substituted NdFeB magnets to cut rare-earth content
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline IPM rotor uses sintered NdFeB N48-class magnets with Nd/Pr as light rare earth and broad Dy dip-coating for coercivity. This idea substitutes ~20% of Nd/Pr with cerium (Ce-blended N48 grade) and applies grain-boundary diffusion of Dy only at pole edges where demagnetisation is highest. Light/heavy rare-earth cost falls ~25% (~£6/unit magnet-equivalent), with the <3% remanence loss recovered by a 4% thicker pole geometry in the V-magnet pockets. Eliminating broad Dy coating reduces heavy-RE usage; demag margin at peak 800V phase current is preserved on the 180/300 kW machine.
ideaData.materialGrade: Ce-blended NdFeB N48 (~20% Nd/Pr→Ce), edge GBD Dy
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Magnet supplier shifts to Ce-blended sinter and edge-only GBD coating instead of full dip; magnet pocket geometry widened 4% in lamination tooling. Net fewer heavy-RE process steps; magnet handling and assembly unchanged on the rotor line. Pole-pocket retooling is a one-time lamination-die change with no added rotor-line stations.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Cerium substitution and edge-only Dy slash rare-earth content per magnet.

ideaData.riskNotes: Ce raises corrosion susceptibility and lowers intrinsic coercivity; demag margin at high temp/peak current is the key risk. Mitigate with edge GBD, thicker poles, surface passivation, and accelerated demag/corrosion testing. Magnet supply qualification and IP on Ce-blend grades to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio 180/300 kW IPM rotor magnet strategy, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 analysis

evidenceSources.title: Nio ET7 rear IPM rotor magnet teardown

title: Grain-boundary-diffused Dy to cut bulk heavy rare-earth
description: Replace bulk dysprosium alloying with grain-boundary diffusion (GBD) of Dy/Tb only into the magnet surface, achieving the same coercivity at 150°C with ~60% less heavy rare earth. Dy usage falls from ~3.5% to ~1.3% by mass, saving ~£6.8/unit on magnet cost for a 2.4 kg magnet set. Maintains demagnetization margin at peak current. Benchmarked against Zeekr 800V 580 kW dual-motor rotor.

ideaData.title: Grain-boundary-diffused Dy to cut bulk heavy rare-earth
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rotor uses NdFeB with bulk dysprosium alloyed throughout (~3.5% by mass) to hold coercivity at 150°C. This idea replaces bulk alloying with grain-boundary diffusion (GBD) of Dy/Tb into the magnet surface, concentrating heavy rare earth where demagnetisation fields peak. Dy content drops from ~3.5% to ~1.3% by mass—~60% less HRE—saving ~£6.8/unit on a 2.4 kg magnet set while maintaining demag margin at peak current. The GBD sputter/diffusion step adds magnet-supplier cost but the HRE reduction dominates on the 800V 580 kW dual-motor rotor.
ideaData.materialGrade: GBD Dy/Tb NdFeB (Dy 3.5%→1.3%), N48SH-class
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Magnet supplier adds a grain-boundary diffusion step (Dy/Tb deposition + vacuum diffusion anneal) replacing bulk alloying; magnet dimensions and rotor assembly unchanged. Net HRE mass cut 60% per magnet with equal coercivity, no rotor-line change. The deeper diffusion anneal lengthens furnace dwell slightly but adds no rotor-assembly operations.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: GBD localises heavy rare earth, cutting Dy/Tb mass ~60% per magnet.

ideaData.riskNotes: GBD depth limits effectiveness on thick magnets; demag margin at 150°C peak current is critical and HRE supply (Dy/Tb) volatile. Mitigate with thickness-matched diffusion profile, demag DV at worst-case temp, and dual-source HRE. Process IP licensing to verify.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr 800V 580 kW dual-motor rotor, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Delta-shaped double-V magnet topology reducing magnet mass
description: Adopt a double-V (delta) buried-magnet IPM topology that raises reluctance torque contribution from ~30% to ~45%, allowing ~12% reduction in NdFeB magnet mass for equal peak torque. Saves ~0.28 kg magnet (~£5.1/unit) and improves flux-weakening for high-speed 800V operation. Rotor lamination bridges retuned for mechanical margin at 18,000 rpm. Benchmarked against Xpeng X-EDU rear motor.

ideaData.title: Delta-shaped double-V magnet topology reducing magnet mass
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline IPM rotor uses a single-V buried-magnet layout with ~30% reluctance-torque contribution. This idea adopts a double-V (delta) topology with two stacked magnet layers per pole, raising reluctance torque to ~45% and saliency ratio, so equal peak torque is met with ~12% less NdFeB magnet mass (~0.28 kg, ~£5.1/unit). The higher saliency also improves flux-weakening for high-speed 800V operation. Rotor lamination bridges and centre-post webs are retuned in M270-35A steel for mechanical margin at 18,000 rpm, balancing magnet retention against bridge saturation leakage.
ideaData.materialGrade: NdFeB N48SH double-V magnets; M270-35A rotor laminations
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: New lamination die for double-V pockets and retuned bridges; two magnet inserts per pole instead of one changes magnet-insertion fixturing and adhesive-fill steps. Magnet mass per pole drops 12%; rotor stack length and balancing unchanged. Adhesive dispense and cure for the second magnet layer add one short station to the rotor line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Higher reluctance torque lets magnet mass shrink for equal peak torque.

ideaData.riskNotes: Thinner bridges raise centrifugal stress at 18,000 rpm and leakage flux; magnet retention and fatigue are critical. Mitigate with FEA bridge sizing, burst-spin testing, and adhesive bonding of magnets. Added pocket count slightly raises insertion complexity.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng X-EDU rear motor, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Xpeng G9 rear X-EDU rotor teardown

title: Carbon-fiber retaining sleeve replacing Inconel for high-speed rotor
description: Replace a press-fit Inconel/steel retaining ring with a filament-wound carbon-fiber sleeve for the surface-region magnets on a 22,000 rpm rotor. CFRP cuts rotating mass ~40% and removes a heavy nickel-alloy part, lowering bearing load and centrifugal stress so magnet bridges thin out. Net ~£7.6/unit including reduced balancing iterations. Benchmarked against Yangwang U9 high-speed drive rotor.

ideaData.title: Carbon-fiber retaining sleeve replacing Inconel for high-speed rotor
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline high-speed rotor retains surface-region magnets with a press-fit Inconel/steel retaining ring sized for centrifugal load at 22,000 rpm. This idea replaces it with a filament-wound carbon-fibre (T700-class) sleeve cured over the rotor, cutting rotating mass ~40% versus the nickel-alloy ring and removing a costly machined part. Lower rotating mass reduces bearing load and centrifugal stress, allowing magnet bridges/sleeve interference to thin and balancing iterations to drop. Net ~£7.6/unit including reduced balancing. CFRP sleeve pre-tension is engineered to keep magnets in radial compression at peak speed.
ideaData.materialGrade: Filament-wound carbon-fibre (T700) sleeve (vs Inconel 718 ring)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces machined/press-fit Inconel ring with a filament-winding + oven-cure operation; deletes the nickel-alloy part and its grinding/fitting. Lower rotating mass cuts balancing iterations. Adds CFRP winding cell and resin cure but removes hard-machining of superalloy. Removing superalloy hard-turning frees a difficult machining bottleneck and lowers tool-crib cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: CFRP sleeve removes a costly superalloy ring and cuts rotating mass 40%.

ideaData.riskNotes: CFRP creep, resin thermal limit (~180°C) and magnet-to-sleeve interference under thermal cycling are key risks; delamination at 22,000 rpm is catastrophic. Mitigate with pre-tension design, burst-spin and thermal-cycle DV, and NDT of winding. Process maturity/cost of CFRP cell to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Yangwang U9 high-speed drive rotor, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown

title: Two-plane automated rotor balancing to cut rework scrap
description: Implement closed-loop two-plane laser-ablation balancing with in-line spin measurement, replacing iterative mill-and-remeasure balancing of the IPM rotor assembly. First-pass yield rises from approximately 85% to 97% to G2.5 grade at 16,000 rpm, cutting rotor scrap, cycle time, and rebalancing labor by about 3.50 euro per unit. Removes added balance weights entirely and shortens the line. Benchmarked against Li Auto 5C rear-drive rotor balancing line.

ideaData.title: Two-plane automated rotor balancing to cut rework scrap
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline IPM rotor is balanced by iterative mill-and-remeasure on a spin balancer, often adding balance weights and re-running. This idea implements closed-loop two-plane laser-ablation balancing with in-line spin measurement: the system measures unbalance, computes correction, and ablates material from two correction planes in one cycle to G2.5 at 16,000 rpm. First-pass yield rises from ~85% to ~97%, cutting rotor scrap, cycle time, and rebalancing labour ~£3/unit and removing added balance weights entirely.
