Laser ablation avoids chip contamination of the assembled magnet rotor and shortens the balancing line.
ideaData.materialGrade: Laser-ablation balancing of assembled NdFeB IPM rotor
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces iterative mill-balance with single-cycle closed-loop laser two-plane correction, removing the milling spindle, swarf handling, and added balance weights. First-pass yield 85%→97% cuts rotor scrap and rebalance labour; shorter line, no chip contamination of magnets. Closed-loop control removes the separate measure-and-return material-handling loop between cells.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Closed-loop laser balancing removes rework iterations and balance weights.

ideaData.riskNotes: Laser ablation removes structural material—depth control must avoid bridge weakening; ablation debris and heat-affected zone near magnets need control. Mitigate with depth-limited recipes, fume extraction, and post-balance spin verification. Capital cost of laser balancer vs payback.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto 5C rear-drive rotor balancing line, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 line study

evidenceSources.title: Laser two-plane rotor balancing cell data
Li Auto MEGA 5C rear drive teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 21940 balancing grade G2.5

title: Direct oil-spray rotor cooling enabling magnet downgrade
description: Add a hollow-shaft direct oil-cooling circuit spraying ATF onto rotor end-rings, lowering peak magnet temperature ~25°C. The cooler operating point allows a one-grade magnet downgrade (N48UH to N45SH) and ~8% less Dy, saving ~£4.3/unit on magnets while sustaining continuous power. Oil path machined into existing shaft. Benchmarked against AITO M9 oil-cooled drive motor.

ideaData.title: Direct oil-spray rotor cooling enabling magnet downgrade
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rotor is cooled only indirectly via the oil-cooled stator jacket, forcing high-grade N48UH magnets to survive peak temperature. This idea machines a hollow-shaft oil-cooling circuit that sprays ATF onto rotor end-rings/lamination ends, lowering peak magnet temperature ~25°C. The cooler operating point allows a one-grade magnet downgrade from N48UH to N45SH and ~8% less Dy, saving ~£4.3/unit while sustaining continuous power. The oil path is bored into the existing rotor shaft with a rotary union feed; ATF doubles as gear oil in the shared EDU sump.
ideaData.materialGrade: ATF rotor-spray; magnets N48UH→N45SH NdFeB
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds hollow-shaft bore, radial spray ports, and a rotary feed union to the rotor shaft; integrates with the EDU oil sump and pump. Enables lower magnet grade and less Dy. Shaft machining adds a step but no new external parts on the oil-cooled drive.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Direct rotor oil-spray lowers magnet temperature, enabling a cheaper grade.

ideaData.riskNotes: Oil churning at the rotor adds spin loss and spray-port machining tolerance affects flow distribution; magnet thermal validation under worst-case duty is essential. Mitigate with CFD-tuned ports, low-viscosity ATF, and continuous-power thermal DV. Sealing of rotary union to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AITO M9 oil-cooled drive motor, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown

evidenceSources.title: AITO M9 oil-cooled drive motor teardown
Direct rotor oil-spray cooling for IPM

title: Integrated cast water jacket replacing welded steel cooling shell
description: Cast the spiral water jacket directly into the HPDC AlSi10MnMg motor housing instead of a separate rolled-and-welded steel cooling shroud with O-ring seals. Eliminates the shroud part, two weld seams, and a leak-test station, removing ~0.6 kg and ~£6.8/unit. Coolant channel cross-section optimized via core pins for 12% lower pressure drop. Benchmarked against Mercedes eATS integrated stator jacket.

ideaData.title: Integrated cast water jacket replacing welded steel cooling shell
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline motor cooling uses a separate rolled-and-welded steel cooling shroud with O-ring seals fitted over the stator. This idea casts the spiral water jacket directly into the HPDC AlSi10MnMg (EN AC-43500) motor housing using collapsible/loose core pins, eliminating the shroud part, two weld seams, and a dedicated leak-test station. It removes ~0.6 kg and ~£6.8/unit; the cast channel cross-section is optimised for 12% lower pressure drop. The aluminium jacket also improves stator-to-coolant heat path versus the steel shroud on the 800V eATS.
ideaData.materialGrade: EN AC-43500 AlSi10MnMg HPDC housing, cast spiral water jacket
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Integrates the cooling jacket into the HPDC casting via core pins, deleting the welded steel shroud, two weld seams, O-rings, and one leak-test station. Casting complexity and core-pin maintenance rise, but part count, welding, and sealing operations drop sharply. Core-pin sets are scheduled into the existing HPDC tool maintenance cycle without new standalone equipment.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Casting the jacket into the housing deletes the welded shroud and its seals.

ideaData.riskNotes: Cast channels risk porosity-driven coolant leaks and core-pin breakage; wall integrity between jacket and bore is critical. Mitigate with high-vacuum HPDC, in-line pressure-decay leak test, and X-ray sampling. Tooling/core-pin durability affects cost case.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes eATS integrated stator jacket, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Combined stator-jacket and rotor oil loop sharing one pump
description: Merge water-glycol stator jacket and ATF rotor-spray cooling into a single oil-only loop using a shared electric oil pump and one heat exchanger, deleting the secondary coolant pump and water/oil interface seals. Removes ~5 fittings and one pump (~£10/unit) and simplifies fill/bleed. Direct end-winding oil contact improves thermal headroom for continuous power. Benchmarked against Tesla-style/BYD direct-oil EDU thermal design.

ideaData.title: Combined stator-jacket and rotor oil loop sharing one pump
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU runs two thermal circuits: a water-glycol stator jacket and a separate ATF rotor-spray loop, each with its own pump and interfaces. This idea merges them into a single oil-only loop using one shared electric oil pump and one oil-to-coolant heat exchanger, deleting the secondary coolant pump and the water/oil interface seals. It removes ~5 fittings and one pump (~£10/unit) and simplifies fill/bleed. Direct end-winding oil contact improves thermal headroom for continuous power. ATF is dielectric, enabling safe direct contact with 800V windings.
ideaData.materialGrade: Single dielectric ATF loop; shared electric oil pump
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the secondary water pump, water/oil barrier seals, and ~5 fittings; one pump and one heat exchanger serve both stator and rotor. Direct end-winding oil contact replaces jacket conduction. Simplifies fill/bleed and reduces assembly joints. Single-fill commissioning at end-of-line shortens fill/bleed station cycle and reduces trapped-air rejects.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Unifying to one oil loop deletes a pump, seals, and fittings.

ideaData.riskNotes: Single-loop must meet both winding and bearing thermal needs; oil-only cooling of the stator may run hotter than glycol, risking derate. Mitigate with optimised spray distribution, oil-flow CFD, and continuous-power thermal DV. Oil ageing/dielectric retention over life to validate.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD direct-oil EDU thermal design, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100 HV creepage in dielectric oil

title: Bare-die SiC MOSFET power module replacing IGBT in inverter
description: Replace 750V Si IGBT modules with 1200V SiC MOSFET half-bridge modules for the 800V inverter, cutting switching loss ~70% and conduction loss at partial load. Higher efficiency permits ~5% smaller battery for equal range and shrinks the heatsink and DC-link cap, a net vehicle-level saving despite die premium, ~£15/unit system. Enables 800V fast charge. Benchmarked against Porsche Taycan/PPE SiC inverter.

ideaData.title: Bare-die SiC MOSFET power module replacing IGBT in inverter
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 800V inverter uses 750V silicon IGBT half-bridge modules with antiparallel diodes. This idea swaps to 1200V SiC MOSFET half-bridge modules, cutting switching loss ~70% and conduction loss at partial load via the low Rds(on) channel. Higher drivetrain efficiency permits ~5% smaller battery for equal range and shrinks the heatsink and DC-link film capacitor, a net vehicle-level saving (~£15/unit system) despite the SiC die premium. SiC also enables true 800V fast charge. Gate drive moves to higher dv/dt with tuned Rg; module uses sintered-Ag die-attach for 175°C operation.
ideaData.materialGrade: 1200V SiC MOSFET module (vs 750V Si IGBT)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Power-module footprint and gate-drive redesigned for SiC dv/dt; heatsink and DC-link cap downsized from efficiency gain. Higher switching frequency reduces magnetics. Die premium offset at vehicle level by smaller battery and thermal hardware; assembly otherwise comparable. Smaller heatsink and DC-link also free packaging volume, easing busbar routing and lowering enclosure cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: SiC efficiency shrinks battery, heatsink, and DC-link for net system saving.

ideaData.riskNotes: Higher dv/dt stresses motor insulation (bearing currents, PD) and EMC; SiC die supply and cost volatility are program risks. Mitigate with dv/dt-tuned gate drive, reinforced winding insulation, shaft grounding, and dual-source SiC. Long-term gate-oxide reliability validation required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Taycan/PPE SiC inverter, 2020; benchmarked in 2021 teardown

evidenceSources.title: SiC vs IGBT 800V traction inverter TCO

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R10 EMC; ISO 26262 ASIL-D

title: Sintered-silver die-attach replacing solder in SiC power module
description: Use pressure-assisted silver sintering for SiC die-attach instead of high-Pb solder, raising thermal cycling life ~5x and allowing junction operation to 175°C. The improved thermal path lets the module use ~12% less SiC die area for the same continuous current, offsetting silver cost for ~£3.4/unit net plus warranty reduction. Benchmarked against AVATR/CATL 800V SiC module.

ideaData.title: Sintered-silver die-attach replacing solder in SiC power module
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline SiC module attaches die to substrate with high-Pb solder, limiting thermal-cycle life and junction temperature. This idea uses pressure-assisted silver sintering (Ag paste sintered ~250°C under pressure) for die-attach, raising thermal-cycling life ~5× and allowing junction operation to 175°C. The improved thermal path lets the module use ~12% less SiC die area for equal continuous current, offsetting the silver cost for ~£3.4/unit net plus warranty reduction. Sintered-Ag also removes lead from the assembly, aiding ELV compliance on the 800V SiC module.
ideaData.materialGrade: Sintered-silver die-attach (vs high-Pb solder), SiC die
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces solder reflow with a pressure-sinter press cycle; enables higher Tj and ~12% smaller SiC die for equal current. Adds sinter tooling and Ag paste cost but removes lead and improves yield/life. Bondline thinner, lowering thermal resistance. Lead-free sintering simplifies environmental compliance and avoids solder-void rework on high-value modules.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Silver sintering boosts thermal life and shrinks SiC die area needed.

ideaData.riskNotes: Sinter porosity/voids and high silver cost are risks; pressure uniformity affects reliability. Mitigate with SAM void inspection, controlled sinter profile, and active-power-cycling qualification. Capital for sinter presses and Ag price exposure to manage.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AVATR/CATL 800V SiC module, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: EU ELV Directive lead-free; ISO 26262

title: Film DC-link capacitor downsizing via reduced switching ripple
description: Raise SiC switching frequency to 16 kHz with interleaved control to cut DC-link RMS ripple current ~30%, allowing the polypropylene film DC-link capacitor to shrink from 500 µF to 350 µF. Removes ~0.25 L of capacitor volume and ~£6/unit while easing busbar layout. Thermal margin on the cap improves at 800V. Benchmarked against Denza/BYD 800V e-axle inverter.

ideaData.title: Film DC-link capacitor downsizing via reduced switching ripple
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 800V inverter sizes its polypropylene film DC-link capacitor at 500 µF for RMS ripple current at the switching point. This idea raises SiC switching frequency to 16 kHz with interleaved/phase-shifted control, cutting DC-link RMS ripple current ~30%, which allows the film cap to shrink from 500 µF to 350 µF. That removes ~0.25 L of capacitor volume and ~£6/unit while easing busbar layout. Thermal margin on the cap improves at 800V because lower ripple means lower self-heating. Control firmware and PWM scheme carry the change.
ideaData.materialGrade: Polypropylene metallised-film DC-link capacitor 500→350 µF
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Smaller film capacitor frees ~0.25 L volume, shortening busbars and shrinking the inverter envelope. No new process—mainly a smaller cap part and firmware PWM change. Lower ripple eases cap thermal design and reduces self-heating. The freed volume tightens the inverter envelope and eases enclosure tooling and gasket length.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Higher SiC frequency cuts ripple, allowing a smaller DC-link capacitor.

ideaData.riskNotes: Higher 16 kHz switching raises SiC losses and EMC; cap downsizing must keep ripple and overvoltage margin at peak fast-charge. Mitigate with thermal/EMC validation, derating analysis, and interleaving stability checks. Cap lifetime under higher temp ripple to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Denza/BYD 800V e-axle inverter, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R10 EMC

title: Integrated PCB gate driver replacing discrete driver board
description: Integrate isolated gate-driver ICs directly onto the main control PCB with planar pulse transformers, deleting the separate driver daughterboard, its connector, and harness. Removes ~14 components and one board (~£4.3/unit), shortens gate loop inductance for cleaner SiC switching, and cuts assembly steps. Desat protection retained on-die. Benchmarked against Luxeed S7 800V inverter control stack.

ideaData.title: Integrated PCB gate driver replacing discrete driver board
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline inverter uses a separate gate-driver daughterboard with its own connector and harness linking to the main control PCB. This idea integrates isolated gate-driver ICs directly onto the main control PCB with planar pulse transformers for isolation, deleting the daughterboard, its board-to-board connector, and harness. It removes ~14 components and one PCB (~£4.3/unit), shortens the gate loop inductance for cleaner SiC switching, and cuts assembly steps. Desaturation (desat) protection is retained on the integrated driver. Creepage/clearance for 800V isolation is laid out on the single board.
ideaData.materialGrade: Integrated PCB gate driver, planar pulse-transformer isolation
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Merges driver onto the main PCB, deleting one board, a board-to-board connector, and harness; removes ~14 parts and associated SMT/insertion steps. Shorter gate loop improves SiC switching. Single-board layout must hold 800V creepage but cuts assembly operations. Removing the board-to-board interface also eliminates a vibration-prone connector failure mode in service.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Integrating the gate driver onto the main PCB deletes a board, connector, and harness.

ideaData.riskNotes: Single-board integration concentrates HV isolation and thermal stress near logic; planar-transformer isolation must meet 800V reinforced clearance. Mitigate with isolation-barrier layout, conformal coat, and EMC/HV-isolation testing. PCB rework cost rises if a driver fails (whole board).
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Luxeed S7 800V inverter control stack, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: IEC 60664 creepage/clearance; UN/ECE R10

title: Laminated busbar replacing cable links between module and caps
description: Replace insulated copper cable links and ring-terminal bolting between power module, DC-link cap, and HV input with a stamped laminated copper busbar. Cuts loop inductance ~40% to reduce SiC overshoot, removes 6 crimped terminals and 4 fasteners (~£3.4/unit), and lowers I²R loss. One press-formed part replaces a sub-assembly. Benchmarked against Maextro/Hongqi 800V inverter busbar.

ideaData.title: Laminated busbar replacing cable links between module and caps
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline links between power module, DC-link capacitor, and HV input use insulated copper cables with ring terminals and bolted joints. This idea replaces them with a stamped laminated copper busbar (dielectric-film-laminated layers) carrying positive and negative planes. It cuts loop inductance ~40%, reducing SiC voltage overshoot, and removes 6 crimped terminals and 4 fasteners (~£3.4/unit) while lowering I²R loss. One press-formed part replaces a cable sub-assembly. The laminated stack-up provides low-inductance, compact 800V DC routing inside the inverter. Lower loop inductance directly cuts SiC turn-off overshoot, improving switching reliability and EMC margin at 800V.
ideaData.materialGrade: Stamped laminated copper busbar (Cu C110), film dielectric
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One stamped/laminated busbar replaces multiple insulated cables, 6 crimp terminals, and 4 fasteners; deletes crimping and routing operations. Press-form tooling added but assembly is a single drop-in part with reduced bolted joints and lower loop inductance. One drop-in busbar removes cable-routing jigs and the crimp-quality inspection step from the line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A laminated busbar consolidates cables and fasteners and cuts loop inductance.

ideaData.riskNotes: Busbar dielectric film must hold 800V with no pinholes; bolted-joint creep and thermal expansion can loosen contacts. Mitigate with hi-pot testing, Belleville-washer joints, and torque-to-yield control. Stamping tooling cost vs cable flexibility trade-off.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Maextro/Hongqi 800V inverter busbar, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: IEC 60664; UN/ECE R10 EMC

title: Pin-fin direct-cooled SiC baseplate eliminating TIM layer
description: Adopt a pin-fin AlSiC baseplate directly immersed in the coolant channel, deleting the thermal interface grease layer and a separate cold plate. Thermal resistance junction-to-coolant drops ~35%, allowing ~10% smaller SiC die for equal current and removing the TIM dispense station, ~£5.1/unit. Sealing via single perimeter gasket. Benchmarked against Audi PPE 800V direct-cooled inverter.

ideaData.title: Pin-fin direct-cooled SiC baseplate eliminating TIM layer
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline SiC module sits on a baseplate bolted to a separate cold plate with thermal-interface grease (TIM) between them. This idea adopts a pin-fin AlSiC (aluminium silicon-carbide MMC) baseplate directly immersed in the coolant channel, deleting the TIM layer and the separate cold plate. Junction-to-coolant thermal resistance drops ~35%, allowing ~10% smaller SiC die for equal current and removing the TIM-dispense station (~£5.1/unit). Sealing is by a single perimeter gasket. AlSiC's CTE match to the substrate also improves thermal-cycle life on the 800V direct-cooled inverter.
ideaData.materialGrade: Pin-fin AlSiC baseplate, direct coolant immersion (TIM deleted)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Direct-cooled pin-fin baseplate removes the cold plate, TIM, and the TIM-dispense station; a single perimeter gasket seals the coolant cavity. Lower thermal resistance shrinks SiC die ~10%. AlSiC adds material cost but cuts assembly steps and improves reliability. Eliminating the TIM-dispense cell also removes a messy consumable and its periodic nozzle-cleaning downtime.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Direct pin-fin cooling deletes the TIM and cold plate and shrinks SiC die.

ideaData.riskNotes: Coolant directly contacts the baseplate—seal integrity and corrosion/erosion of pin-fins are critical; gasket leak risks the module. Mitigate with pressure-decay leak test, coolant-compatibility validation, and AlSiC corrosion screening. AlSiC cost vs die savings to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi PPE 800V direct-cooled inverter, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Audi PPE 800V direct-cooled SiC inverter teardown

title: Net-shape forged helical gears reducing machining stock
description: Switch reduction gears to precision warm-forged near-net blanks, leaving only finish grinding stock. Removes rough-hobbing and turning operations, cutting ~0.4 kg machining swarf per gearset and ~2 minutes cycle time. Material utilization rises from ~60% to ~85% on 20MnCr5, saving ~£5.1/unit plus tool wear. Tooth root strength improved by forged grain flow. Benchmarked against Mercedes eATS two-stage reduction.

ideaData.title: Net-shape forged helical gears reducing machining stock
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline reduction gears are machined from rough-turned/hobbed cylindrical blanks of 20MnCr5 case-hardening steel, generating heavy swarf. This idea switches to precision warm-forged near-net blanks that already carry the tooth profile, leaving only finish-grinding stock. It removes rough-hobbing and turning, cuts ~0.4 kg machining swarf per gearset, and saves ~2 minutes cycle time. Material utilisation rises from ~60% to ~85% on 20MnCr5 (~£5.1/unit) plus reduced tool wear. Forged grain flow follows the tooth contour, raising tooth-root bending strength on the 800V two-stage reduction.
ideaData.materialGrade: 20MnCr5 case-hardening steel, warm-forged near-net blank
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Warm-forging produces near-net tooth blanks, deleting rough-hobbing and turning and cutting swarf 0.4 kg/set with ~2 min less cycle. Material utilisation 60%→85%. Adds forging dies but removes machining tools and improves root strength via grain flow. Reduced swarf volume also cuts coolant/chip-handling load and lengthens grinding-wheel life downstream.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Net-shape forging slashes machining swarf and cycle time on gears.

ideaData.riskNotes: Forged profile accuracy and die wear affect grind stock consistency; forging-induced distortion before heat-treat must be controlled. Mitigate with die DV, in-process gauging, and grind-allowance buffer. Forging-die NRE and minimum volume to justify.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes eATS two-stage reduction, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Near-net forged EV gear cost study

title: Honed gear flanks replacing hard-grinding for NVH targets
description: Replace generating grinding of helical reduction gears with gear honing, achieving the surface finish and flank modification needed for 17,000 rpm NVH at lower cycle time and tool cost. Honing removes the grinding wheel consumable and cuts ~1.4 min/part, ~£3.8/unit, while the cross-hatch finish lowers mesh whine ~2 dB. Benchmarked against Zeekr 800V single-speed reducer.

ideaData.title: Honed gear flanks replacing hard-grinding for NVH targets
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline helical reduction gears are finished by generating (continuous-generation) grinding to meet 17,000 rpm NVH targets. This idea replaces grinding with gear honing, which achieves the required surface finish and flank micro-geometry (lead/profile crowning) at lower cycle time and tool cost. Honing removes the grinding-wheel consumable and cuts ~1.4 min/part (~£3.8/unit); the cross-hatch finish lowers mesh whine ~2 dB by improving lubricant retention and reducing flank waviness. Applied to case-hardened 20MnCr5 gears on the 800V single-speed reducer after carburising. Retaining a generated-grinding fallback for outlier distortion de-risks the transition during process ramp-up.
ideaData.materialGrade: 20MnCr5 case-hardened gear; honed flank finish (vs ground)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Honing replaces the grinding cell, deleting grinding-wheel consumables and cutting ~1.4 min/part. Cross-hatch finish improves NVH without a separate superfinish. Hone tooling/abrasive-ring cost added but cycle time and consumable spend drop on the hardened gear line. Lower abrasive consumption and reduced wheel-change downtime further improve gear-line availability.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Honing replaces grinding, cutting cycle time and wheel consumables.

ideaData.riskNotes: Honing removes less stock than grinding—pre-hone distortion control after carburising is critical to hit flank tolerance and NVH. Mitigate with low-distortion heat-treat, hone-ring dressing control, and end-of-line NVH gating. Validation of flank durability at 17,000 rpm required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr 800V single-speed reducer, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Gear honing vs grinding NVH/cost data

title: Lower-alloy steel gear with optimized case-carburizing
description: Substitute 27MnCr5 for premium 18CrNiMo7-6 on the intermediate gear, paired with low-pressure vacuum carburizing and high-pressure gas quench to hit the same case depth and core hardness. Reduces alloy cost ~£2.7/unit and distortion (cutting grind stock), with bending fatigue validated to the 800V torque spectrum. Benchmarked against Xpeng rear single-speed gearbox.

ideaData.title: Lower-alloy steel gear with optimized case-carburizing
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline intermediate gear uses premium 18CrNiMo7-6 case-hardening steel with conventional gas carburising. This idea substitutes 27MnCr5—a lower-alloy steel without nickel/molybdenum—paired with low-pressure (vacuum) carburising and high-pressure gas quench to hit the same case depth and core hardness. It reduces alloy cost ~£2.7/unit and distortion (cutting grind stock) versus oil-quench. Bending-fatigue and pitting are validated against the 800V torque spectrum. The cleaner LPC process avoids intergranular oxidation, partially compensating the leaner alloy's hardenability. Reduced post-quench distortion shrinks the finish-grind allowance, improving gear-grinding throughput and dimensional consistency.
ideaData.materialGrade: 27MnCr5 (vs 18CrNiMo7-6), low-pressure vacuum carburised
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Leaner 27MnCr5 plus LPC + high-pressure gas quench replaces alloy-rich steel and oil quench, cutting alloy cost and distortion (less grind stock). Heat-treat moves to vacuum furnace; gear machining unchanged. Lower distortion improves yield on the hardened gear. Vacuum carburising batches replace endothermic-atmosphere furnaces, cutting soot cleanup and atmosphere-gas use.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Leaner steel with vacuum carburising matches performance at lower alloy cost.

ideaData.riskNotes: Lower hardenability of 27MnCr5 narrows the process window; case/core properties and bending fatigue must be re-validated to the torque spectrum. Mitigate with LPC recipe optimisation, hardness/depth SPC, and full DV. Vacuum-furnace capacity and capital to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng rear single-speed gearbox, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Xpeng G9 rear gearbox gear teardown

title: Single-piece differential housing replacing bolted two-half case
description: Replace a two-piece bolted differential carrier with a single forged carrier using a pressed-in welded ring gear, deleting 8 bolts, the joint face machining, and a sealing interface. Removes ~0.5 kg and ~£4.3/unit plus one leak path. Laser-welded ring gear cuts assembly time. Validated for the EDU peak axle torque. Benchmarked against BYD e-Platform 3.0 differential.

ideaData.title: Single-piece differential housing replacing bolted two-half case
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline differential uses a two-piece bolted carrier with the ring gear bolted to a flange, requiring joint-face machining and a sealing interface. This idea replaces it with a single forged carrier and a pressed-in, laser-welded ring gear, deleting 8 bolts, the joint-face machining, and a sealing interface. It removes ~0.5 kg and ~£4.3/unit plus one leak path, and laser welding cuts assembly time versus bolting. The carrier is forged from a Cr-Mo steel; the welded ring-gear joint is validated for EDU peak axle torque and fatigue.
ideaData.materialGrade: Forged Cr-Mo differential carrier, laser-welded ring gear
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Single forged carrier with laser-welded ring gear deletes 8 bolts, a bolted flange, joint-face machining, and a seal. Laser welding replaces bolting, cutting assembly time and one leak path. Forging + weld fixturing added but part count and machining drop.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One-piece welded differential carrier deletes bolts, machining, and a seal.

ideaData.riskNotes: Laser-welded ring-gear joint must survive peak axle torque and fatigue without distortion affecting gear runout; weld defects are non-repairable. Mitigate with weld DV, runout gating, and NDT sampling. Welding of hardened ring gear needs heat-input control.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD e-Platform 3.0 differential, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Hybrid passive-active lubrication deleting full oil pump
description: Adopt a hybrid lubrication scheme where churning/splash from the ring gear supplies gear-mesh oil and a small electric pump only feeds bearings and rotor cooling, allowing a downsized 6W pump instead of a 25W gerotor unit. Removes ~half the pump cost (~£6.8/unit) and one pressure regulator. Oil galleries cast into housing. Benchmarked against Li Auto 5C EDU lubrication.

ideaData.title: Hybrid passive-active lubrication deleting full oil pump
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU lubrication uses a 25W electric gerotor pump pressure-feeding gear mesh, bearings, and rotor cooling, with a pressure regulator. This idea adopts a hybrid scheme: churning/splash from the ring gear lubricates the gear mesh, and a small electric pump (~6W) feeds only bearings and rotor cooling. The downsized pump removes ~half the pump cost (~£6.8/unit) and one pressure regulator. Oil galleries are cast into the HPDC housing to route splash-collected oil. Lower active-pump flow also cuts parasitic electrical load on the 800V EDU.
ideaData.materialGrade: Cast oil galleries (AlSi10MnMg housing); 6W vs 25W pump
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Splash/churn feeds gear mesh while a downsized 6W pump serves bearings/rotor only, deleting the larger pump and a pressure regulator. Oil galleries cast into the housing replace some pressurised lines. Fewer/smaller active components and lower parasitic load. Cast galleries replace external oil tubing, removing tube-bending and clip-fastening operations from assembly.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Hybrid splash-plus-mini-pump lubrication downsizes the pump and deletes a regulator.

ideaData.riskNotes: Splash lubrication may starve mesh at low speed/cold start or steep grades; bearing/rotor flow must stay adequate. Mitigate with gallery CFD, cold-start oiling tests, and worst-case durability DV. Oil level/foaming sensitivity to vehicle attitude to validate.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto 5C EDU lubrication, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown

title: Plastic gerotor oil-pump rotor set replacing sintered steel
description: Replace sintered-steel gerotor inner/outer rotors with glass-filled PPS injection-molded rotors rated for ATF at 130°C. Cuts pump rotor cost ~£2/unit, reduces mass and inertia for faster prime, and removes a machining/sizing step. Clearances held by precision molding. Validated for 800V EDU oil flow at 16,000 rpm pump speed. Benchmarked against Nio 2nd-gen EDU oil pump.

ideaData.title: Plastic gerotor oil-pump rotor set replacing sintered steel
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline oil-pump gerotor inner/outer rotors are sintered steel, machined and sized. This idea replaces them with glass-filled PPS (polyphenylene sulphide, e.g. PPS-GF40) injection-moulded rotors rated for ATF at 130°C. It cuts pump-rotor cost ~£2/unit, lowers mass and inertia for faster pump prime, and removes a machining/sizing step. Tooth-tip clearances are held by precision moulding. Validated for 800V EDU oil flow at 16,000 rpm pump speed. PPS resists ATF and additives and tolerates the thermal/chemical environment of the gerotor pocket. Lower rotor inertia also lets the pump reach delivery pressure faster after cold start, aiding early lubrication.
ideaData.materialGrade: PPS-GF40 glass-filled gerotor rotors (vs sintered steel)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Injection-moulded PPS rotors replace sintered-steel parts, deleting machining/sizing and cutting mass/inertia for faster prime. Net-shape moulding holds clearances. Mould tooling added but per-part cost and process steps drop on the oil pump. Removing sized-steel machining also eliminates deburring and washing steps for the rotor set.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Moulded PPS gerotor rotors cut cost, mass, and a machining step.

ideaData.riskNotes: Polymer rotors can wear/swell in hot ATF and lose clearance, dropping volumetric efficiency; high-speed strength at 16,000 rpm is a concern. Mitigate with PPS-GF grade selection, ATF-aged clearance testing, and endurance DV. Dimensional stability over temperature to validate.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio 2nd-gen EDU oil pump, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Shared single oil for gearbox and motor cooling deleting filter
description: Use one low-viscosity ATF for both gear lubrication and direct motor oil-cooling with a single sump and magnetic drain plug, deleting the secondary fluid, a barrier seal, and one spin-on filter in favor of an integrated screen. Removes ~£3.4/unit and simplifies service fill. Lower-viscosity oil also cuts churning loss ~1.5%. Benchmarked against AITO M9 dual-motor EDU.

ideaData.title: Shared single oil for gearbox and motor cooling deleting filter
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU uses separate fluids—gear oil and a motor coolant—with a barrier seal and a spin-on filter for the gear oil. This idea uses one low-viscosity ATF for both gear lubrication and direct motor oil-cooling with a single sump and magnetic drain plug, deleting the secondary fluid, a barrier seal, and the spin-on filter in favour of an integrated screen. It removes ~£3.4/unit and simplifies service fill, while the lower-viscosity oil cuts churning loss ~1.5%. The shared dielectric ATF directly contacts 800V windings safely.
ideaData.materialGrade: Single low-viscosity dielectric ATF, integrated screen filter
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One shared ATF and sump deletes a barrier seal, the secondary fluid, and the spin-on filter (replaced by an integrated screen) plus its housing/mount. Single-fill service. Lower viscosity reduces churning loss. Fewer parts and one fluid simplify assembly and service.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One shared oil deletes a seal, second fluid, and the spin-on filter.

ideaData.riskNotes: Shared ATF must lubricate gears and cool windings without contaminating either; integrated screen offers less filtration than spin-on, risking wear-debris circulation. Mitigate with magnetic plug, fine screen, oil-analysis durability, and dielectric-retention validation. Service interval to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AITO M9 dual-motor EDU, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown

evidenceSources.title: AITO M9 dual-motor EDU lubrication teardown

title: Cast-in oil-to-coolant heat exchanger in EDU housing
description: Integrate the oil-to-water heat exchanger as cast channels in the HPDC EDU housing rather than a bolted-on stacked-plate cooler, removing the cooler part, its 2 seals, 4 bolts, and 2 oil hoses. Saves ~£7.6/unit and ~0.7 kg, shortens oil path for lower pressure drop, and eliminates an external leak interface. Benchmarked against Mercedes integrated eATS thermal block.

ideaData.title: Cast-in oil-to-coolant heat exchanger in EDU housing
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU uses a bolted-on stacked-plate oil-to-water heat exchanger with seals, bolts, and external oil hoses. This idea integrates the oil-to-water exchanger as cast channels in the HPDC AlSi10MnMg EDU housing, removing the cooler part, its 2 seals, 4 bolts, and 2 oil hoses. It saves ~£7.6/unit and ~0.7 kg, shortens the oil path for lower pressure drop, and eliminates an external leak interface. Counterflow oil and coolant channels are cast adjacent with a thin cast wall; the aluminium provides the conduction path on the 800V EDU thermal block.
ideaData.materialGrade: EN AC-43500 AlSi10MnMg HPDC housing, cast oil-water channels
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Casts the heat-exchanger channels into the housing, deleting the bolted stacked-plate cooler, 2 seals, 4 bolts, and 2 hoses. Core pins form counterflow channels. Casting complexity rises but part count, external joints, and oil-path length drop, lowering pressure drop. Counterflow core pins integrate into the existing die-cast tool, adding no separate cooler-assembly station.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Casting the oil-water exchanger into the housing deletes the bolt-on cooler and hoses.

ideaData.riskNotes: Cast inter-fluid wall must prevent oil/coolant cross-leak—porosity is the key risk; thermal performance must match the plate cooler. Mitigate with high-vacuum HPDC, cross-leak pressure test, and thermal DV. Core-pin durability and casting yield to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes integrated eATS thermal block, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Thermistor-in-busbar deleting standalone coolant temp sensor
description: Embed an NTC thermistor into the inverter coolant inlet boss and infer winding temperature via a thermal model, deleting a separate threaded coolant temperature sensor and its connector/harness branch. Removes ~£1.7/unit and one leak-prone fitting while retaining derating accuracy within ±3°C. Software model calibrated on dyno. Benchmarked against Volvo EX90 EDU thermal control.

ideaData.title: Thermistor-in-busbar deleting standalone coolant temp sensor
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU fits a separate threaded coolant-temperature sensor with its own connector and harness branch in the coolant line. This idea embeds an NTC thermistor into the inverter coolant-inlet boss and infers winding temperature via a calibrated thermal model, deleting the standalone sensor and its fitting. It removes ~£1.7/unit and one leak-prone threaded fitting while retaining derating accuracy within ±3°C. The thermistor sits on the control PCB or in the boss; the model is calibrated on dyno against embedded winding sensors for the 800V EDU.
ideaData.materialGrade: Boss-mounted NTC thermistor + thermal model (sensor deleted)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces a threaded coolant sensor + connector/harness branch with a boss-embedded NTC and software model, deleting one leak-prone fitting and harness branch. No new hardware beyond the thermistor; calibration is a software/dyno task. Fewer parts and leak paths. The boss-mounted thermistor solders onto the existing PCB, removing one in-line fitting and leak-test point.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Embedding a thermistor and modelling temperature deletes a discrete sensor.

ideaData.riskNotes: Model-inferred winding temperature can lag transient hotspots, risking thermal protection accuracy for derating (ASIL relevance). Mitigate with conservative thermal model, dyno calibration across duty cycles, and validation against embedded sensors. Functional-safety classification to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Volvo EX90 EDU thermal control, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 analysis
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 thermal-derating safety goal

title: Single 800V coolant loop merging inverter and motor branches
description: Combine separate inverter and motor coolant branches into one series 800V EDU loop with a single inlet/outlet pair, deleting one Y-splitter, 2 hoses, and 4 quick-connects at the e-axle. Saves ~£6/unit and reduces fill/bleed complexity; flow split tuned by internal orifice. Lowers overall coolant volume ~0.4 L. Benchmarked against Porsche/PPE integrated drive cooling.

ideaData.title: Single 800V coolant loop merging inverter and motor branches
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline e-axle runs separate inverter and motor coolant branches with a Y-splitter, extra hoses, and quick-connects. This idea combines them into one series 800V EDU loop with a single inlet/outlet pair, deleting one Y-splitter, 2 hoses, and 4 quick-connects at the e-axle. It saves ~£6/unit, reduces fill/bleed complexity, and lowers coolant volume ~0.4 L; the inverter-then-motor flow split is tuned by an internal orifice. Series flow accepts the inverter's lower heat-rejection upstream of the motor on the integrated drive cooling.
ideaData.materialGrade: Single series coolant loop, internal orifice flow split
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Series single loop deletes the Y-splitter, 2 hoses, and 4 quick-connects, with one inlet/outlet pair simplifying fill/bleed and cutting coolant 0.4 L. Internal orifice replaces external balancing. Fewer external joints and leak paths at the e-axle. One inlet/outlet pair shortens the e-axle coolant manifold and reduces leak-test points at end-of-line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Merging coolant branches into one series loop deletes splitter, hoses, and connectors.

ideaData.riskNotes: Series flow raises motor inlet temperature (pre-heated by inverter), risking thermal margin at sustained power; orifice split must be robust. Mitigate with thermal DV at worst-case duty, orifice sizing analysis, and flow validation. Pressure-drop increase to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche/PPE integrated drive cooling, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Porsche PPE integrated drive cooling teardown

title: One-piece HV interface module consolidating three-phase terminals
description: Replace three individual phase connectors between inverter and motor with a single integrated three-phase busbar terminal block sealed by one gasket, deleting 2 connector bodies, 6 ring terminals, and 3 bolts. Removes ~£5.1/unit, cuts a leak interface, and lowers contact resistance. Direct laser-welded phase joints inside the housing. Benchmarked against BMW Gen6 800V integrated HV interface.

ideaData.title: One-piece HV interface module consolidating three-phase terminals
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline inverter-to-motor phase connection uses three individual phase connectors, each with connector bodies, ring terminals, and bolts. This idea replaces them with a single integrated three-phase busbar terminal block sealed by one gasket, deleting 2 connector bodies, 6 ring terminals, and 3 bolts. It removes ~£5.1/unit, cuts a leak interface, and lowers contact resistance via direct laser-welded phase joints inside the housing. The consolidated terminal block aligns the three 800V phases on one sealed plane, shortening busbar runs in the integrated EDU.
ideaData.materialGrade: Integrated 3-phase busbar terminal block, laser-welded joints
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One sealed 3-phase terminal block replaces three connectors, deleting 2 bodies, 6 ring terminals, and 3 bolts; one gasket seals all phases. Laser-welded internal joints cut contact resistance. Fewer parts, one leak interface, shorter busbars. Internal laser welding removes external bolting torque steps and the per-phase connector mating operation.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Consolidating three phase connectors into one terminal block deletes connectors and bolts.

ideaData.riskNotes: Single sealed interface concentrates 800V creepage and serviceability into one part; laser-welded joints are non-serviceable. Mitigate with creepage layout, hi-pot testing, and weld-joint DV. Repair strategy if one phase joint fails to define.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Gen6 800V integrated HV interface, 2025; benchmarked in 2025 analysis

evidenceSources.title: BMW Gen6 eDrive Neue Klasse HV integration

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100; IEC 60664 creepage

title: Aluminum HV busbars with selective plating replacing copper
description: Switch internal HV power busbars from copper to 6101-T6 aluminum with selective tin/silver plating only at bolted joints. For the 800V/400A path the cross-section grows ~30% but mass drops ~50% and material cost ~60%, netting ~£6.8/unit. Joint creep mitigated by Belleville washers. Validated for fast-charge current. Benchmarked against BYD blade-platform HV distribution.

ideaData.title: Aluminum HV busbars with selective plating replacing copper
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline internal HV power busbars are copper (C110). This idea switches them to 6101-T6 aluminium with selective tin/silver plating only at bolted joints. For the 800V/400A path the cross-section grows ~30% to match conductivity, but mass drops ~50% and material cost ~60%, netting ~£6.8/unit. Joint creep (aluminium's relaxation) is mitigated with Belleville washers and controlled torque. Plating only at contacts limits cost. The busbar is validated for fast-charge current and thermal rise; it serves the HV distribution in the blade-platform-style architecture.
ideaData.materialGrade: 6101-T6 aluminium busbar, selective Sn/Ag joint plating
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Aluminium busbars (selectively plated at joints) replace copper; cross-section grows ~30% but mass halves. Stamping/forming similar to copper; plating localised to contacts. Belleville-washer joints added. Material cost and mass drop significantly. Lighter busbars also ease handling and reduce the structural support brackets in the HV bay.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Aluminium busbars cut HV-conductor material cost and mass versus copper.

ideaData.riskNotes: Aluminium joint creep and galvanic corrosion at bolted Cu/Al interfaces risk resistance rise and hotspots at fast-charge current. Mitigate with Sn/Ag plating, Belleville washers, torque control, and thermal-cycle joint DV. Larger cross-section packaging to manage.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD blade-platform HV distribution, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100 HV; fast-charge current rating

title: Spring-contact HVIL replacing dedicated interlock loop wiring
description: Integrate the high-voltage interlock (HVIL) into the main HV connector with short-pin spring contacts instead of a separate low-voltage interlock harness routed to each HV joint. Deletes ~3 LV connectors and ~1.2 m of wire (~£2.5/unit) while keeping fail-safe disconnect detection. Simplifies service de-energization. Benchmarked against Audi PPE 800V connector system.

ideaData.title: Spring-contact HVIL replacing dedicated interlock loop wiring
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline HVIL (high-voltage interlock) uses a dedicated low-voltage loop harness routed to each HV joint to detect disconnection. This idea integrates HVIL into the main HV connector using short-pin spring contacts that break before the HV pins on disconnect, instead of a separate LV interlock harness. It deletes ~3 LV connectors and ~1.2 m of wire (~£2.5/unit) while keeping fail-safe disconnect detection and simplifying service de-energisation. The short-pin scheme provides the mandatory interlock signal within the 800V connector body itself.
ideaData.materialGrade: Short-pin spring-contact HVIL in HV connector (harness deleted)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Integrates the interlock into the HV connector via short pins, deleting ~3 LV connectors and ~1.2 m of interlock wire plus routing. Fail-safe break-before-make retained. Fewer connectors and harness length; assembly and service de-energisation simplified. Fewer routed wires shorten harness lay-up time and remove interlock-continuity test points from the line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: In-connector short-pin interlock deletes the dedicated HVIL harness.

ideaData.riskNotes: Integrated interlock must guarantee break-before-make sequencing across tolerances and vibration for fail-safe HV disconnect (safety-critical). Mitigate with contact-sequence validation, vibration/durability testing, and FMEA per ISO 26262. Regulatory acceptance of the scheme to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi PPE 800V connector system, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100 HVIL; ISO 26262; FMVSS 305

title: Inductive position sensor replacing resolver on EDU
description: Replace the wound resolver and its rotor-stator transformer with a PCB-based inductive position sensor, deleting the resolver winding assembly, its precision bracket, and the resolver-to-digital converter. Cuts ~£7.6/unit and ~0.15 kg, with comparable accuracy for FOC at 18,000 rpm and easier 800V EMC. Mounts on existing end-cap PCB. Benchmarked against Zeekr/CATL e-axle inductive sensing.

ideaData.title: Inductive position sensor replacing resolver on EDU
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU uses a wound resolver with a rotor-stator rotary transformer and a resolver-to-digital converter (RDC) for rotor position. This idea replaces it with a PCB-based inductive position sensor (planar coil target on the rotor end), deleting the resolver winding assembly, its precision bracket, and the RDC. It cuts ~£7.6/unit and ~0.15 kg, with comparable accuracy for field-oriented control at 18,000 rpm and easier 800V EMC. The sensor mounts on the existing end-cap PCB, simplifying integration and removing analogue resolver excitation circuitry.
ideaData.materialGrade: PCB inductive position sensor (vs wound resolver + RDC)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces the wound resolver, precision bracket, and RDC with a PCB coil sensor mounted on the existing end-cap PCB, deleting a sub-assembly and analogue excitation. Fewer parts, lower mass, simpler EMC. Sensor target added to rotor end; assembly simplified. Mounting on the existing end-cap PCB removes the resolver-bracket fitting and alignment station entirely.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A PCB inductive sensor replaces the resolver assembly and its converter.

ideaData.riskNotes: Inductive sensors are sensitive to air-gap, eccentricity, and high-dv/dt SiC EMC, risking position error for FOC and safety derate. Mitigate with shielded layout, eccentricity calibration, EMC validation, and ASIL-rated diagnostics. Accuracy at 18,000 rpm to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr/CATL e-axle inductive sensing, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown

evidenceSources.title: PCB inductive position sensor automotive data

ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 ASIL-D position sensing; UN/ECE R10 EMC

title: Coreless Hall current sensing replacing closed-loop fluxgate
description: Replace closed-loop fluxgate phase current sensors with PCB-integrated coreless Hall sensors measuring busbar field, deleting the magnetic core, secondary winding, and sensor module housing. Saves ~£3.4/unit across two sensed phases and reduces inverter volume. Bandwidth and accuracy meet 16 kHz SiC FOC needs with on-die temperature compensation. Benchmarked against Xpeng 800V inverter current sensing.

ideaData.title: Coreless Hall current sensing replacing closed-loop fluxgate
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline inverter senses phase current with closed-loop fluxgate sensors (magnetic core + secondary winding + module housing). This idea replaces them with PCB-integrated coreless Hall sensors measuring the magnetic field around the busbar, deleting the magnetic core, secondary winding, and sensor-module housing. It saves ~£3.4/unit across two sensed phases and reduces inverter volume. Bandwidth and accuracy meet 16 kHz SiC FOC needs with on-die temperature compensation and gradient-field rejection. The Hall die sits on the main PCB straddling the phase busbar slot.
ideaData.materialGrade: Coreless PCB Hall current sensor (vs closed-loop fluxgate)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Coreless Hall sensors on the main PCB replace fluxgate modules, deleting magnetic cores, secondary windings, and sensor housings on two phases. No separate sensor sub-assembly. Smaller inverter volume; busbar slot integrates the sense path. Fewer parts and assembly steps. PCB-level integration deletes the fluxgate module mounting and its calibration fixture from the line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Coreless Hall sensing deletes the fluxgate core, winding, and module housing.

ideaData.riskNotes: Coreless Hall accuracy is sensitive to busbar geometry, stray fields, and temperature, affecting current control and overcurrent protection. Mitigate with gradient-field layout, on-die temp compensation, calibration, and EMC validation. ASIL relevance of current sense to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng 800V inverter current sensing, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262; UN/ECE R10 EMC

title: Sensorless high-speed control reducing redundant position sensor
description: Deploy flux-observer sensorless control above 1,500 rpm with the inductive sensor used only at low speed, eliminating a redundant backup position sensor and its harness previously fitted for ASIL-D fallback. Removes ~£2.5/unit and one connector while meeting functional-safety via model redundancy. Improves field-weakening robustness at 800V. Benchmarked against Nio second-gen drive control.

ideaData.title: Sensorless high-speed control reducing redundant position sensor
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline drive control fits a redundant backup position sensor and harness for ASIL-D fallback alongside the primary sensor. This idea deploys flux-observer sensorless control above 1,500 rpm, using the inductive sensor only at low speed, so the redundant backup sensor and its harness are eliminated. It removes ~£2.5/unit and one connector while meeting functional-safety via model redundancy (analytical redundancy replaces hardware redundancy). It also improves field-weakening robustness at 800V. The observer estimates rotor flux/position from phase currents and voltages in the FOC loop.
ideaData.materialGrade: Flux-observer sensorless control (backup sensor deleted)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Sensorless observer above 1,500 rpm removes a redundant backup position sensor and connector—software-led change with no new hardware. Fewer parts and one less harness branch. Primary inductive sensor retained for low speed; assembly simplified. The change is software-led, retiring one component-placement and one connector-mating step on the PCB line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Sensorless observer provides analytical redundancy, deleting a backup sensor.

ideaData.riskNotes: Sensorless estimation degrades at low speed and during faults; replacing hardware redundancy with model redundancy must satisfy ASIL-D safety case. Mitigate with robust observer design, fault-injection testing, and independent diagnostic monitoring. Regulatory/safety-assessor acceptance to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio second-gen drive control, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 analysis

evidenceSources.title: Sensorless FOC with analytical redundancy for EV

ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 ASIL-D functional safety

title: Single-MCU consolidation of motor and DCDC control
description: Consolidate separate motor-control and auxiliary DC-DC controller MCUs onto a single automotive multi-core SoC with hardware partitioning, deleting one MCU, its crystal, power supply, and CAN transceiver. Removes ~14 components and ~£6/unit while cutting inter-controller latency. Retains ASIL-D for the motor lockstep core. Benchmarked against Hongqi 800V integrated power controller.

ideaData.title: Single-MCU consolidation of motor and DCDC control
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU runs separate MCUs for motor control and the auxiliary DC-DC controller, each with crystal, power supply, and CAN transceiver. This idea consolidates both onto a single automotive multi-core SoC with hardware partitioning, deleting one MCU, its crystal, power supply, and CAN transceiver. It removes ~14 components and ~£6/unit while cutting inter-controller latency. ASIL-D is retained on the lockstep motor-control core; the DC-DC control runs on a partitioned core with freedom-from-interference. The SoC integrates onto the existing control PCB in the 800V power controller.
ideaData.materialGrade: Single multi-core automotive SoC (Lockstep ASIL-D core)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One multi-core SoC replaces two MCUs, deleting ~14 parts (crystal, PMIC, CAN transceiver) and SMT placements. Hardware partitioning isolates motor and DC-DC functions. Fewer components, lower latency; software complexity (partitioning) rises but BOM and board area drop. Fewer SMT placements shrink board area, lowering PCB cost and reflow-defect opportunities per controller.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Consolidating two MCUs onto one SoC deletes a controller and its support parts.

ideaData.riskNotes: Co-hosting ASIL-D motor control with DC-DC on one SoC requires proven freedom-from-interference (partitioning, lockstep); fault in shared resource is the key risk. Mitigate with hardware partitioning, FFI analysis, and ISO 26262 assessment. SoC supply and software effort to manage.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Hongqi 800V integrated power controller, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 ASIL-D; freedom-from-interference

title: 3-in-1 EDU integrating motor, inverter and reducer housing
description: Integrate motor, SiC inverter, and single-speed reducer into one shared HPDC AlSi10MnMg housing, deleting separate inverter and gearbox cases, their mating flanges, ~30 fasteners, 3 external HV cables, and 4 coolant hoses. Saves ~£30/unit and ~4 kg, shortens phase busbars, and removes two leak interfaces. Benchmarked against BYD 8-in-1/3-in-1 e-axle.

ideaData.title: 3-in-1 EDU integrating motor, inverter and reducer housing
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline drive uses separate motor, inverter, and gearbox housings with mating flanges, external HV cables, and coolant hoses. This idea integrates motor, SiC inverter, and single-speed reducer into one shared HPDC AlSi10MnMg (EN AC-43500) housing—a 3-in-1 EDU—deleting separate inverter and gearbox cases, their mating flanges, ~30 fasteners, 3 external HV cables, and 4 coolant hoses. It saves ~£30/unit and ~4 kg, shortens phase busbars, and removes two external leak interfaces. The shared casting integrates coolant galleries and the inverter cavity directly.
ideaData.materialGrade: EN AC-43500 AlSi10MnMg HPDC 3-in-1 EDU housing
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One large HPDC housing replaces three cases, deleting mating flanges, ~30 fasteners, 3 HV cables, and 4 hoses. Internal busbars replace external cables. Big-casting tooling and machining fixtures added, but assembly steps, part count, and leak interfaces fall sharply. Big-casting machining replaces three separate fixturing setups, though it concentrates final-assembly test.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: 3-in-1 integration into one housing deletes cases, cables, hoses, and fasteners.

ideaData.riskNotes: Co-located inverter and motor share thermal/vibration/EMC environment; a single large casting concentrates scrap value and complicates repair. Mitigate with thermal isolation, EMC shielding, vibration DV, and high-vacuum casting yield control. NVH and serviceability to validate.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD 3-in-1/8-in-1 e-axle, 2021; benchmarked in 2022 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100; UN/ECE R10 EMC

title: High-vacuum HPDC EDU case enabling structural weld repairs delete
description: Move the EDU housing to high-vacuum die casting with optimized gating to cut gas porosity, allowing T5 heat-treat and reliable leak performance without impregnation sealing. Eliminates the vacuum-impregnation step and lowers scrap from porosity rejects from ~6% to ~1.5%, saving ~£6/unit. Enables thinner ribbed walls for ~0.5 kg mass cut. Benchmarked against Maextro/Hongqi cast e-axle housing.

ideaData.title: High-vacuum HPDC EDU case enabling structural weld repairs delete
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU housing is conventionally die-cast and requires vacuum-impregnation sealing to pass coolant/oil leak tests due to gas porosity, with notable porosity-reject scrap. This idea moves to high-vacuum HPDC with optimised gating and overflow design to cut entrapped-gas porosity, enabling T5 heat-treat and reliable leak performance without impregnation. It eliminates the vacuum-impregnation step and lowers porosity-reject scrap from ~6% to ~1.5% (~£6/unit), and enables thinner ribbed walls for ~0.5 kg mass reduction on the cast e-axle housing in AlSi10MnMg. Thinner ribbed walls additionally improve cast-fill and reduce thermal-management mass on the e-axle housing.
ideaData.materialGrade: EN AC-43500 AlSi10MnMg, high-vacuum HPDC, T5 temper
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: High-vacuum die casting with optimised gating cuts gas porosity, deleting the vacuum-impregnation sealing line and enabling T5 and thinner ribbed walls (−0.5 kg). Porosity scrap 6%→1.5%. Vacuum-system tooling/maintenance added but a full process step is removed. Deleting the impregnation tank and its consumable resin removes a full process cell and its effluent handling.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: High-vacuum casting removes the impregnation step and cuts porosity scrap.

ideaData.riskNotes: Vacuum-system reliability and gating optimisation are critical—porosity excursions reappear if vacuum drops, risking leaks. Mitigate with vacuum monitoring per shot, X-ray sampling, and pressure-decay leak test. Capital and maintenance of vacuum HPDC to manage.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Maextro/Hongqi cast e-axle housing, 2024; benchmarked in 2024 analysis

evidenceSources.title: High-vacuum HPDC vs impregnation for e-drive

title: Press-fit bearing bores deleting separate bearing carriers
description: Machine rotor and intermediate-shaft bearing seats directly into the cast EDU housing instead of using separate steel bearing carrier inserts, deleting 2 carrier parts, 8 bolts, and 2 alignment dowels. Saves ~£5.1/unit and one stack-up tolerance, improving shaft alignment for NVH. Requires precision line-boring fixture. Benchmarked against Mercedes integrated eATS housing.

ideaData.title: Press-fit bearing bores deleting separate bearing carriers
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU uses separate steel bearing-carrier inserts bolted into the housing for the rotor and intermediate shafts. This idea machines the bearing seats directly into the cast AlSi10MnMg EDU housing, deleting 2 carrier parts, 8 bolts, and 2 alignment dowels. It saves ~£5.1/unit and one stack-up tolerance, improving shaft alignment for NVH. Precision line-boring of the seats in one setup holds bore-to-bore parallelism. Steel bearing liners or direct steel-on-aluminium fits with appropriate interference and thermal-growth allowance are validated for the 800V EDU.
ideaData.materialGrade: Direct-machined bearing bores in AlSi10MnMg housing
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Line-bores bearing seats into the housing, deleting 2 steel carriers, 8 bolts, and 2 dowels; one machining setup holds bore parallelism. Removes a tolerance stack-up. Precision line-boring fixture added but part count and assembly drop, improving alignment/NVH. One-setup line-boring removes carrier-fitting and dowel-pinning stations and their inspection gates.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Machining bearing bores into the housing deletes carrier inserts and fasteners.

ideaData.riskNotes: Aluminium bore wear and differential thermal expansion vs steel bearings risk fit loss and bearing creep; line-boring precision is critical. Mitigate with steel liners/coatings, interference-fit analysis, thermal DV, and bore-roundness gating. NVH validation of alignment required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes integrated eATS housing, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 teardown

title: Hairpin laser-weld inline CT inspection cutting end-of-line scrap
description: Add inline optical/thermal weld inspection of all hairpin joints right after laser welding instead of catching defects at end-of-line hi-pot, so a single bad weld is reworked early rather than scrapping a finished stator worth ~£119. First-pass quality detection raises yield ~2.5 points, saving ~£3/unit net of the camera cell. Benchmarked against Audi PPE hairpin welding line.

ideaData.title: Hairpin laser-weld inline CT inspection cutting end-of-line scrap
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline hairpin stator welds are only verified at end-of-line hi-pot, so a single bad crown weld can scrap a finished stator worth ~£119. This idea adds inline optical/thermal weld inspection of all hairpin joints immediately after laser welding, so a defective weld is detected and reworked early rather than scrapping a completed stator. First-pass quality detection raises yield ~2.5 points, saving ~£3/unit net of the camera cell. The vision/thermal cell checks weld-bead geometry and porosity on each crown joint on the 800V hairpin line.
ideaData.materialGrade: Inline optical/thermal weld inspection of hairpin crown joints
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds a vision/thermal inspection cell right after laser welding to catch defects per-joint, enabling early rework instead of end-of-line scrap of a ~£119 stator. Raises first-pass yield ~2.5 points. Camera-cell capital added but scrap value recovered; no change to welding itself.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Inline weld inspection catches defects early, avoiding finished-stator scrap.

ideaData.riskNotes: Inspection must reliably distinguish acceptable vs defective welds (false-reject/escape balance); missed defects still reach hi-pot. Mitigate with calibrated vision+thermal fusion, defect-library training, and correlation to hi-pot results. Cell throughput must match line takt.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi PPE hairpin welding line, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 line study

evidenceSources.title: Audi PPE hairpin stator weld teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: IEC 60034 winding integrity

title: Die-cast copper rotor cage option for induction auxiliary motor
description: For the front induction (ASM) motor of a dual-motor 800V AWD, adopt a die-cast copper rotor cage instead of aluminum, raising rotor conductivity ~40% to cut rotor I²R loss and remove all rare-earth magnets entirely. The magnet-free rotor saves ~£13/unit versus an IPM front motor and improves drag-free coasting efficiency. Benchmarked against Audi e-tron/PPE front ASM.

ideaData.title: Die-cast copper rotor cage option for induction auxiliary motor
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline front induction (ASM) motor of a dual-motor 800V AWD uses a die-cast aluminium rotor cage, or an IPM front motor with magnets. This idea adopts a die-cast copper rotor cage, raising rotor conductivity ~40% versus aluminium to cut rotor I²R loss and—being induction—removes all rare-earth magnets entirely. The magnet-free rotor saves ~£13/unit versus an IPM front motor and improves drag-free coasting efficiency (no spin loss from magnets). Copper die-casting requires high mould temperature and tooling robust to molten copper on the front ASM.
ideaData.materialGrade: Die-cast copper rotor cage (ASM), vs aluminium / IPM magnets
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Copper die-casting replaces aluminium cage or an IPM rotor, removing magnets entirely. Higher-temperature copper casting needs robust (often heated) tooling and shorter die life. Net rare-earth elimination and lower rotor loss; rotor processing changes but magnet handling is removed. Heated copper die-casting needs robust tooling and shorter die life, but deletes all magnet-handling stations.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A die-cast copper induction rotor removes rare-earth magnets and cuts rotor loss.

ideaData.riskNotes: Copper die-casting suffers short die life and porosity at high melt temperature, risking cage voids and bar breakage; induction efficiency lags IPM at part load. Mitigate with heated dies, X-ray bar inspection, and drive-cycle efficiency validation. Tooling cost vs magnet saving to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi e-tron/PPE front ASM, 2019; benchmarked in 2021 teardown

evidenceSources.title: Audi e-tron front induction-motor copper-rotor teardown
Die-cast copper rotor for traction induction motors

title: Reduced-magnet-count spoke rotor using ferrite-assist design
description: Adopt a hybrid spoke rotor combining a reduced NdFeB content with flux-concentrating ferrite segments, cutting heavy/light rare-earth magnet mass ~25% for the same airgap flux. Saves ~£8.5/unit on a 2.2 kg magnet set while ferrite adds negligible cost. Spoke geometry raises torque density and tolerates higher temperature. Benchmarked against Denza/BYD low-rare-earth motor program.

ideaData.title: Reduced-magnet-count spoke rotor using ferrite-assist design
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline IPM rotor relies fully on NdFeB magnets for airgap flux. This idea adopts a hybrid spoke rotor combining reduced NdFeB content with flux-concentrating ferrite segments arranged radially (spoke), cutting heavy/light rare-earth magnet mass ~25% for the same airgap flux. It saves ~£8.5/unit on a 2.2 kg magnet set while low-cost ferrite adds negligible cost. The spoke geometry concentrates flux to raise torque density and tolerates higher temperature (less Dy needed). Rotor laminations in M270-35A retain the magnets against centrifugal load at the spoke posts.
ideaData.materialGrade: Hybrid spoke rotor: reduced NdFeB + ferrite; M270-35A laminations
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Spoke geometry with ferrite flux-concentration replaces some NdFeB, cutting rare-earth mass 25%. New lamination die for spoke pockets and added ferrite-insertion step; NdFeB volume drops. Higher temperature tolerance reduces Dy. Magnet-assembly fixturing changes but rare-earth content falls. Ferrite handling adds a brittle-part station but NdFeB volume and magnet-sorting load both fall.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Ferrite-assisted spoke rotor cuts rare-earth magnet mass for equal flux.

ideaData.riskNotes: Spoke posts carry high centrifugal stress and demag risk; brittle ferrite handling and combined-magnet manufacturing add complexity. Mitigate with FEA post sizing, burst-spin testing, demag DV at temperature, and robust ferrite fixturing. Torque ripple/NVH of spoke design to validate.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Denza/BYD low-rare-earth motor program, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 analysis

title: Hybrid ceramic-steel bearing only on high-speed rotor end
description: Use a hybrid ceramic-ball bearing solely at the high-speed rotor drive end to handle 18,000 rpm and electrical-discharge risk, while retaining standard steel bearings elsewhere, rather than fitting premium bearings throughout. Targeted use removes the need for a separate shaft grounding ring and cuts bearing-current EDM failures, net ~£2.5/unit including warranty. Benchmarked against Porsche Taycan high-speed drive bearings.

ideaData.title: Hybrid ceramic-steel bearing only on high-speed rotor end
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rotor bearings are all standard steel, or premium bearings are fitted throughout to address 18,000 rpm and electrical-discharge (EDM) bearing-current risk. This idea uses a hybrid ceramic-ball (Si3N4 balls, steel races) bearing solely at the high-speed rotor drive end to handle speed and break the bearing-current path, while retaining standard steel bearings elsewhere. Targeted use removes the need for a separate shaft-grounding ring and cuts bearing-current EDM failures, net ~£2.5/unit including warranty. The ceramic balls are electrically insulating, blocking circulating currents from SiC dv/dt.
ideaData.materialGrade: Hybrid Si3N4 ceramic-ball bearing (drive-end only)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Fits a hybrid ceramic bearing only at the high-speed drive end and deletes the shaft-grounding ring, rather than premium bearings throughout. One bearing changes; assembly otherwise unchanged. Ceramic insulation blocks EDM currents, reducing warranty failures. Deleting the grounding ring removes one press-fit and its contact-resistance test from rotor assembly.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Targeted ceramic bearing blocks bearing currents and deletes the grounding ring.

ideaData.riskNotes: Ceramic-ball bearings cost more and are sensitive to shock loading; deleting the grounding ring relies on full current interruption across all conditions. Mitigate with bearing-current measurement, shock-load DV, and EDM-life testing across SiC dv/dt. Validation of single-end sufficiency required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Taycan high-speed drive bearings, 2020; benchmarked in 2021 teardown

title: Full 8-in-1 800V power-electronics integration at system level
description: Integrate motor, SiC inverter, reducer, OBC, DC-DC, PDU, BMS interface, and VCU control into one 8-in-1 800V e-axle sharing housing, coolant loop, and HV busbars. Versus discrete boxes this deletes ~6 enclosures, ~12 HV connectors, and ~8 coolant joints, saving ~£51/vehicle and ~12 kg while cutting harness length. Common 800V DC backbone simplifies fast charge. Benchmarked against BYD 8-in-1 e-axle.

ideaData.title: Full 8-in-1 800V power-electronics integration at system level
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 800V powertrain uses discrete boxes for motor, inverter, reducer, OBC, DC-DC, PDU, BMS interface, and VCU, each with enclosure, connectors, and cooling. This idea integrates all eight into one 8-in-1 800V e-axle sharing housing, coolant loop, and HV busbars. Versus discrete boxes it deletes ~6 enclosures, ~12 HV connectors, and ~8 coolant joints, saving ~£51/vehicle and ~12 kg while cutting harness length. A common 800V DC backbone simplifies fast charge. The shared HPDC AlSi10MnMg housing carries internal busbars and cast coolant galleries across all functions.
ideaData.materialGrade: 8-in-1 HPDC AlSi10MnMg e-axle, shared HV busbars + coolant
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Integrates eight power-electronics functions into one housing, deleting ~6 enclosures, ~12 HV connectors, and ~8 coolant joints; internal busbars replace cables. Large shared casting and complex assembly/test, but massive cuts in part count, harness length, and external interfaces. Modular sub-assembly test partitions the deep integration so faulty modules are caught before final close-up.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: 8-in-1 integration deletes enclosures, connectors, and coolant joints across the powertrain.

ideaData.riskNotes: Deep integration concentrates thermal/EMC/vibration coupling and makes any single fault scrap a high-value unit; serviceability and supplier scope are challenges. Mitigate with partitioned thermal/EMC zones, modular sub-assembly test, and robust DV. Repair-vs-replace strategy to define.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD 8-in-1 e-axle, 2021; benchmarked in 2022 teardown
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100; UN/ECE R10 EMC

title: Boost-charge via motor windings deleting standalone 800V booster
description: Reuse the drive motor windings and inverter as a boost converter to charge an 800V pack from a 400V DC fast charger, deleting the dedicated standalone DC-DC boost module, its magnetics, and cooling. Removes a ~£38/vehicle assembly and ~5 kg while retaining 400V-station compatibility. Requires firmware and contactor reconfiguration only. Benchmarked against Xpeng/Hyundai-style 800V boost charging.

ideaData.title: Boost-charge via motor windings deleting standalone 800V booster
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 800V vehicles charging at 400V DC stations use a dedicated standalone DC-DC boost module (with magnetics and cooling) to step 400V up to the 800V pack. This idea reuses the drive motor windings (as inductors) and the inverter (as a boost converter) to perform the boost, deleting the standalone boost module, its magnetics, and cooling. It removes a ~£38/vehicle assembly and ~5 kg while retaining 400V-station compatibility. The change is mainly firmware plus a contactor/relay reconfiguration to route the charge current through the inverter and windings.
ideaData.materialGrade: Motor-winding/inverter boost (standalone DC-DC deleted)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Reuses existing motor windings and inverter as the boost stage via firmware and added contactors, deleting the dedicated boost module, its magnetics, and cooling. No new power hardware beyond switching/relays; large enclosure and ~5 kg removed. Software/controls effort is the main cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Reusing the motor and inverter for boost-charging deletes the standalone DC-DC module.

ideaData.riskNotes: Boost-charging heats windings/inverter and can apply torque-producing current—rotor lock/thermal and EMC during charge are risks. Mitigate with zero-torque current control, thermal limits, contactor sequencing, and charge-mode safety case. Station interoperability validation required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng/Hyundai-style 800V boost charging, 2021; benchmarked in 2022 analysis
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100; GB/T 18487 charging; ISO 15118

title: Shared 800V coolant and HV architecture across front/rear EDUs
description: Standardize one 800V HV-distribution and coolant architecture shared by front and rear EDUs using common connectors, busbar pitch, and a single series coolant manifold, removing variant-specific harnesses and duplicate PDU branches. Cuts ~£17/vehicle and reduces part numbers ~30% across the AWD platform. Eases plant complexity and spares. Benchmarked against Zeekr 800V AWD platform.

ideaData.title: Shared 800V coolant and HV architecture across front/rear EDUs
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline AWD platform fits variant-specific HV harnesses and duplicate PDU branches for front and rear EDUs. This idea standardises one 800V HV-distribution and coolant architecture shared by both EDUs—common connectors, busbar pitch, and a single series coolant manifold—removing variant-specific harnesses and duplicate PDU branches. It cuts ~£17/vehicle and reduces part numbers ~30% across the AWD platform, easing plant complexity and spares. The shared manifold and standardised 800V connector pitch let front and rear units use carry-over interfaces. Standardised interfaces also let the same coolant manifold and HV connector tooling serve both axles, cutting validation scope.
ideaData.materialGrade: Common 800V HV + single coolant manifold architecture
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Standardises connectors, busbar pitch, and one coolant manifold across front/rear EDUs, removing variant harnesses and duplicate PDU branches. Cuts part numbers ~30%, easing plant logistics and spares. Mainly a design/architecture standardisation; tooling reused across variants. Fewer unique part numbers cut line-side bins, kitting variants, and changeover time between front/rear builds.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Commonising HV and coolant architecture across EDUs cuts part numbers and duplicate branches.
