ideaData.riskNotes: Common architecture must satisfy both front and rear EDU current/thermal needs without over-spec penalty; series coolant manifold may compromise one unit's margin. Mitigate with worst-case sizing, flow-balance analysis, and platform DV. Constraint on future variant flexibility to manage.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr 800V AWD platform, 2023; benchmarked in 2023 analysis

evidenceSources.title: Zeekr AWD front/rear EDU interface teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R100

title: Platform inverter scaling via die-count instead of unique designs
description: Scale inverter power across the lineup by varying SiC die count on a common power-module footprint and shared gate driver/control PCB, rather than designing unique inverters per motor. Reuse of busbars, caps, and housing across 3 power classes cuts NRE and raises volume per part number, lowering unit cost ~£10 via economies of scale. Benchmarked against Mercedes modular inverter family.

ideaData.title: Platform inverter scaling via die-count instead of unique designs
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline inverter range uses unique designs per motor power class, multiplying NRE and part numbers. This idea scales inverter power by varying SiC die count on a common power-module footprint and a shared gate-driver/control PCB, rather than redesigning per motor. Reuse of busbars, DC-link caps, and housing across 3 power classes cuts NRE and raises volume per part number, lowering unit cost ~£10 via economies of scale. Only the SiC die population and thermal sizing change between variants on the modular inverter family.
ideaData.materialGrade: Common SiC power-module footprint, die-count scaling
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Scales power by die count on one module/PCB/housing across 3 classes, reusing busbars, caps, and enclosure. Cuts NRE and consolidates part numbers, raising per-part volume. Manufacturing lines and tooling are shared; only die population and thermal trim vary. Shared lines and tooling across three classes raise utilisation and cut per-variant fixture and test sets.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Die-count scaling on a common platform raises volume and cuts inverter unit cost.

ideaData.riskNotes: A common footprint may over-spec low-power or under-cool high-power variants, eroding optimality; shared design must validate across the full range. Mitigate with worst-case thermal/EMC sizing, per-variant DV, and derating maps. Lower-volume class margins to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes modular inverter family, 2022; benchmarked in 2023 analysis

evidenceSources.title: Modular SiC inverter platform cost study

title: Common EDU platform with module-count motor length scaling
description: Define one EDU family where torque scales by stator stack length and SiC die count on shared housing, reducer, and bearing set, eliminating bespoke EDUs per model. Carry-over of ~70% of parts across power variants raises volume and cuts tooling amortization, lowering EDU unit cost ~£15. Simplifies validation and plant changeover. Benchmarked against BMW Gen6 modular eDrive family.

ideaData.title: Common EDU platform with module-count motor length scaling
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline lineup uses bespoke EDUs per model, each with unique housing, reducer, and bearings. This idea defines one EDU family where torque scales by stator stack length and SiC die count on a shared housing, reducer, and bearing set, eliminating model-specific EDUs. Carry-over of ~70% of parts across power variants raises volume and cuts tooling amortisation, lowering EDU unit cost ~£15. It also simplifies validation and plant changeover. Only stack length, magnet/winding spec, and die count vary; the housing accommodates the longest stack with shimmed spacers for shorter ones.
ideaData.materialGrade: Common EDU platform; stack-length + die-count scaling
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Shared housing, reducer, and bearings across power variants with stack-length/die-count scaling; ~70% part carry-over raises volume and cuts tooling amortisation. Plant changeover and validation simplified. Housing sized for longest stack; spacers handle shorter variants. Shared housing and reducer tooling cut changeover and reduce fixture sets carried per model on the line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A common EDU platform with stack-length scaling raises carry-over and cuts unit cost.

ideaData.riskNotes: Common housing sized for the largest variant adds mass/cost to smaller ones; shared reducer must cover the full torque range. Mitigate with platform load-case envelope, variant-specific DV, and mass/cost trade-off analysis. Constraint on extreme-variant optimisation to manage.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Gen6 modular eDrive family, 2025; benchmarked in 2025 analysis

evidenceSources.title: BMW Gen6 eDrive Neue Klasse modular family

title: Magnet-free externally-excited rotor EDU eliminating rare earth
description: Adopt an externally-excited synchronous motor (EESM) using a wound rotor with inductive (brushless) excitation, removing all NdFeB magnets and Dy/Tb from the EDU. Eliminates ~£26/unit of rare-earth magnets and supply-chain risk, improves high-speed efficiency via field control, and cuts spin loss when coasting. Adds rotor winding and exciter offset by magnet delete. Benchmarked against BMW Gen5 EESM eDrive.

ideaData.title: Magnet-free externally-excited rotor EDU eliminating rare earth
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline EDU uses an NdFeB IPM motor with Dy/Tb-doped magnets. This idea adopts an externally-excited synchronous motor (EESM) with a wound rotor energised by inductive (brushless) excitation, removing all NdFeB magnets and heavy rare earth from the EDU. It eliminates ~£26/unit of rare-earth magnets and the associated supply-chain risk, improves high-speed efficiency via active field control, and cuts spin (drag) loss when coasting since field can be zeroed. The added rotor winding, exciter, and rotary transformer are offset by the magnet delete. Copper rotor winding and rotor cooling are key on the 800V EESM.
ideaData.materialGrade: Externally-excited (EESM) wound copper rotor, brushless exciter
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces the magnet IPM rotor with a wound copper rotor plus brushless rotary-transformer exciter, deleting all NdFeB/Dy. Adds rotor winding, slip-ring-free exciter, and rotor cooling, but removes magnet supply and handling. Field-control electronics added; net rare-earth elimination. Removing magnet insertion deletes a clean-handling cell; rotor winding and exciter add new but stable stations.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: An externally-excited rotor eliminates all rare-earth magnets from the drive.

ideaData.riskNotes: EESM adds rotor copper loss, an exciter, and rotor cooling complexity; rotor thermal management and exciter reliability are key risks at high speed. Mitigate with rotor oil-cooling, robust brushless exciter design, and efficiency/thermal DV across the drive cycle. Cost balance vs magnet price to confirm.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Gen5 EESM eDrive, 2021; benchmarked in 2022 teardown

evidenceSources.title: BMW iX3/Gen5 EESM eDrive rotor teardown

title: AA6082-T6 forged steering knuckle replacing GJS-450 cast iron
description: Switch the front upright from GJS-450 ductile-iron casting to a closed-die AA6082-T6 aluminium forging. Removes 2.1kg/corner (8.4kg/vehicle), cuts the machining envelope by 30% and eliminates two casting cores. Net saving roughly £9.3/corner from reduced unsprung mass enabling smaller brakes and lighter wheel bearings. Forging tooling amortises over 280k units. Benchmarks BMW G60 5-Series aluminium knuckle architecture.

ideaData.title: AA6082-T6 forged steering knuckle replacing GJS-450 cast iron
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline front upright is a GJS-450-10 ductile-iron sand casting weighing ~5.0kg/corner, requiring two sand cores for the damper bore and bearing seat. The change forges it from AA6082-T6 (Al-Mg-Si, ~310 MPa yield after T6 solution+artificial age) on a 4000t closed-die press, then T6 heat-treats and CNC-machines the bearing bore, ball-joint taper and brake-caliper pads. Forged flow lines give superior fatigue versus cast iron at ~40% the density, allowing a 2.1kg/corner reduction. The wrought structure tolerates thinner sections, shrinking the rough-machining envelope ~30% and deleting both casting cores. Reduced unsprung mass permits downsized wheel bearings and brake hardware.
ideaData.materialGrade: AA6082-T6 forged (vs GJS-450-10 ductile iron)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Closed-die forging on a 4000t press replaces the iron foundry and core-making cells; removes two sand cores. CNC machining envelope drops ~30%, cutting cycle time and tool wear. Requires T6 solution/age furnace line. Forging die amortises over 280k units; near-net forging lifts material utilisation versus rough casting.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Lighter wrought corner cuts material mass and machining while enabling downsized brakes/bearings.

ideaData.riskNotes: Aluminium knuckle fatigue at ball-joint and bearing seats needs full corner durability and pothole/curb-strike validation; galvanic corrosion at steel fastener interfaces requires isolation. Forging die lead-time and a single forging supplier create supply risk; bore tolerance after T6 distortion must be controlled.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW G60 5-Series aluminium front knuckle, 2023; benchmarked in 2024 chassis teardown

evidenceSources.title: BMW G60 front suspension corner teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79 steering integrity; component durability per OEM curb-strike load cases

title: 980MPa dual-phase steel stamped lower control arm
description: Replace forged-steel front lower control arm with a two-shell 980MPa DP stamped-and-welded design. Down-gauges wall from 4.2mm to 2.8mm, saving 1.3kg/arm while meeting 35kN buckling. Stamping replaces forging plus six machining ops, dropping piece cost about £6/corner and halving cycle time. Higher yield strength removes a heat-treat step. Benchmarks Mercedes EQE rear multilink stamped arm strategy.

ideaData.title: 980MPa dual-phase steel stamped lower control arm
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline front lower control arm is a forged-steel (C45/microalloyed) part with ~4.2mm effective walls, requiring six machining operations and a normalising heat-treat. The change stamps two half-shells from 2.8mm DP980 dual-phase steel (980 MPa UTS, ~650 MPa yield, ferrite-martensite microstructure) and MIG/laser-welds them into a closed box. The high yield strength carries the 35kN longitudinal buckling load at reduced gauge, dropping 1.3kg/arm. Because DP980 is cold-formed and work-strengthened, the forging-route normalising step is deleted. Spot/laser-welded seams and a CDP e-coat give corrosion durability; bushing and ball-joint bores are pierced and reamed in the stamping die set.
ideaData.materialGrade: DP980 dual-phase steel (980 MPa UTS), 2.8mm
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Progressive stamping plus a weld station replaces forging and six machining ops; cycle time roughly halves. Deletes the normalising heat-treat furnace. Springback control on DP980 requires die compensation and higher press tonnage. Two-shell box build adds a weld fixture but cuts net part cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: High-strength steel lets down-gauging replace forging and a heat-treat step.

ideaData.riskNotes: DP980 springback and edge-cracking at pierced holes need die compensation and trimmed-edge ductility control; weld-seam fatigue on the closed box must pass full arm durability. Two-shell stamping can trap moisture, so drain/seal and e-coat coverage require validation. Tolerance stack at bushing bores tighter than forging.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes EQE rear multilink stamped-arm strategy, 2022; benchmarked 2023

evidenceSources.title: Mercedes EQE rear multilink arm teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: Component durability per OEM chassis load cases; no specific regulation

title: Hollow tube stabiliser bar replacing solid forged bar
description: Convert solid 26mm front anti-roll bar to a DOM seamless hollow tube (28mm OD, 4mm wall) with the same roll stiffness. Mass drops from 4.6kg to 2.7kg, a 41% reduction, while bending fatigue is maintained via induction-hardened ends. Saves about £4.7/vehicle in raw steel and reduces forging energy. Benchmarks Audi Q5 hollow stabiliser bar adoption.

ideaData.title: Hollow tube stabiliser bar replacing solid forged bar
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline is a solid 26mm-diameter bar in 51CrV4 spring steel, bent and induction-hardened. The change uses a DOM (drawn-over-mandrel) seamless tube of 28mm OD × 4mm wall in 26MnB5/equivalent, CNC-bent then quench-and-tempered, with induction-hardened formed eyes. Because torsional stiffness scales with the polar moment of the section, a slightly larger OD with a hollow core matches the solid bar's roll rate while removing the low-stress core material, cutting mass from 4.6kg to 2.7kg (−41%). Bending and torsional fatigue at the lever arms are preserved by induction hardening and shot-peening the formed ends. Lower mass reduces forging/heating energy and raw steel input.
ideaData.materialGrade: 26MnB5/51CrV4 DOM tube, 28mm OD × 4mm wall
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Tube bending and end-forming replace solid-bar forging; reduces furnace heating energy and raw steel mass ~40%. Adds tube-end flattening/eye-forming and shot-peen. Induction hardening line retained. Lower handling mass eases automation. End-form fatigue requires process control.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Removing low-stress core steel cuts mass, raw material and forging energy at equal roll stiffness.

ideaData.riskNotes: Tube-end forming can thin walls and seed fatigue cracks, so eye geometry and shot-peen coverage need validation; internal corrosion of the bore requires sealing or coating. Wall eccentricity from DOM drawing affects stiffness scatter and must be controlled. Mature technology, low overall risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi Q5 hollow front stabiliser bar, 2021; widely teardown-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Audi Q5 hollow anti-roll bar teardown

title: GFRP composite transverse leaf spring rear axle module
description: Replace two rear coil springs plus lower links with a single glass-fibre composite transverse leaf spring that also reacts lateral loads. Deletes two coils, two spring seats and one link per axle, saving 4.8kg/vehicle and removing eight assembly fasteners. System cost falls roughly £15/vehicle at 150k volume despite higher material price. Benchmarks Volvo SPA transverse composite leaf spring.

ideaData.title: GFRP composite transverse leaf spring rear axle module
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline rear axle uses two steel coil springs, two spring seats/isolators and a discrete lower lateral link per side. The change is a single transverse glass-fibre-reinforced epoxy (E-glass/epoxy, ~45 vol% UD fibre) leaf spring spanning the axle, mounted at two body points and connected to both wheel carriers. The anisotropic UD layup carries vertical wheel rate while its in-plane stiffness reacts lateral cornering loads, letting it replace a lower link. The leaf is made by RTM or compression-moulding of prepreg, then NDT-inspected. Composite specific strain energy is far higher than spring steel, so one moulded part deletes two coils, two seats and one link, cutting 4.8kg/vehicle and eight fasteners.
ideaData.materialGrade: E-glass/epoxy UD GFRP transverse leaf (~45 vol% fibre)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: RTM/compression-moulding cell replaces coil-winding, seat stamping and link forging lines; one moulded part consolidates five components. Adds composite NDT (ultrasonic/CT) and bonded/bolted end-fitting bonding. Removes eight fastener install operations at axle assembly. Cure-cycle takt is the line constraint.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Multifunction composite leaf consolidates springs and a link, cutting parts, fasteners and mass.

ideaData.riskNotes: GFRP creep, stone-impact damage and end-fitting bond fatigue need long-term durability and damage-tolerance validation; cure quality and void content require per-part NDT. Lateral-load reaction through the leaf changes kinematics, demanding full K&C and crash validation. Single composite supplier and high material cost are risks.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Volvo SPA transverse composite leaf spring (XC90/S90), 2016; production-verified

evidenceSources.title: Volvo SPA rear transverse leaf spring teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: Rear suspension durability and crash load paths per Euro NCAP and OEM standards

title: Flow-formed aluminium wheel replacing low-pressure cast
description: Move 19-inch wheels from gravity low-pressure casting to flow-formed (spin-forged) rim barrels. Grain refinement allows 1.4kg/wheel reduction (5.6kg/set) at equal radial fatigue, and the thinner rim cuts aluminium input 12%. Net saving about £7.6/wheel from material and improved scrap yield, offsetting the flow-forming machine. Benchmarks Zeekr 001 flow-formed wheel program.

ideaData.title: Flow-formed aluminium wheel replacing low-pressure cast
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline 19-inch wheel is fully low-pressure die-cast in A356.2 (AlSi7Mg0.3) and T6-treated, giving a relatively coarse, porous rim. The change casts only the centre/spider then flow-forms (spin-forges) the rim barrel: a cast preform barrel is heated and rolled against rollers over a mandrel, axially elongating and radially compressing the metal. This work refines grain, closes porosity and aligns flow, roughly doubling barrel yield strength so the rim can be thinned ~12% at equal radial/cornering fatigue (per SAE rotary and 13°-impact tests). Mass falls 1.4kg/wheel. Reduced aluminium input plus higher metal utilisation and lower scrap drive the saving, partially offsetting the flow-forming machine investment.
ideaData.materialGrade: A356.2 (AlSi7Mg0.3-T6) flow-formed barrel
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds a flow-forming (spin-forging) machine after low-pressure casting; rim barrel walls thin ~12%, cutting Al input and improving scrap yield. Improved metallurgy reduces porosity-driven scrap. Slightly longer process chain but lower per-wheel material cost; T6 furnace retained.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Spin-forging refines grain to thin the rim, cutting aluminium input and scrap at equal fatigue.

ideaData.riskNotes: Flow-forming process window (preheat, roller feed/pressure) must be tightly controlled to avoid laps and wall-thickness scatter; thinner rim demands re-validation of 13° and 90° impact and rotary-bending fatigue per regulation. Machine capex and cycle time can constrain throughput. Mature in industry, moderate risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr 001 flow-formed wheel program, 2022; teardown-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Flow-forming vs cast wheel fatigue data

ideaData.regulatoryContext: Wheel impact/fatigue per ECE R124 and GB/T 5334 (impact, rotary bending, radial fatigue)

title: Single-piece cast aluminium rear subframe vs welded steel
description: Replace 14-piece welded steel rear subframe with a single AlSi10MnMg high-pressure die-cast unit. Consolidates 14 stampings and 38 weld joints into one casting, removing the entire weld cell and saving 6.5kg/vehicle. Tooling is high but piece cost drops about £19/vehicle and takt time falls 40s. Benchmarks Tesla-style megacasting echoed in Xpeng G6 rear underbody.

ideaData.title: Single-piece cast aluminium rear subframe vs welded steel
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline rear subframe is a 14-piece assembly of CR340/590 steel stampings joined by ~38 MIG/spot welds, requiring a dedicated weld cell with fixtures and inline weld inspection. The change is a single high-pressure die-cast (HPDC) unit in AlSi10MnMg (self-hardening F or T5, ~120 MPa yield) cast on a large-tonnage (≥6000t) machine, with cast-in suspension pickup bosses and machined mounting faces. Consolidating 14 parts and 38 joints into one casting deletes the weld cell, cuts 6.5kg/vehicle and removes weld-distortion stack-up, improving pickup-point precision. Heat-treat-free AlSiMnMg alloy avoids quench distortion. NDT (X-ray) and CNC of the bushing/bolt interfaces complete the part.
ideaData.materialGrade: AlSi10MnMg HPDC (heat-treat-free, ~120 MPa yield)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: ≥6000t HPDC machine and large die replace 14 stamping dies and the entire rear-subframe weld cell with its 38 joints. Cuts ~40s takt and weld inspection. Adds X-ray NDT and CNC of pickup faces. High die capex amortised over volume; vacuum die-casting needed for integrity.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One megacasting deletes 13 parts, 38 welds and the weld cell, slashing labour and mass.

ideaData.riskNotes: HPDC porosity at thick suspension nodes risks fatigue failure, requiring vacuum casting and per-part X-ray; repairability after crash is poor versus steel. Large-die capex and a single casting supplier raise tooling and supply risk. Alloy ductility for crash energy absorption needs validation against load cases.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng G6 rear underbody gigacasting, 2023; teardown-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: Rear crash load paths and suspension durability per Euro NCAP / C-NCAP and OEM standards

title: Friction-welded tubular half-shaft replacing solid bar
description: Replace solid forged half-shaft bar with a friction-welded tubular shaft joining two splined stub ends to a thin-wall tube. Torsional capacity of 3200Nm is retained while mass drops 1.1kg/side and rotating inertia falls 22%. Friction welding removes a forging operation and reduces steel input, saving about £5.1/side. Benchmarks BYD e-axle tubular half-shaft.

ideaData.title: Friction-welded tubular half-shaft replacing solid bar
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline interconnecting shaft is a solid forged-and-machined bar in 1045/37Cr4 induction-hardened steel. The change rotary-friction-welds two splined forged stub ends onto a seamless thin-wall tube (e.g. 28mm OD), forming a hollow shaft. Because torsional stiffness and strength depend on the polar section modulus, which is dominated by outer radius, a hollow tube retains the 3200Nm capacity while removing low-stress core steel, cutting 1.1kg/side and lowering rotating inertia ~22% (improving NVH and efficiency). Rotary friction welding produces a forged-quality solid-state joint without filler, then the weld flash is turned off and the splines induction-hardened. Steel input and a forging pass are reduced.
ideaData.materialGrade: 37Cr4/1045 tube + forged splined stubs, friction-welded
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Rotary friction-welding cell joins tube to stub ends, replacing a solid-bar forging operation and reducing steel mass. Adds weld-flash turning and weld-zone NDT. Induction-hardening of splines retained. Lower rotating inertia eases balancing. Solid-state weld avoids filler/consumables.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Hollow friction-welded shaft removes core steel and a forging pass at equal torque capacity.

ideaData.riskNotes: Friction-weld joint integrity at the tube-to-stub interface is critical; flash, bond-line oxides and HAZ properties need weld-monitoring and torsional fatigue validation to 3200Nm. Tube wall eccentricity affects balance and stiffness scatter. Process is mature in driveline; supplier weld-parameter control is the main risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD e-axle tubular friction-welded half-shaft, 2022; teardown-confirmed

title: Integrated wheel-bearing hub (Gen3) replacing Gen1 plus knuckle press
description: Adopt a Gen3 flanged hub-bearing unit that bolts directly to the upright, deleting the press-fit Gen1 bearing, separate hub and two snap rings. Removes the press station and one balancing op, cutting warranty NVH claims. Assembly time falls 25s/corner and piece cost about £3.4/corner net. Pre-greased sealed-for-life design. Benchmarks Porsche Macan EV Gen3 hub.

ideaData.title: Integrated wheel-bearing hub (Gen3) replacing Gen1 plus knuckle press
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline corner uses a Gen1 double-row angular-contact ball bearing press-fit into the knuckle, a separate forged hub and two retaining snap rings, demanding a press-and-stake station and a hub balancing op. The change adopts a Gen3 flanged hub-bearing unit: a single pre-greased, sealed-for-life cartridge in 100Cr6/SAE52100 bearing steel with an integral wheel-mounting flange and a bolt-on flange to the upright. Bolting replaces the interference press, deleting the Gen1 bearing, separate hub and both snap rings. The factory-set preload and integrated seals reduce NVH and contamination warranty claims. Assembly becomes a torque-to-spec bolt operation, removing press-fit scrap and the balancing step.
ideaData.materialGrade: SAE52100/100Cr6 sealed Gen3 hub-bearing unit
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Bolt-on Gen3 cartridge deletes the bearing press-fit station, the separate hub, two snap rings and one balancing op. Cuts ~25s/corner assembly and press-fit scrap. Adds torque-monitored bolting. Factory-set preload improves quality consistency; sealed-for-life removes service relube.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Consolidated bolt-on hub-bearing removes parts, a press station and NVH warranty.

ideaData.riskNotes: Bolted flange must maintain bearing preload and runout under load; flange-face flatness and bolt-tension control are critical to avoid seal lip wear and NVH. Sealed-for-life grease must survive thermal cycling from regen braking. Mature technology; supplier preload consistency is the key risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Macan EV Gen3 hub-bearing, 2024; teardown-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Porsche Macan EV front corner teardown

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79; wheel-bearing durability per OEM corner load cases

title: Carbon-ceramic-free: cast-iron disc with laser-clad coating
description: Apply a laser-clad tungsten-carbide friction layer to a grey-iron GG20 disc, eliminating the carbon-ceramic option while meeting EU7 brake-dust limits. Wear and corrosion drop 90% versus bare iron, deleting zinc plating and extending service to 150k km. Adds £26/disc cladding but avoids £238 ceramic premium, saving roughly £34/corner on equipped trims. Benchmarks Mercedes-Porsche surface-coated disc.

ideaData.title: Carbon-ceramic-free: cast-iron disc with laser-clad coating
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline premium brake option is a carbon-ceramic (C/SiC) disc costing ~£238/corner over iron, chosen for wear, dust and corrosion. The change keeps a grey-iron GG20/EN-GJL-200 disc but applies a laser metal-deposition (LMD) clad layer: a tungsten-carbide-reinforced metal-matrix coating (WC in a Fe/Ni matrix) is fused onto the friction faces by a high-power diode laser, then ground flat. The hard carbide layer cuts wear and corrosion ~90% versus bare iron, deleting the zinc anti-corrosion plating and meeting Euro 7 brake-particulate limits, while extending service to 150k km. Cladding adds ~£26/disc but avoids the ~£238 ceramic premium, netting ~£34/corner on equipped trims.
ideaData.materialGrade: GJL-200 grey iron + WC/Fe-Ni laser-clad friction layer
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds a laser-cladding (LMD) cell plus post-clad grinding to the iron-disc line; deletes the zinc-plating bath. Replaces the entire carbon-ceramic process route on equipped trims. Cladding cycle time and laser capex are the constraints; iron casting and machining otherwise unchanged.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Coated iron meets dust limits and durability, avoiding the costly carbon-ceramic premium.

ideaData.riskNotes: Clad-layer adhesion and thermal-fatigue cracking under repeated high-energy stops need extensive dyno and field validation; coating uniformity and grinding tolerance affect judder/DTV. Euro 7 particulate compliance must be measurement-verified. Laser-cladding throughput and capex are scale risks. New process, higher uncertainty.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes/Porsche surface-coated brake disc, 2023; OEM-confirmed program

evidenceSources.title: Coated brake disc for particulate reduction

ideaData.regulatoryContext: Euro 7 brake particulate emission limits; UN/ECE R90 brake replacement parts

title: Air suspension to FSD frequency-selective passive damper system
description: Replace four-corner air spring plus ECAS compressor with steel coils and frequency-selective passive dampers on mid trims. Deletes compressor, reservoir, four air struts, ride-height sensors and valve block, removing 14 components and 9kg/vehicle. System cost falls about £102/vehicle while retaining 80% of comfort metric. Benchmarks Li Auto trim-walk to passive coils on entry variants.

ideaData.title: Air suspension to FSD frequency-selective passive damper system
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline mid-trim chassis uses four-corner air springs with an ECAS air-supply unit (compressor, dryer, reservoir, valve block) and ride-height sensors. The change substitutes chrome-silicon steel coil springs with frequency-selective damping (FSD) twin-tube dampers, whose internal bypass valve self-tunes damping by input frequency (soft for low-frequency body motion, firm for high-frequency wheel events) without electronics. This deletes the compressor, reservoir, four air struts, height sensors and valve block, removing 14 components and 9kg/vehicle, while passive FSD retains ~80% of the air-system comfort metric. System cost falls ~£102/vehicle; the trade is loss of ride-height adjustability, acceptable on mid trims.
ideaData.materialGrade: Chrome-silicon (SiCr) steel coil + FSD twin-tube damper
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the air-supply module assembly, four air-strut builds, sensor/valve fitting and leak-test station; replaces with coil-and-damper corner builds reusing standard strut tooling. Removes pneumatic-line routing and 14 components. Simplifies end-of-line calibration (no air system bleed/charge).

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Passive frequency-selective dampers replace the entire air system, deleting parts and a leak-test.

ideaData.riskNotes: Comfort and customer perception versus air suspension must be tuning-validated; loss of ride-height/leveling affects laden behaviour and headlamp aim, needing reassessment. FSD valve consistency and durability require supplier validation. Trim-walk positioning risk if customers expect air on the badge. Mature components, integration risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto trim-walk to passive coils on entry variants, 2023; OEM-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: Headlamp leveling per UN/ECE R48 when ride-height control removed

title: Disconnect coupling deleting rear e-axle drag at cruise
description: Add a dog-clutch disconnect to the rear secondary e-axle that decouples reduction gears and half-shafts during single-motor cruise, cutting spin losses by 0.9kW. The 35Wh/100km saving lets battery cells shrink 1.2kWh at equal range, a system-level material saving of about £60/vehicle that dwarfs the £15 actuator. Benchmarks AVATR dual-motor disconnect e-axle.

ideaData.title: Disconnect coupling deleting rear e-axle drag at cruise
ideaData.technicalDescription: In a dual-motor EV the secondary rear e-axle is back-driven at cruise, spinning its reduction gears, bearings and motor rotor and incurring ~0.9kW of churning, bearing and iron-loss drag. The change adds a face-toothed dog-clutch disconnect (electromechanical or e-vacuum actuated) on the rear half-shaft/reduction interface that decouples the gear train and rotor when only the primary axle drives. Synchronised re-engagement is managed by the inverter matching rotor speed. Eliminating drag saves ~35Wh/100km, which lets the pack shrink ~1.2kWh in case-hardened-steel gears terms — a ~£60/vehicle cell-material saving versus the ~£15 dog-clutch actuator. Gears are 20MnCr5 case-hardened; the clutch hub is splined to the half-shaft.
ideaData.materialGrade: 20MnCr5 case-hardened gears; dog-clutch hub steel
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds a dog-clutch module and actuator to the rear e-axle housing plus its harness and inverter speed-sync control. Minor machining for the clutch hub and shift collar. No removal of existing parts; net component add at axle, but enables battery-cell reduction upstream. Requires sealed actuator integration.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Decoupling axle drag cuts energy use, enabling a smaller, cheaper battery pack at equal range.

ideaData.riskNotes: Dog-clutch re-engagement must be fast and shock-free for AWD demand events; mis-synchronised engagement risks tooth clash and NVH. Actuator durability over cycle life and cold-temperature engagement need validation. Functional-safety case for traction availability required. Proven in production; controls calibration is the main risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AVATR dual-motor disconnect e-axle, 2023; OEM-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: Functional safety (ISO 26262) for drive-torque availability

title: GJS-500 ductile-iron caliper bracket replacing forged steel
description: Switch the floating-caliper anchor bracket from forged C45 steel to GJS-500-7 ductile iron casting. Casting near-net shape removes three milling ops and the forging die, while 500MPa tensile meets 28kN clamp reaction. Saves about £2.7/corner and improves dimensional repeatability. Slight 180g mass gain accepted at the unsprung rear. Benchmarks Denza rear caliper bracket sourcing.

ideaData.title: GJS-500 ductile-iron caliper bracket replacing forged steel
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline floating-caliper anchor bracket is forged from C45 medium-carbon steel and then milled on three faces for the guide-pin bores and pad-abutment surfaces. The change casts it near-net in GJS-500-7 ductile (nodular) iron, whose ~500 MPa tensile and ~320 MPa yield comfortably react the ~28kN caliper clamp/drag load with a small section increase. Near-net casting forms the guide-pin bosses and abutment ledges directly, deleting the forging die and three milling operations (only the bores are reamed). Nodular graphite gives good damping and machinability. A modest 180g mass gain is accepted at the unsprung rear corner; dimensional repeatability improves versus forging flash variation.
ideaData.materialGrade: GJS-500-7 ductile iron (vs forged C45 steel)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Sand/shell casting replaces forging plus three milling ops, deleting the forging die. Only guide-pin bores are reamed. Near-net shape lifts material utilisation and dimensional consistency. Adds foundry sourcing; casting cycle and post-machining minimal. Low industrialisation effort.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Cheaper near-net iron casting removes forging and machining at adequate strength.

ideaData.riskNotes: Casting porosity or nodularity loss at the highly loaded guide-pin boss could reduce fatigue life, requiring foundry process control and X-ray sampling. Slight unsprung-mass increase must be NVH-checked. Mature material and process; low overall risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Denza rear caliper bracket ductile-iron sourcing, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R90 brake component; OEM brake durability standards

title: Hydroformed front subframe rails replacing welded box sections
description: Hydroform the front subframe side rails as closed tubes, replacing two-shell stamped-and-welded box sections. Eliminates 22 spot welds and one sealing operation per rail, improving torsional stiffness 18% at equal mass. Process consolidation cuts about £12/vehicle and removes a weld fixture. Benchmarks Audi space-frame hydroformed node experience applied to subframe.

ideaData.title: Hydroformed front subframe rails replacing welded box sections
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline front subframe side rails are two-shell stamped halves (CR340LA/HSLA) spot-welded into a box section, with a sealant bead to close the cavity; each rail needs ~22 spot welds and a sealing op. The change hydroforms a single seamless tube into the rail profile: a pre-bent tube is placed in a die and internal pressure (typically 100–300 MPa) expands it to the cavity shape, forming a continuous closed section. The seamless box has no weld flanges, improving torsional stiffness ~18% at equal mass and removing 22 welds plus the sealing operation per rail. Work hardening during forming raises local strength. Subsequent piercing forms bracket holes; end nodes weld to the cross-member.
ideaData.materialGrade: CR340LA/HSLA hydroformed tube (vs stamped two-shell)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Tube pre-bend and high-pressure hydroform press replace two stamping dies and ~22 spot welds per rail plus a sealing op; removes a weld fixture. Seamless section needs no flange land, saving material. Adds hydroform press capex and high-pressure tooling; cycle time and die cost are constraints.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Seamless hydroformed rail deletes welds and a seal op while stiffening the structure.

ideaData.riskNotes: Hydroforming thinning at tight-radius corners can fall below minimum gauge, risking buckling; pre-bend and pressure curve must be tuned and wall thickness mapped. High-pressure tooling capex and a specialised supplier add risk. Crash-folding behaviour of the closed section must be re-validated. Established at Audi but capital-intensive.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi space-frame hydroformed node experience, 2019; applied to subframe rails
ideaData.regulatoryContext: Front crash load paths per Euro NCAP and OEM standards

title: Bonded micro-cellular PU jounce bumper replacing molded rubber top mount
description: Replace the molded-rubber damper top mount with a tunable micro-cellular polyurethane (Cellasto-type) bonded unit. Single-component design deletes the steel cup, snubber and washer stack, cutting four parts to one and saving 0.3kg/corner. Piece cost falls about £1.8/corner with better high-frequency isolation. Benchmarks BMW micro-cellular top-mount strategy.

ideaData.title: Bonded micro-cellular PU jounce bumper replacing molded rubber top mount
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline strut top mount is a molded-rubber bushing in a stamped-steel cup with a separate rubber snubber (jounce bumper) and a washer/retainer stack — four parts pressed and bonded together. The change is a single tunable micro-cellular polyurethane (Cellasto-type MCU) unit bonded directly to the damper rod interface. MCU is a closed-cell PU elastomer whose volumetric compressibility lets one moulded geometry provide both the static spring rate and progressive jounce snubbing, with superior high-frequency vibration isolation and a near-constant rate over temperature. Consolidating to one component deletes the steel cup, separate snubber and washer stack, cutting four parts to one and 0.3kg/corner, while piece cost drops ~£1.8/corner.
ideaData.materialGrade: Micro-cellular polyurethane (MCU, Cellasto-type)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Single MCU moulding replaces a four-part press/bond sub-assembly, deleting the steel cup stamping and snubber moulding and one bonding station. Net-shape PU moulding gives the tuned rate directly. Reduces inventory SKUs. Low industrialisation; moulding cycle is the constraint.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One micro-cellular PU part consolidates a four-piece mount and improves isolation.

ideaData.riskNotes: MCU long-term set/creep and UV/ozone ageing must be validated for ride-height retention and rate stability; bond integrity to the metal interface needs adhesion testing. Tuning the single part for both static rate and jounce is a calibration task. Mature material; low risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW micro-cellular PU top-mount strategy, 2021; teardown-confirmed

title: Plunging tripod inboard CV joint replacing Rzeppa double-offset
description: Adopt a GI-type tripod plunging joint inboard instead of a double-offset Rzeppa ball joint. Three rollers and a tulip replace six balls, a cage and a wire ring, cutting part count and grinding operations. Lower friction trims 12W drag and saves about £4.7/side. Suited to 2800Nm e-axle torque with ±26mm plunge. Benchmarks Nio inboard tripod half-shaft.

ideaData.title: Plunging tripod inboard CV joint replacing Rzeppa double-offset
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline inboard plunging joint is a double-offset (DO) Rzeppa-type unit: six precision balls running in ground inner/outer races, held by a windowed cage and a wire ring, with axial plunge via sliding races. The change uses a GI-type tripod joint: a three-legged spider (trunnion) in 20MnCr5 carrying three needle-supported rollers that slide in a tulip housing's three tracks. Three rollers and a tulip replace six balls, a cage and a wire ring, cutting part count and several track-grinding operations. The rolling tripod has lower plunge friction (trimming ~12W drag) and accommodates ±26mm axial travel at the 2800Nm e-axle torque. Tracks are induction- or case-hardened; rollers are sealed in a boot with high-temp grease.
ideaData.materialGrade: 20MnCr5 case-hardened tripod/tulip CV joint
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Tripod-and-tulip joint deletes balls, cage and wire-ring components and reduces precision-grinding operations versus the Rzeppa race set. Fewer parts simplify assembly and grease-fill. Lower friction reduces drag. Hardening line retained; net machining content drops. Moderate retooling of the half-shaft inboard end.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Three-roller tripod replaces a six-ball cage joint, cutting parts, grinding and drag.

ideaData.riskNotes: Tripod joints can generate a 3rd-order axial shudder (GAF) under high torque at angle, needing low-friction roller design and tuning to meet NVH; boot durability at ±26mm plunge and high temperature must be validated. Established driveline tech; NVH calibration is the main risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio inboard tripod half-shaft, 2022; teardown-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Tripod vs Rzeppa CV joint comparison

title: Steel-backed aluminium hybrid brake disc replacing full cast iron
description: Use an aluminium hat riveted to a thin grey-iron friction ring (composite floating disc) instead of monobloc cast iron. Removes 2.4kg/corner unsprung mass and reduces coning. Aluminium hat is machined not cast-iron, cutting one foundry pour. Net saving about £5.1/corner after rivet assembly, with reduced thermal judder warranty. Benchmarks Porsche composite floating disc.

ideaData.title: Steel-backed aluminium hybrid brake disc replacing full cast iron
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline disc is a monobloc grey-iron (GJL-250) casting where the hat and friction ring are one heavy iron part. The change is a two-piece composite floating disc: a thin GJL friction ring connected to an aluminium (6061-T6) top hat via stainless floating rivets/pins that allow radial thermal growth. The aluminium hat replaces iron in the non-friction zone, removing ~2.4kg/corner unsprung mass; the floating connection lets the ring expand without coning/distortion, reducing disc-thickness-variation judder and its warranty. The hat is machined from aluminium (one fewer iron pour), and only the thin ring is cast. Net saving ~£5.1/corner after the rivet/pin assembly step.
ideaData.materialGrade: GJL-250 iron ring + 6061-T6 Al hat, floating-riveted
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Splits the disc into a cast iron ring plus a machined aluminium hat joined by a floating rivet/pin station; reduces iron poured per disc and adds an assembly op. Removes the heavy monobloc casting tooling for the hat. Floating-pin install and balancing are new steps; lower mass eases handling.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Aluminium hat cuts unsprung mass and judder warranty while reducing iron poured.

ideaData.riskNotes: Floating-pin clearance must balance ring float against rattle NVH; pin wear and corrosion at the Al-iron interface need galvanic isolation and durability validation. Two-piece balancing and thermal-cycle robustness add validation. Mature in performance brakes; cost at volume and NVH are key risks.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche composite floating disc, 2020; production-verified
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R90 brake discs; Euro 7 particulate considerations

title: Integrated e-corner module consolidating hub motor, brake and steering
description: Package an in-wheel hub motor, by-wire brake and steer-by-wire actuator into one e-corner module, deleting half-shafts, CV joints, steering rack and column tubes vehicle-wide. Removes 11 driveline parts and the entire steering linkage, saving 14kg/vehicle and a full assembly station. System saving about £153/vehicle at scale. Benchmarks Yangwang U9 distributed e-corner architecture.

ideaData.title: Integrated e-corner module consolidating hub motor, brake and steering
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline corner relies on a central e-axle feeding half-shafts and CV joints to the wheel, plus a steering rack, column and intermediate shaft. The change is an e-corner module integrating an in-wheel PMSM hub motor (NdFeB N42SH magnets for ~150°C tolerance), a brake-by-wire caliper and an independent steer-by-wire actuator into one wheel-carrier unit. Distributing propulsion to the wheels deletes half-shafts, inboard/outboard CV joints, the central reduction housing and the entire steering linkage (rack, column, U-joints). This removes ~11 driveline parts and the steering linkage, saving ~14kg/vehicle and a full chassis assembly station, with each corner becoming a self-contained bolt-on unit (~£153/vehicle system saving at scale).
ideaData.materialGrade: NdFeB N42SH (150°C) hub-motor magnets; Al carrier
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces axle, half-shaft, CV and steering-linkage assembly stations with a single e-corner module fitment; modules built off-line and bolted on. Deletes ~11 parts and the steering line. Adds high-voltage and by-wire harness integration. Major architecture change with new validation tooling.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Distributed e-corner deletes driveshafts and steering linkage vehicle-wide.

ideaData.riskNotes: In-wheel motors add large unsprung mass, hurting ride/road-holding and NVH, demanding active damping and careful tuning; sealing the motor against water/dust/heat at the wheel is hard. By-wire brake and steer need ASIL-D redundancy and homologation. High novelty, significant durability and safety-case risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Yangwang U9 distributed e-corner/active architecture, 2024; OEM-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79 steer-by-wire, R13-H braking, ISO 26262 ASIL-D redundancy

title: Tailor-rolled blank shock tower replacing multi-thickness welded stack
description: Form the front strut tower from a tailor-rolled blank with continuous 1.2–2.4mm thickness, replacing a three-piece reinforced weld stack. Deletes two reinforcement patches and 16 spot welds per side, saving 1.6kg/vehicle. Single-stamping eliminates a sub-assembly fixture, cutting about £6.8/vehicle. Benchmarks Mercedes tailor-rolled body-in-white reinforcements.

ideaData.title: Tailor-rolled blank shock tower replacing multi-thickness welded stack
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline strut/shock tower is a three-piece stack: a base panel plus two local reinforcement patches spot-welded on to add thickness where the damper loads peak, needing ~16 welds per side and a sub-assembly fixture. The change forms the tower from a tailor-rolled blank (TRB) in HSLA steel whose gauge varies continuously 1.2–2.4mm along the coil via flexible cold rolling, putting metal only where loads demand. One stamping from the TRB replaces the patched stack, deleting two reinforcements and 16 welds per side and a fixture, saving ~1.6kg/vehicle. The seamless thickness transition also removes weld-edge stress risers, improving fatigue at the damper mount. Net ~£6.8/vehicle.
ideaData.materialGrade: HSLA tailor-rolled blank, 1.2–2.4mm variable gauge
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Single TRB stamping replaces three stampings, two reinforcement patches, 16 spot welds and one sub-assembly fixture per side. Requires a tailor-rolled coil supplier and die designed for variable thickness. Reduces weld-cell load and inventory SKUs; forming springback across gauge transitions needs control.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Variable-gauge blank puts metal only where needed, deleting reinforcements and welds.

ideaData.riskNotes: Forming a part across a continuous thickness gradient causes uneven springback and strain, demanding die compensation and blank-positioning accuracy; TRB coil availability is supplier-limited. Fatigue at the damper mount must be validated. Proven at Mercedes BIW; supply and forming control are the risks.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes tailor-rolled BIW reinforcements, 2020; production-verified
ideaData.regulatoryContext: Front suspension load paths per OEM crash/durability standards

title: Plastic-composite rack housing replacing aluminium die-cast steering rack
description: Overmold a glass-reinforced PA66-GF50 housing around the steering rack tube instead of an AlSi9Cu3 die-casting. Mass falls 1.1kg and the casting plus its machining of mounting bosses is eliminated, molded-in instead. Corrosion sealing and NVH damping improve. Net saving about £7.6/vehicle on a belt-drive EPS rack. Benchmarks Audi composite steering housing studies.

ideaData.title: Plastic-composite rack housing replacing aluminium die-cast steering rack
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline EPS rack housing is an AlSi9Cu3 high-pressure die-casting that must be machined for the mounting bosses, ball-nut bore and tie-rod interfaces. The change injection-overmoulds a PA66-GF50 (50% glass-filled polyamide 66) housing around the steel rack tube and metal bearing inserts, with mounting bosses and brackets moulded in. The high glass loading gives the stiffness and creep resistance to react belt-drive EPS reaction loads, while the polymer's damping improves NVH and the moulded skin eliminates corrosion sealing. Deleting the casting and its boss machining, and replacing them with a single moulding with insert-moulded metal interfaces, cuts 1.1kg and ~£7.6/vehicle. Metal inserts at high-load bolt points preserve clamp load.
ideaData.materialGrade: PA66-GF50 overmoulded (vs AlSi9Cu3 die-cast)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Injection overmoulding with metal-insert loading replaces HPDC and CNC boss machining; bosses/brackets moulded net-shape. Deletes the casting machining line and corrosion sealing. Adds insert-moulding tooling and inspection of weld-line strength. Lower mass and integrated brackets cut assembly content.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Glass-filled polymer housing deletes the casting and its machining at lower mass.

ideaData.riskNotes: Polymer creep and thermal expansion at the bearing/EPS interface can shift preload and lash over life and temperature, requiring metal inserts and long-term validation; weld-line strength and moisture-driven dimensional change (PA66 hygroscopy) need control. Steering is safety-critical, so durability bar is high. Higher uncertainty.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi composite steering housing studies, 2021; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79 steering durability and integrity requirements

title: Single-stage helical reduction replacing two-stage e-axle gearset
description: Redesign the secondary e-axle to a single helical reduction stage with a higher-speed 18000rpm motor, deleting one gear pair, an intermediate shaft and two bearings. Removes four ground gear flanks and one shaft grinding op, cutting about £14/axle and 2.3kg. Efficiency holds at 96% in the cruise window. Benchmarks Zeekr single-stage rear drive unit.

ideaData.title: Single-stage helical reduction replacing two-stage e-axle gearset
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline secondary e-axle uses a two-stage helical reduction (~9:1) with an intermediate countershaft and four supporting bearings to step down a ~12000rpm motor. The change raises motor speed to ~18000rpm so a single helical stage (~7:1) achieves the wheel speed, deleting one gear pair, the intermediate shaft and two bearings. This removes four ground gear flanks and one shaft-grinding operation, cutting ~2.3kg and ~£14/axle. Gears are 20MnCr5 case-hardened and ground; the higher motor speed needs higher-grade bearings and rotor balancing but mesh efficiency holds at ~96% in the cruise window. Fewer meshes and bearings also reduce churning loss and oil volume.
ideaData.materialGrade: 20MnCr5 case-hardened single-stage helical gears
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes one gear pair, an intermediate shaft and two bearings, removing four gear-grinding flanks and one shaft-grind op. Simplifies the gearbox housing and reduces oil fill. Requires a higher-speed (18krpm) rotor with tighter balancing and uprated bearings. Net lower machining and assembly content per axle.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Higher motor speed enables one reduction stage, cutting gears, a shaft and bearings.

ideaData.riskNotes: 18000rpm operation raises rotor dynamics, bearing speed limits and gear-whine NVH risk, requiring high-precision balancing, bearing selection and gear micro-geometry tuning; lubrication at high speed must avoid churning/aeration. Single-stage higher torque per mesh stresses the gear. Established at Zeekr; high-speed NVH is the key risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr single-stage rear drive unit, 2023; teardown-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Zeekr rear drive unit gearbox teardown

title: Press-fit ball joint replacing bolted three-piece tapered joint
description: Integrate the lower ball joint as a press-fit cartridge into the control arm rather than a separate bolted, tapered unit with castle nut and cotter pin. Deletes the bolt, nut and pin, removing a torque-and-pin station. Saves about £2.4/corner and 0.2kg. Maintainability handled by service replacement of the full arm. Benchmarks BYD Seal press-fit ball joint.

ideaData.title: Press-fit ball joint replacing bolted three-piece tapered joint
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline lower ball joint is a discrete unit clamped to the control arm via a tapered stud, castle nut and cotter pin, needing a torque-and-pin assembly station and three fasteners. The change is a press-fit ball-joint cartridge swaged or pressed directly into a machined bore in the aluminium/steel control arm, retained by interference and a circlip. This deletes the bolt, nut and cotter pin, removing the torque-and-pin op and 0.2kg, and improves stiffness by eliminating the bolted-joint compliance. The ball stud (induction-hardened C45) runs in a low-friction PA/PTFE bearing seat with a sealed boot. Serviceability shifts to replacing the complete arm, accepted on modern platforms. Net ~£2.4/corner.
ideaData.materialGrade: Induction-hardened C45 ball stud, press-fit cartridge
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Press-fit/swage station integrates the ball joint into the arm bore, deleting the bolt-nut-pin install station and three fasteners. Adds a controlled-force press and retention check. Reduces SKUs and torque-monitoring. Net-shape arm bore needs reaming for interference fit.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Press-fit cartridge removes three fasteners and a torque-and-pin station.

ideaData.riskNotes: Press-fit retention force must withhold pull-out under worst-case suspension load; bore tolerance and press-force monitoring are critical to avoid loose joints. Loss of field-serviceability of the joint alone is a service-cost trade. Mature method; press-force control is the main risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD Seal press-fit lower ball joint, 2022; teardown-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79; suspension-joint pull-out per OEM load cases

title: Hollow-bored differential pinion shaft replacing solid forging
description: Gun-drill the differential pinion shaft to a hollow core, removing 0.6kg rotating mass at equal 18CrNiMo7 case-hardened torsional capacity. Lower inertia improves NVH and efficiency marginally. Bore replaces no machining but reduces forging input 14%, saving about £2.9/axle. Benchmarks Hongqi rear differential hollow pinion.

ideaData.title: Hollow-bored differential pinion shaft replacing solid forging
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline differential pinion (input) shaft is a solid forging in 18CrNiMo7-6 case-hardening steel, gear-cut and case-hardened. The change gun-drills a concentric axial bore through the shaft, exploiting that torsional shear stress is highest at the outer fibres and near zero at the centre, so removing core material costs little capacity. The hollow shaft sheds ~0.6kg rotating mass at equal torsional strength, lowering inertia for marginal NVH and efficiency gains. The bore also lets forging input drop ~14% by starting from a smaller pre-form, saving ~£2.9/axle. Case-hardening depth and the pinion teeth are unchanged; bore concentricity is held to preserve balance.
ideaData.materialGrade: 18CrNiMo7-6 case-hardened, gun-drilled hollow
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds a gun-drilling/deep-bore operation but reduces forging billet size ~14%. Lower rotating mass eases balancing. No change to gear-cutting or carburising line. Bore concentricity tolerance and deburring are the new controls; net material saving outweighs added bore cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Hollowing the low-stress core cuts rotating mass and forging input at equal torque.

ideaData.riskNotes: Gun-drill bore concentricity and surface finish affect balance and can seed fatigue if rough; case-hardening of a thinner wall must avoid through-hardening/embrittlement. Torsional and bending fatigue at the bore need validation. Mature technique; bore quality is the main risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Hongqi rear differential hollow pinion, 2022; benchmarked

title: Two-piece bolted propshaft replacing three-piece with center bearing
description: Shorten the AWD propshaft to a two-piece carbon-wrapped aluminium design, deleting the center support bearing, its bracket and one universal joint. Carbon overwrap raises the bending critical speed above 8500rpm, removing the intermediate support. Saves 2.1kg and about £11/vehicle. Benchmarks AITO M9 lightweight propshaft.

ideaData.title: Two-piece bolted propshaft replacing three-piece with center bearing
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline AWD propshaft is a three-piece steel design with a centre support bearing, its rubber-isolated bracket and an extra universal joint to keep each span below its bending critical speed. The change is a two-piece carbon-fibre-wrapped aluminium (6061) tube shaft: a filament-wound CFRP overwrap on a thin aluminium tube raises the specific bending stiffness so the critical (whirl) speed exceeds ~8500rpm for a longer span, eliminating the need for the intermediate support. This deletes the centre bearing, bracket and one U-joint, cutting 2.1kg and ~£11/vehicle, and improves NVH by removing a resonance path. Bonded/swaged end yokes connect to the existing CV/U-joints; balance is set after overwrap.
ideaData.materialGrade: CFRP-wrapped 6061 aluminium propshaft tube
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Filament-winding/overwrap cell plus end-yoke bonding replaces the steel-tube and centre-bearing sub-assembly; deletes the bearing, bracket and one U-joint. Removes a support-mount install at vehicle assembly. Adds composite balancing and NDT. Two-piece simplifies underbody packaging.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Stiff CFRP-Al shaft removes the centre bearing, bracket and a U-joint.

ideaData.riskNotes: CFRP-to-aluminium bond joint durability and torsional fatigue at the end yokes must be validated; impact/stone damage tolerance and balance retention over life need testing. Galvanic isolation at the Al-CFRP interface required. Established at AITO; bonded-joint validation is the key risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AITO M9 lightweight propshaft, 2023; teardown-confirmed

title: Cold-formed splined CV outer race replacing machined-from-bar
description: Net-shape cold-form the outer race ball tracks of the wheel-side fixed CV joint instead of turning and grinding from bar stock. Cold forming removes two grinding passes and improves surface hardness through work-hardening, lifting fatigue 15%. Material utilisation rises from 62% to 88%, saving about £3.6/side. Benchmarks Maextro front half-shaft joint sourcing.

ideaData.title: Cold-formed splined CV outer race replacing machined-from-bar
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline wheel-side fixed (Rzeppa) CV joint outer race has its six curved ball tracks turned and ground from bar stock in 55Cr3/SAE1055, wasting ~38% of material as chips and needing two grinding passes. The change cold-forms (cold-extrudes/irons) the ball tracks and the internal spline net-shape in a multi-station press: the tracks are formed to near-final geometry, with only a finish grind. Cold work aligns grain flow along the tracks and work-hardens the surface, lifting rolling-contact fatigue ~15%, and removes two grinding passes. Material utilisation rises from ~62% to ~88%, the dominant saving (~£3.6/side). The race is then induction- or case-hardened at the tracks and finish-ground to running tolerance.
ideaData.materialGrade: 55Cr3/SAE1055 cold-formed CV outer race
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Multi-station cold-forming press replaces bar turning and removes two grinding passes; material utilisation climbs from 62% to 88%, cutting scrap and chip handling. Adds forming-die tooling; retains a finish grind and hardening. Work-hardened surface improves fatigue. Net lower per-part cost and machining time.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Net-shape cold forming cuts grinding and lifts material yield from 62% to 88%.

ideaData.riskNotes: Cold-forming track geometry and spline accuracy must hold tolerance to avoid CV NVH and uneven ball loading; die wear shifts dimensions and needs monitoring. Residual stress from forming must be managed before hardening. Established cold-forming method; die life and track accuracy are the risks.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Maextro front half-shaft CV joint sourcing, 2023; benchmarked

evidenceSources.title: Cold-formed CV outer race process data

title: Hydraulic-mount-free elastomer e-axle mount with tuned voided bushing
description: Replace the hydraulically damped e-axle torque-rod mount with a tuned voided elastomer bushing, since EV powertrain lacks combustion shake. Deletes the fluid chamber, diaphragm and decoupler, simplifying to a single molded part. Saves about £5.1/mount across three mounts (£15/vehicle) with equal NVH on the EV duty cycle. Benchmarks Luxeed S7 EV powertrain mounts.

ideaData.title: Hydraulic-mount-free elastomer e-axle mount with tuned voided bushing
ideaData.technicalDescription: ICE-derived powertrain mounts use hydraulically damped (hydromount) designs with a fluid chamber, diaphragm and decoupler to suppress low-frequency engine shake and idle vibration. An EV e-axle has no combustion firing pulses or idle shake, so the change replaces the hydromount torque-rod and axle mounts with tuned voided natural-rubber/elastomer bushings, where moulded voids set independent radial/axial rates to control e-axle torque reaction and high-frequency gear whine isolation. This deletes the fluid chamber, diaphragm and decoupler, reducing each mount to a single moulded part, saving ~£5.1/mount across three mounts (~£15/vehicle) with equal NVH on the EV duty cycle. Vulcanised rubber-to-metal bonding retains the steel sleeves for bolt-up.
ideaData.materialGrade: Tuned voided natural-rubber elastomer bushing
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Single-part rubber-to-metal moulding replaces the multi-component hydromount (chamber, diaphragm, fluid fill, decoupler), deleting the fluid-fill and assembly steps. Simplifies supply and inventory. Voided geometry tunes rates in the mould. Lower part count and no fluid handling at the mount supplier.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: EV lacks combustion shake, so a simple voided bushing replaces the costly hydromount.

ideaData.riskNotes: Voided-bushing rate tuning must control e-axle torque-roll and gear-whine transfer without the hydromount's damping; high-torque launch shudder and durability of the rubber under heat need validation. Some EV duty cycles still benefit from damping, so NVH sign-off is essential. Mature material; tuning is the risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Luxeed S7 EV powertrain mounts, 2023; teardown-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Voided elastomer EV mount NVH data

title: Aluminium-MMC brake rotor replacing grey iron on light trims
description: Adopt an aluminium-SiC metal-matrix-composite rotor for low-mass EV trims with strong regen, where mechanical braking is light-duty. Cuts 3.0kg/corner unsprung mass and corrosion-driven warranty, deleting zinc coating. MMC premium offset by regen-reduced wear extending life to vehicle lifetime, net about £6.8/corner over the cycle. Benchmarks Nio ET5 lightweight rotor trials.

ideaData.title: Aluminium-MMC brake rotor replacing grey iron on light trims
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline rotor is grey iron (GJL-250) with a zinc anti-corrosion coating. On regen-heavy EV trims mechanical braking is light-duty, so the change uses an aluminium-silicon-carbide metal-matrix-composite (Al-SiC MMC, e.g. A359 + ~20% SiC particulate) rotor cast and then machined. The SiC reinforcement raises stiffness, hardness and thermal conductivity, giving wear and fade resistance adequate for low-energy braking at ~1/3 the density of iron, cutting 3.0kg/corner unsprung mass. Aluminium does not rust, deleting the zinc coating and corrosion-driven warranty. The MMC material premium is offset because regen-reduced wear extends pad/rotor life to vehicle lifetime, netting ~£6.8/corner over the cycle.
ideaData.materialGrade: Al-SiC MMC (A359 + ~20% SiC) rotor
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: MMC casting plus PCD/diamond machining (SiC is abrasive) replaces iron casting and zinc plating; deletes the corrosion-coat line. Requires diamond tooling and tighter machining control. Lighter rotor eases handling and unsprung mass. Casting of particulate MMC needs stir/squeeze process control.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Lightweight non-corroding MMC suits regen-dominant braking, deleting coating and warranty.

ideaData.riskNotes: MMC rotors have limited high-energy thermal capacity, so application must be restricted to regen-dominant trims and validated against emergency-stop and downhill fade; pad-material compatibility and SiC abrasive machining add development. Higher material/tooling cost and novelty. Restricted-use case is the key constraint.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio ET5 lightweight MMC rotor trials, 2023; OEM-confirmed

evidenceSources.title: Al-MMC brake rotor feasibility for EVs

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R90; Euro 7 brake particulate; emergency-braking performance per R13-H

title: Multi-link rear consolidated into single forged H-arm
description: Replace a five-link rear suspension with an AA6082 forged H-arm integrating two lateral and one trailing function. Reduces links from five to two per side, deleting six bushings and six fasteners per corner. Mass falls 3.2kg/corner and assembly time 60s/axle. System saving about £22/vehicle. Benchmarks BMW integral-link consolidation direction.

ideaData.title: Multi-link rear consolidated into single forged H-arm
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline rear suspension is a five-link design: separate camber, toe, trailing and lateral links, each with its own bushings and fasteners. The change consolidates three of these functions into a single AA6082-T6 forged H-arm (a one-piece A-/H-shaped lower control arm reacting two lateral and one trailing load path), cutting links from five to two per side. This deletes six bushings and six fasteners per corner and 3.2kg/corner, while the forged aluminium arm with tuned voided bushings preserves the kinematic/compliance (K&C) targets. Assembly time falls ~60s/axle. The forging carries the combined loads with flow-line strength; bushing eyes are machined and pressed/vulcanised. System saving ~£22/vehicle.
ideaData.materialGrade: AA6082-T6 forged H-arm (multi-link consolidation)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One forged H-arm replaces three links plus six bushings and six fasteners per corner; deletes their forging/stamping, press and install operations and ~60s/axle assembly. Adds a larger forging die and CNC of multiple bushing eyes. Fewer SKUs and joints simplify the axle build.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Multifunction forged arm consolidates three links, six bushings and six fasteners.

ideaData.riskNotes: Consolidating links removes independent toe/camber adjustment and compliance tuning paths, so achieving K&C targets and ride/handling with one arm is challenging; forged-arm fatigue at multi-axis load junctions needs validation. Loss of tunability constrains platform variants. High integration risk.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW integral-link rear consolidation direction, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79; rear-suspension durability per OEM K&C standards

title: Roll-formed spring seat replacing machined aluminium seat
description: Roll-form the lower coil-spring seat from coated steel sheet instead of machining it from an aluminium extrusion. Forming replaces turning and reduces material 40%, while a captive elastomer isolator is clipped not bonded. Saves about £2/corner with equal corrosion life via cathodic e-coat. Benchmarks Volvo roll-formed spring seat.

ideaData.title: Roll-formed spring seat replacing machined aluminium seat
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline lower coil-spring seat is machined from an aluminium extrusion to form the spring-locating pocket and isolator groove, generating chips and using extra material. The change stamps/roll-forms the seat from coated HSLA steel sheet, forming the spring pocket and drain features net-shape, then clips on a captive EPDM elastomer isolator rather than bonding it. Sheet forming replaces turning, reduces material input ~40%, and the clipped isolator removes a bonding op. Cathodic e-coat (CDP) gives equal corrosion life to the aluminium part. The formed-steel seat carries the coil seating load adequately, saving ~£2/corner.
ideaData.materialGrade: Coated HSLA steel sheet, roll-formed (vs Al extrusion)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Stamping/roll-forming and a clip-on isolator replace extrusion machining and bonding; material input drops ~40% and turning/chip handling is eliminated. Adds CDP e-coat for corrosion. Simple forming die; low industrialisation. Clipped isolator removes adhesive cure time.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Sheet forming replaces machining and cuts material with a clip-on isolator.

ideaData.riskNotes: Formed-steel seat must resist coil-induced fretting and corrosion at the spring contact, so e-coat coverage and isolator retention need validation; clipped isolator pull-off and noise must be checked. Low overall risk; corrosion durability is the main item.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Volvo roll-formed spring seat, 2021; teardown-confirmed

title: Steer-by-wire deleting steering column and intermediate shaft
description: Implement full steer-by-wire, deleting the steering column, two universal joints, the intermediate shaft and the firewall seal grommet. Replaces mechanical path with a feedback actuator and road-wheel actuator on the rack. Removes a crash-collapse column assembly, saving 3.5kg and about £34/vehicle net after redundant ECU. Benchmarks Maextro steer-by-wire deployment.

ideaData.title: Steer-by-wire deleting steering column and intermediate shaft
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline steering has a mechanical path: steering wheel, collapsible column, two universal joints, an intermediate shaft and a firewall seal grommet feeding the EPS rack. Full steer-by-wire (SbW) removes this entire mechanical link, replacing it with a hand-wheel feedback actuator (force-feedback motor) and a road-wheel actuator integrated on the rack, connected by redundant wiring and a fail-operational ASIL-D controller. Deleting the column, two U-joints, intermediate shaft and grommet removes the crash-collapse column assembly (freeing crash-packaging and enabling flat floors/variable ratio), saving 3.5kg and ~£34/vehicle net after the redundant ECU and dual-wind motor. Power and signal redundancy ensure steering availability on single-fault.
ideaData.materialGrade: Dual-redundant SbW actuators (no mechanical column)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the column, two U-joints, intermediate shaft, grommet and the column-to-rack assembly station; replaces with feedback-actuator and road-wheel-actuator mounting plus redundant harness routing. Simplifies LHD/RHD and cockpit assembly. Adds redundant-power wiring and EOL calibration of force feedback.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Removing the mechanical steering column deletes multiple parts and a crash-collapse assembly.

ideaData.riskNotes: Loss of mechanical backup demands fail-operational ASIL-D redundancy in power, sensing and actuation; homologation under R79 SbW provisions and customer trust are hurdles. Force-feedback tuning for natural feel and latency is hard. High novelty and safety-case cost; redundancy must be proven.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Maextro steer-by-wire deployment, 2024; OEM-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN/ECE R79 steer-by-wire; ISO 26262 ASIL-D fail-operational redundancy

title: Bushing-integrated control arm with molded-in voided bushings
description: Vulcanise the rubber bushings directly into the aluminium control-arm eyes rather than press-fitting separate steel-sleeve bushings. Deletes the outer sleeve and the press operation, and removes interference-fit scrap. Saves about £3.1/corner and one press station. Tuned voids give axial-radial rate split. Benchmarks Mercedes integrated-bushing arm.

ideaData.title: Bushing-integrated control arm with molded-in voided bushings
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline control arm receives separate bushings, each a rubber element vulcanised inside an outer steel sleeve, then press-fit into the arm eye with interference — needing a press station and risking press scrap. The change vulcanises the rubber directly into the machined aluminium control-arm eye (the eye itself becomes the outer race), deleting the outer steel sleeve and the press-fit operation. Tuned moulded voids set the axial-vs-radial rate split for ride/handling compliance. Bonding rubber to the prepared/primed aluminium bore in the vulcanising mould removes the interference-fit scrap and one station, saving ~£3.1/corner, and improves rate consistency by removing sleeve press-in variation.
ideaData.materialGrade: Vulcanised rubber bonded into AA6082 arm eye
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Direct vulcanisation into the arm eye deletes the outer steel sleeve and the bushing press-fit station, removing interference-fit scrap. Requires arm-eye surface prep/priming and an in-line vulcanising mould. Adds bond-adhesion control; net lower part count and assembly content.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Moulding rubber into the arm eye deletes the sleeve and the press-fit station.

ideaData.riskNotes: Rubber-to-aluminium bond integrity over thermal/load cycling is critical; bond failure causes lash and NVH, so surface prep and adhesion validation are essential. Service replacement now requires the full arm. Vulcanising tolerances affect rate scatter. Moderate risk centred on bond durability.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes integrated-bushing control arm, 2022; teardown-confirmed

title: Single-piece die-cast front shock tower plus longitudinal node
description: Die-cast the front shock tower integrated with the longitudinal rail front node in AlSi10MnMg, replacing 9 stamped parts and 46 spot welds. Consolidation removes a weld sub-cell and saves 5.4kg/vehicle. Casting tooling amortises over 200k units, yielding about £16/vehicle. Improves NVH path stiffness 22%. Benchmarks Xpeng front megacast node.

ideaData.title: Single-piece die-cast front shock tower plus longitudinal node
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline front shock tower and its junction to the longitudinal rail is a 9-piece stamped-steel sub-assembly joined by ~46 spot welds in a dedicated weld cell. The change die-casts the shock tower integrated with the longitudinal front node as one AlSi10MnMg (heat-treat-free, ~120 MPa yield) HPDC part on a large-tonnage machine, with cast-in damper-mount bosses and rail interfaces. Consolidating 9 parts and 46 welds removes the weld sub-cell and 5.4kg/vehicle, while the seamless casting stiffens the damper-load NVH path ~22% (no spot-weld compliance). Casting tooling amortises over ~200k units, yielding ~£16/vehicle; vacuum HPDC and X-ray NDT ensure integrity at the loaded boss.
ideaData.materialGrade: AlSi10MnMg HPDC (heat-treat-free, ~120 MPa yield)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Large-tonnage vacuum HPDC and X-ray NDT replace 9 stamping dies and a 46-weld sub-cell; cast-in bosses delete machining of mounts (only finish-machine interfaces). Removes weld inspection and a fixture. High die capex over 200k units; casting cycle time is the constraint.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One casting replaces 9 stampings and 46 welds, deleting a weld cell and stiffening the path.

ideaData.riskNotes: HPDC porosity at the highly loaded damper boss risks fatigue, needing vacuum casting and per-part X-ray; crash energy management of cast aluminium versus steel must be validated and repairability is poorer. Die capex and single-supplier risk. Mixed steel-aluminium body joining adds galvanic/joining complexity.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng front megacast node, 2023; teardown-confirmed
ideaData.regulatoryContext: Front crash load paths per Euro NCAP / C-NCAP and OEM standards

title: Plastic composite coil spring replacing steel coil
description: Adopt a glass-fibre-reinforced polymer coil spring replacing the chrome-silicon steel coil. Cuts 1.9kg/corner unsprung mass and eliminates shot-peening, e-coat and chip protection entirely, removing three finishing ops. Corrosion-proof for life. Material premium offset by deleted coatings, net about £3.4/corner. Benchmarks Audi GFRP coil spring program.

ideaData.title: Plastic composite coil spring replacing steel coil
ideaData.technicalDescription: The baseline coil spring is chrome-silicon (SiCr) steel wire, hot/cold-coiled, quenched-and-tempered, shot-peened, e-coated and chip-protected. The change winds a glass-fibre-reinforced polymer (GFRP, E-glass/epoxy filament-wound) coil whose helical fibre architecture stores spring energy at ~1/3 the density of steel, cutting 1.9kg/corner unsprung mass. Because the composite is inherently corrosion-proof, shot-peening, e-coat and chip protection are entirely deleted, removing three finishing operations and their lines. The GFRP material premium is offset by the deleted coatings, netting ~£3.4/corner, with corrosion-free life eliminating chip/rust warranty. End coils are over-moulded with seat features; rate is set by fibre angle and wire diameter.
ideaData.materialGrade: E-glass/epoxy filament-wound GFRP coil spring
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Filament-winding and cure replace steel coiling, quench-temper, shot-peen, e-coat and chip-protect lines, deleting three finishing ops. Adds composite cure takt and NDT.
