Throughput per chamber lower than continuous plating, so capacity planning needed. Colour-match of satin/gun-metal across batches controlled by in-situ optical monitoring.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi Q6 e-tron chrome-delete satin trim, 2024; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: REACH Annex XIV (Cr(VI) authorisation) and ELV Directive 2000/53/EC; PVD removes hexavalent-chrome compliance burden

title: Single integrated front-end carrier module in-line assembly
description: Consolidate cooling pack, crash beam, headlamp brackets and AGS into one glass-reinforced PP-LGF40 front-end carrier delivered pre-assembled. Reduces 22 fasteners and 7 sub-brackets to a single 6-bolt fit, cutting line time 40s/vehicle and ~£12/vehicle. Hybrid metal inserts carry crash loads. Supplier sub-assembly shifts labour off main line. Benchmarks BMW's front-end module integration on Neue Klasse.

ideaData.title: Single integrated front-end carrier module in-line assembly
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline front end is built up on-line from a stamped-steel cooling-pack carrier, separate crash beam, headlamp brackets and active grille shutter, joined with ~22 fasteners and 7 sub-brackets. The change consolidates these into one glass-reinforced PP-LGF40 (40% long-glass-fibre PP, ~7 GPa) front-end carrier with over-moulded steel inserts that carry crash and tow loads, delivered pre-assembled by the supplier. Cooling pack, AGS, horns and lamp datums mount to the carrier off-line; the module fits to BIW via a 6-bolt interface, cutting main-line time ~40 s/veh. Hybrid metal-plastic construction matches stiffness while moving labour off the critical line.
ideaData.materialGrade: PP-LGF40 (40% long-glass PP) with hybrid steel inserts
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Reduces 22 fasteners + 7 sub-brackets to a 6-bolt module fit; ~40 s/veh main-line time removed by shifting sub-assembly to supplier. Front-end carrier injection-moulded with over-moulded steel inserts. Fewer line-side bins and torque stations; single module logistics (one part number) vs many.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Module integration cuts fastener count, line time and line-side complexity.

ideaData.riskNotes: Crash-load path through over-moulded inserts must be validated (insert pull-out, weld-line at insert, low-temp impact). Dimensional stack-up of lamp/cooling datums on a single plastic carrier needs CTQ control. Supplier capacity and JIT sequencing risk; dual-source the module. Creep at engine-bay temperature checked.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Neue Klasse front-end module, 2025; benchmarked program
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R42 / R17; UN GTR9 pedestrian lower-leg datums carried on module

title: Active grille shutter electric actuator pin-count reduction
description: Replace 4-wire AGS actuator with a 2-wire LIN smart actuator carrying integrated position feedback, deleting two harness leads, one connector cavity and the discrete Hall sensor. Saves ~£1.6/vehicle in copper, terminals and assembly plus diagnostics over LIN. One PWM driver channel freed in the zonal controller. Benchmarks Porsche's LIN-actuated cooling-air-flap module on Macan EV.

ideaData.title: Active grille shutter electric actuator pin-count reduction
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline active grille shutter uses a 4-wire actuator (two power, two discrete Hall-sensor feedback) wired point-to-point to the zonal controller with a discrete position sensor. The change adopts a 2-wire LIN smart actuator with integrated position feedback and an embedded driver, deleting two harness leads, one connector cavity and the discrete Hall sensor. LIN bus carries command and diagnostics, freeing one PWM driver channel in the zonal controller. Saves copper, terminals and assembly time per vehicle. Smart-actuator local closed-loop control also improves flap position accuracy and enables fault reporting over the bus.
ideaData.materialGrade: LIN smart actuator (2-wire), integrated position sensor
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes two leads, one connector cavity and a discrete sensor per AGS; harness simplified and one PWM channel freed in the zonal controller. Single LIN drop replaces point-to-point feedback wiring, easing routing and assembly clip count. Diagnostics over LIN reduce end-of-line test steps.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Smart LIN actuator removes discrete sensor and wires, cutting copper and assembly.

ideaData.riskNotes: LIN actuator must meet under-hood temperature, vibration and EMC; validate flap stall detection and ice-break torque. Single-source smart-actuator supply risk, so qualify a second source. Bus loading and node count on the shared LIN segment verified against the timing budget.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Macan EV LIN cooling-air-flap module, 2024; benchmarked

title: Two-shot moulded badge replacing chromed emblem assembly
description: Replace adhesive-mounted chromed three-part emblem with a single 2K injection-moulded badge (clear PMMA over metallised PC) with integral snap clips, deleting plating, foam tape and a backing plate. Saves ~£1.8/badge and one assembly operation; snap-fit removes alignment fixture. Laser-clear illumination compatible. Benchmarks Mercedes' illuminated moulded star emblem approach.

ideaData.title: Two-shot moulded badge replacing chromed emblem assembly
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline emblem is a three-part chromed assembly (electroplated PC ring, metallised insert, backing plate) bonded with foam tape and aligned in a fixture. The change moulds a single 2K (two-shot) badge: a metallised PC substrate over-moulded with a clear PMMA lens, with integral snap clips. This deletes the plating line, foam tape and backing plate; snap-fit removes the alignment fixture. The clear-over-metallised construction is laser-clear-illumination compatible for lit emblems. 2K moulding gives a sealed, optically deep look in one operation. Saves per-badge cost and one assembly step while improving weatherability versus plated parts.
ideaData.materialGrade: 2K: metallised PC + clear PMMA lens
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces plating + tape + backing-plate + fixture with one 2K injection-mould operation with integral snaps. Deletes the alignment fixture and adhesive station; emblem fits by snap-in. Vacuum metallisation on the PC substrate replaces electroplating. One part number vs three.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Two-shot moulding consolidates a multi-part plated badge into one snap-fit part.

ideaData.riskNotes: 2K bond between PC and PMMA must resist thermal-cycle delamination; validate with thermal shock and humidity. Snap-clip retention/pull-off and gap-flush on a curved Class-A surface need DFM. Metallised-layer adhesion and laser-etch clarity for lit variants verified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes illuminated star emblem moulded approach, 2021; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R26 external projections (rounded/recessed emblem); lit emblems per R48 light-emitting limits

title: Lower body cladding TPO downgauge with co-injection
description: Downgauge rocker and wheel-arch cladding from 2.8mm to 2.2mm using a higher-flow TPO (MFR 18) with recycled-core co-injection, cutting ~0.6kg and £2/vehicle resin while holding stone-impact and thermal-sag specs. Recycled core uses 30% PCR. Clip towers reinforced to retain pull-off force. Benchmarks Zeekr's lightweight cladding material strategy on 001.

ideaData.title: Lower body cladding TPO downgauge with co-injection
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rocker and wheel-arch cladding is 2.8 mm solid TPO (thermoplastic olefin). The change downgauges to 2.2 mm using a higher-flow TPO (MFR 18 g/10min) with a recycled core via co-injection (30% PCR sandwiched between virgin skins). Thinner wall plus easier flow fills the longer rocker without short-shots, cutting ~0.6 kg and £2/veh resin. Stone-impact (chip) and thermal-sag specs are held by the stiffer skin and rib tuning; clip towers are locally reinforced to retain pull-off force. Co-injection keeps PCR off Class-A surfaces. Same tool family with adjusted gating; the change is largely material and wall-stock.
ideaData.materialGrade: TPO MFR18 (high-flow), 30% PCR co-injected core
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Downgauge 2.8->2.2 mm reduces shot weight and cycle slightly; co-injection adds a second barrel/PCR feed. Clip towers locally thickened. Higher MFR improves fill of long thin cladding, lowering short-shot scrap. Same tool family, revised gating; PCR confined to core.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Downgauging with higher-flow TPO plus recycled core cuts resin mass and cost.

ideaData.riskNotes: Thinner wall risks reduced stone-impact and thermal-sag margin; validate chip resistance, -40C impact and 90C sag. Clip pull-off must be re-verified after downgauge. PCR core lot-to-lot variation controlled by spec and incoming inspection to avoid surface read-through.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr 001 lightweight cladding material strategy, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ELV recyclate-content targets (EU End-of-Life Vehicles); PCR core supports recycled-content compliance

title: Flush deployable door handle to fixed paddle simplification
description: Replace motorised flush-deploy handle with a fixed semi-flush mechanical paddle plus capacitive unlock, deleting the actuator motor, gear train, anti-pinch sensor and 5-wire harness per door. Saves ~£18/vehicle across four doors and removes a recurring warranty driver in cold climates. Frees four LIN nodes. Benchmarks Xpeng's simplified handle design on G6.

ideaData.title: Flush deployable door handle to fixed paddle simplification
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline is a motorised flush-deploy door handle with a DC actuator, gear train, anti-pinch sensor and 5-wire harness per door, presenting and retracting the paddle. The change is a fixed semi-flush mechanical paddle with capacitive unlock, deleting the actuator motor, gear train, anti-pinch sensor and most of the harness per door. A mechanical Bowden/latch release retains crash-safe operation; a capacitive pad on the paddle handles approach unlock. Removes a recurring cold-climate freeze/ice warranty driver and frees four LIN nodes (one per door). Saves ~£18/veh across four doors in motors, sensors, gears and copper, plus assembly simplification.
ideaData.materialGrade: Mechanical paddle (PA66-GF + zinc latch interface), capacitive sensor
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes per-door motor, gear train, anti-pinch sensor and 3 of 5 harness wires; four LIN nodes freed. Door-module assembly simplified, with no actuator calibration at EOL. Lower door-trim mass and packaging. One handle variant across trims reduces SKUs.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Deleting the deploy actuator removes motor/sensor cost and a cold-climate warranty driver.

ideaData.riskNotes: Mechanical paddle must meet inertial-latch (crash) and FMVSS 206/ECE R11 door-retention; validate that capacitive unlock works wet/gloved and resists false triggers. Perceived-quality downgrade vs flush handle, so position on base trim. Aero/Cd delta from semi-flush vs flush quantified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng G6 simplified door-handle, 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: FMVSS 206 / UN ECE R11 door latches & retention; inertial-load latch requirement governs paddle design

title: SMC-to-steel reversal on bumper beam using press-hardened boron
description: Replace aluminium-extrusion bumper beam with a press-hardened 22MnB5 roll-formed beam at 1.4mm, matching crash energy at lower material cost and deleting the MIG-welded crash-can interface in favour of bolted crush cans. Saves ~£6/vehicle versus aluminium and reuses existing PHS line. Benchmarks Hongqi's PHS crash-structure strategy on E-HS9.

ideaData.title: SMC-to-steel reversal on bumper beam using press-hardened boron
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline bumper beam is an extruded 6000-series aluminium section MIG-welded to crash cans. The change replaces it with a press-hardened 22MnB5 boron-steel roll-formed beam at 1.4 mm gauge (austenitised and die-quenched to ~1500 MPa UTS, fully martensitic), matching crash-energy absorption at lower material cost. Bolted crush cans replace the MIG-welded interface, deleting the weld cell and easing repair. The change reuses the existing press-hardening-steel (PHS) line. Despite higher density, the high strength allows a thin section that is cost-competitive; saves ~£6/veh versus aluminium extrusion while keeping the same crash performance envelope.
ideaData.materialGrade: 22MnB5 press-hardened boron steel, ~1500 MPa UTS, 1.4 mm
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Roll-form + press-harden on the existing PHS line replaces aluminium extrusion + MIG weld. Bolted crush cans delete the weld cell and fixtures, improving repairability. Hot-stamping adds austenitising/quench; trimming requires laser or hard-tool cut. One beam variant; reuses installed hot-stamp capacity.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Switching from aluminium extrusion to thin PHS boron beam cuts material cost at equal crash energy.

ideaData.riskNotes: Mass increases vs aluminium, so verify against vehicle weight targets. PHS trimming and hole-making need laser cutting; hydrogen embrittlement and AlSi coating controlled. Crush-can bolt-joint fatigue and low-speed repairability validated to insurance-rating crash modes.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Hongqi E-HS9 PHS crash-structure strategy, 2021; benchmarked

evidenceSources.title: PHS vs Al bumper-beam cost/crash study

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R42 low-speed bumper; RCAR/insurance low-speed crash; pedestrian leg-form (GTR9) at fascia

title: Power-fold mirror delete to manual-fold with PVD glass
description: On base trim, replace power-fold mirror with manual-fold housing retaining heated PVD-coated glass, deleting fold motor, position sensor and 3-wire LIN feed per side. Saves ~£6.8/vehicle and two harness branches; heating stays on a single 2-wire feed. Reduces mirror mass 180g. Benchmarks Li Auto's trim-tiered mirror content strategy.

ideaData.title: Power-fold mirror delete to manual-fold with PVD glass
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline base-trim mirror uses a power-fold mechanism (fold motor, position sensor, 3-wire LIN feed per side) plus heated, PVD-coated glass. The change deletes the power-fold module on base trim, retaining a manual-fold housing with the same heated PVD glass. Removing the fold motor and sensor deletes two harness branches per side; heating stays on a simple 2-wire feed. Mirror mass drops ~180 g per side. The PVD (vacuum-metallised) mirror glass keeps its hydrophobic/anti-glare performance. Saves ~£6.8/veh in motor, sensor, gears and copper, trim-tiered so higher trims keep power-fold.
ideaData.materialGrade: Manual-fold housing (PP/ASA), heated PVD-coated mirror glass
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes fold motor, position sensor and 3-wire LIN feed per side; heating on a 2-wire feed retained. Mirror sub-assembly simplified, ~180 g/side lighter, no fold calibration at EOL. Trim-tiered SKU: shared glass/heater, different housing. Fewer connector cavities in door harness.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Deleting power-fold on base trim removes motor/sensor and wiring while keeping heated glass.

ideaData.riskNotes: Manual fold raises parking-damage exposure and is a perceived-content downgrade, so restrict to base trim. Verify heated-glass feed and PVD-glass durability unchanged. Pedestrian break-away (ECE R26) fold function retained mechanically.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto trim-tiered mirror content strategy, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R46 field-of-view; R26 mirror break-away (pedestrian) retained via manual fold

title: One-piece blow-moulded rear spoiler replacing bonded assembly
description: Convert two-shell ASA spoiler (upper/lower bonded with PUR) to a single gas-assist blow-moulded part with integral mounting studs, deleting the adhesive operation, fixture cure time and one shell tool. Saves ~£3.1/vehicle and 12% mass. Integral channel routes brake-lamp harness. Benchmarks Nio's integrated spoiler moulding on ET5.

ideaData.title: One-piece blow-moulded rear spoiler replacing bonded assembly
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rear spoiler is a two-shell ASA design (upper + lower) bonded with PUR adhesive, requiring a bond fixture and cure time plus two shell tools. The change is a single gas-assist blow-moulded part with integral mounting studs and an internal channel that routes the high-mount brake-lamp harness. Blow moulding forms a hollow, stiff, one-piece spoiler, deleting the adhesive operation, fixture cure and one shell tool. Saves ~£3.1/veh and ~12% mass. Wall-thickness and pinch-off control maintain Class-A and the aero profile. Integral studs remove separate fastener/boss parts; the internal channel deletes external clips for lamp wiring.
ideaData.materialGrade: Blow-moulded ASA/PP (gas-assist), integral studs
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One blow-mould tool replaces two injection shell tools + bond fixture; deletes PUR adhesive dispense and cure-station dwell. Integral studs and harness channel delete brackets/clips. Hollow section is ~12% lighter. Pinch-off/parison control is the new CTQ.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Blow-moulding consolidates a bonded two-shell spoiler into one hollow part, deleting adhesive and a tool.

ideaData.riskNotes: Blow-moulded Class-A surface and wall uniformity are harder than injection; validate gloss, sink and stud pull-out. UV/weatherability of the ASA grade and the aero/downforce profile re-checked. Integral lamp-channel sealing against water ingress verified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio ET5 integrated spoiler moulding, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R26 external projections; CHMSL integration per R48/R7

title: Fender conversion from steel stamping to injection-moulded PA6
description: Replace stamped-steel front fenders with online-paintable PPE/PA blend mouldings, deleting two stamping dies, hemming and e-coat masking, and cutting 2.4kg/vehicle. Self-supporting geometry removes three reinforcement brackets and a corrosion concern. Saves ~£7.6/vehicle at 180k volume. Online-paint grade survives 200C e-coat bake. Benchmarks BMW's thermoplastic fender heritage carried to Neue Klasse.

ideaData.title: Fender conversion from steel stamping to injection-moulded PA6
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline front fenders are stamped steel requiring two dies, hemming, e-coat masking and corrosion protection. The change moulds online-paintable PPE/PA (modified polyphenylene-ether / polyamide blend, e.g. Noryl GTX class) fenders that survive the ~200C e-coat bake and run through the body paint line. Self-supporting geometry removes three reinforcement brackets and the corrosion concern, cutting 2.4 kg/veh. Deleting two stamping dies and hemming saves tooling and process. The blend's high heat-distortion temperature and low CTE keep gap/flush stable through bake. Saves ~£7.6/veh at 180k volume; the ductile material also improves low-speed dent recovery.
ideaData.materialGrade: PPE/PA online-paint blend (Noryl GTX-class), ~200C bake-stable
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes two stamping dies, hemming and e-coat masking for the fender; injection-moulds a paintable part processed on the existing body paint line. Removes 3 reinforcement brackets and corrosion sealer; 2.4 kg lighter. A conductive grade or grounding clips needed for e-stat paint adhesion.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Thermoplastic fenders delete stamping dies, hemming and corrosion process while cutting weight.

ideaData.riskNotes: Thermal expansion mismatch with the steel body causes gap/flush variation hot/cold, so design floating datums and validate -40 to +90C. E-coat-bake dimensional stability and e-stat paint adhesion (conductive primer) verified. Higher material cost offset by tooling/process; volume sensitivity checked.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW thermoplastic fender heritage to Neue Klasse, 2025; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R42 / pedestrian GTR9 (fender edge); paint-shop VOC compatibility

title: Grille consolidation to single closed panel with radar window
description: Replace multi-slat open grille plus separate radar bezel with one closed body-colour panel incorporating a co-moulded radar-transparent window, deleting 9 chrome slats, a mesh insert and the bezel sub-assembly. Saves ~£5.1/vehicle and improves aero by 6 counts. EV closed face needs no airflow opening. Benchmarks Yangwang's sealed front-face design on U8.

ideaData.title: Grille consolidation to single closed panel with radar window
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline grille is a multi-slat open design (9 chrome slats + mesh insert) with a separate radar bezel sub-assembly. The change is one closed body-colour panel with a co-moulded radar-transparent window (low-loss PC/PMMA section tuned for 77 GHz transmission), deleting the chrome slats, mesh and bezel. The EV's closed front face needs no airflow opening, so the sealed panel improves aero by ~6 counts. Radar-window thickness is set to an integer multiple of half-wavelength to minimise insertion loss; metallised decor is avoided over the radar aperture. Saves ~£5.1/veh by consolidating decor and bezel parts into one moulding.
ideaData.materialGrade: Body-colour PC/ASA panel with co-moulded 77 GHz radar window
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Consolidates 9 slats + mesh + bezel into one moulded/painted panel with a co-moulded radar window. Deletes the bezel sub-assembly and slat assembly steps. Radar-window wall thickness and absence of metallisation over the aperture are new CTQs. One part number replaces several.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Closing the EV front face into one panel deletes decor slats and the radar bezel sub-assembly.

ideaData.riskNotes: Radar transmission loss and beam distortion through the painted window are critical, so validate insertion loss, ghost targets and emblem placement with the radar supplier and avoid metallic paint flake over the aperture. Closed-face thermal/airflow to coolers re-checked. Pedestrian and low-speed impact on the panel verified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Yangwang U8 sealed front-face design, 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R42; 77 GHz radar emissions per ETSI/FCC; pedestrian GTR9 at front face

title: Bumper energy absorber from EPP to ribbed PP structural foam
description: Replace moulded EPP pedestrian absorber with an injection-moulded ribbed PP structural-foam crash box tuned to the same lower-leg impact curve, deleting a separate bead-foam supplier and assembly clip-in step. Saves ~£2.4/vehicle and integrates into the fascia mould family. Benchmarks Denza's integrated absorber rib design on N7.

ideaData.title: Bumper energy absorber from EPP to ribbed PP structural foam
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline pedestrian-protection absorber is a moulded EPP (expanded polypropylene bead-foam) block sourced from a separate supplier and clipped into the fascia. The change is an injection-moulded ribbed PP structural-foam crash box tuned via rib geometry to the same lower-leg impact force/deflection curve, deleting the bead-foam supplier and the clip-in step. The structural-foam process (chemical/physical blowing agent) gives controlled crush with lower density. It integrates into the existing fascia mould family, so tooling and logistics simplify. Saves ~£2.4/veh by removing a second supplier and an assembly operation while meeting GTR9 lower-leg criteria.
ideaData.materialGrade: Injection structural-foam PP (ribbed energy absorber)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces the bead-foam EPP supplier and clip-in step with an injection structural-foam part in the existing fascia mould family. Deletes a supplier interface and one assembly operation; rib-tuned crush replaces foam-density tuning. Single-source within the fascia tool family lowers logistics.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Ribbed structural-foam absorber deletes the EPP supplier and assembly clip-in.

ideaData.riskNotes: Lower-leg (GTR9) impact tuning via ribs is geometry-sensitive, so validate across temperature (-20 to +40C) and impactor angles. Structural-foam surface/density consistency controlled by gas-counter-pressure. Packaging fit within the fascia and AGS clearance verified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Denza N7 integrated absorber rib design, 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN GTR9 / ECE R127 pedestrian lower-leg protection governs absorber tuning

title: Full bumper system standardisation across two body styles
description: Carry one rear bumper system (fascia, beam, absorber, sensor brackets) across sedan and wagon by aligning lamp interfaces and tow-point, deleting one full tool set and halving sensor-bracket SKUs. Saves ~£11/vehicle blended plus £0.94M tooling avoidance. Common harness routing cuts two part numbers. Benchmarks Audi's modular bumper carryover across A6 variants.

ideaData.title: Full bumper system standardisation across two body styles
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline runs separate rear bumper systems (fascia, beam, absorber, sensor brackets, harness) for sedan and wagon. The change carries one rear bumper system across both body styles by aligning lamp interfaces and the tow-point so the beam, absorber and sensor-bracket geometry are common, deleting one full tool set and halving sensor-bracket SKUs. Common harness routing removes two part numbers. The structural beam and crash cans are validated to the worse-case body style. Saves ~£11/veh blended through volume aggregation plus £0.94M tooling avoidance, simplifying aftersales and assembly to a single configuration.
ideaData.materialGrade: Common PP-LGF beam + PP-TD fascia (shared across body styles)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One tool set instead of two; sensor-bracket SKUs halved and two harness part numbers deleted. Single line configuration and common bins across sedan/wagon. Tooling avoidance ~£0.94M. Validation to worst-case body style; aftersales carries one rear-bumper system.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Carrying one rear bumper across two body styles aggregates volume and deletes a tool set.

ideaData.riskNotes: The common system must pass crash and sensor-coverage for both body styles, sizing to the worst case (potential slight over-design on the lighter variant). Styling is constrained by shared lamp/tow interfaces; align early with design. Tow-point load case verified for both.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi A6 modular bumper carryover across variants, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R42 low-speed; RCAR low-speed crash validated for both body styles

title: Single-board rear lamp replacing PCB-plus-flex stack
description: Consolidate the rear-lamp PCB plus two flex tails into one rigid metal-core PCB hosting tail, stop, indicator and reverse LEDs, deleting two FFC connectors and a flex circuit per lamp. Saves ~£4.1/vehicle in flex, connectors and solder joints and improves thermal pull from LEDs. One LIN node per lamp retained. Benchmarks Mercedes' single-board rear-lamp architecture on EQE.

ideaData.title: Single-board rear lamp replacing PCB-plus-flex stack
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rear lamp uses an FR4 PCB plus two flex (FFC) tails and connectors linking sub-boards for tail, stop, indicator and reverse LEDs. The change consolidates onto one rigid IMS/MCPCB (insulated-metal-substrate / metal-core PCB, aluminium base) hosting all LED functions, deleting two FFC connectors and a flex circuit per lamp. The metal core improves thermal pull from the LEDs (lower junction temperature, longer life). One LIN node per lamp is retained. Saves ~£4.1/veh in flex, connectors and solder joints, and improves first-pass yield by removing flex-to-board joints. Single-board layout simplifies optical alignment of emitters.
ideaData.materialGrade: Aluminium-core MCPCB (IMS), single-board
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces FR4 + 2 flex tails + connectors with one MCPCB per lamp; deletes two FFC mating operations and flex handling. The metal core lowers LED junction temperature, easing thermal derating. Fewer solder joints raise SMT first-pass yield; one board reduces lamp assembly steps.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A single metal-core board deletes flex tails/connectors and improves LED thermal life.

ideaData.riskNotes: MCPCB rigidity vs the lamp 3D form may need board partitioning or a bent-metal core; validate fit and the thermal interface to the housing. A single board concentrates failure, so test for solder-joint fatigue under thermal cycling. ECE R7 photometry re-validated after optical re-layout.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes EQE single-board rear-lamp architecture, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R7 (tail/stop/reverse) and R6 (indicator) photometric/colorimetric limits

title: Shared LED driver across headlamp low/high/DRL channels
description: Replace three discrete constant-current drivers with one multi-channel buck driver IC time-sharing low-beam, high-beam and DRL strings, deleting two driver ICs, their inductors and EMC filters per headlamp. Saves ~£4.8/vehicle and 1.6W standby. Single SPI control reduces connector pins from 8 to 5. Benchmarks Audi's shared-driver Matrix LED module on Q6 e-tron.

ideaData.title: Shared LED driver across headlamp low/high/DRL channels
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline headlamp uses three discrete constant-current LED drivers (low-beam, high-beam, DRL), each with its own inductor and EMC filter. The change uses one multi-channel buck driver IC time-sharing the three LED strings under SPI control, deleting two driver ICs, their inductors and EMC filters per headlamp. Because low, high and DRL are not all at full output simultaneously, a shared multi-output driver covers the duty with margin. Saves ~£4.8/veh and ~1.6 W standby; SPI control reduces connector pins from 8 to 5. Per-channel current-sense maintains regulation and fault detection across the shared driver.
ideaData.materialGrade: Multi-channel buck LED driver IC (shared), SPI
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces 3 driver ICs + inductors + EMC filters with one multi-channel driver per headlamp; BOM and board area shrink. Connector drops 8->5 pins. Fewer magnetics ease SMT placement and EMC tuning. Single firmware controls all channels.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One shared multi-channel driver replaces three discrete drivers, cutting ICs, magnetics and pins.

ideaData.riskNotes: The shared driver must supply worst-case simultaneous loads (e.g. high-beam + DRL) with thermal headroom, so size for peak and validate junction temperature. Channel cross-talk and dimming flicker (PWM) verified to EMC/flicker specs. A single-point failure affects multiple functions; a fail-operational strategy is defined.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi Q6 e-tron shared-driver Matrix LED module, 2024; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R48 installation; R112/R123 (AFS) photometry; CISPR 25 EMC for driver

title: Micro-LED matrix headlamp module replacing DMD projector
description: Replace 1.3MP DLP-DMD adaptive projector with a monolithic 25k-pixel micro-LED matrix module, deleting the DMD chip, relay optics and dedicated cooling fan while collapsing optics into one lens. Saves ~£29/vehicle at scale and 9W. Single high-density driver replaces three boards. Benchmarks Mercedes' Digital Light micro-LED roadmap.

ideaData.title: Micro-LED matrix headlamp module replacing DMD projector
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline adaptive high-beam uses a 1.3 MP DLP-DMD (digital micromirror device) projector with relay optics, a light engine and a dedicated cooling fan. The change is a monolithic ~25k-pixel micro-LED matrix module (a directly emissive addressable die array, e.g. uAFS-class) with a single projection lens, deleting the DMD chip, relay optics and fan. Direct emission collapses the optical train into one lens and one high-density driver replaces three boards. Saves ~£29/veh at scale and ~9 W. Pixel-level addressing gives glare-free high beam and projection without moving micromirrors, improving reliability by deleting the fan and DMD.
ideaData.materialGrade: Monolithic micro-LED matrix (~25k px, uAFS-class), PC lens
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the DMD chip, relay optics and cooling fan; collapses optics to one lens. One high-density driver board replaces three. Fewer optical-alignment steps; no fan assembly/seal. New die-attach and thermal-interface process for the high-flux matrix emitter.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A monolithic micro-LED matrix removes the DMD, relay optics and fan for a far simpler optical engine.

ideaData.riskNotes: Micro-LED matrix maturity, thermal density and per-pixel binning/yield are the main risks; needs robust supply and burn-in screening. High-flux thermal management without a fan must be validated. ECE R48/R123 glare-free and beam-pattern compliance re-certified; high upfront NRE.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes Digital Light micro-LED roadmap, 2024; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R123 (AFS) / R48; glare-free high-beam and projection limits govern matrix control

title: DRL light-guide from machined PMMA to moulded PC light pipe
description: Switch signature DRL light guide from polished PMMA to optical-grade PC with moulded-in prism extraction features, deleting a secondary diamond-cutting step and improving thermal robustness near LEDs. Saves ~£2/vehicle and one fixture. Fewer LEDs needed (8 vs 11) for equal luminance via optimised coupling. Benchmarks Porsche's PC light-guide DRL on Taycan.

ideaData.title: DRL light-guide from machined PMMA to moulded PC light pipe
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline signature DRL light guide is polished PMMA with a secondary diamond-cut (machined) extraction feature. The change moulds the guide from optical-grade polycarbonate with moulded-in prism extraction features, deleting the diamond-cutting step and improving thermal robustness near LEDs (PC's higher HDT). Optimised in-mould prisms improve coupling efficiency, so equal luminance is achieved with 8 LEDs versus 11. Saves ~£2/veh and one fixture. PC's higher heat resistance allows tighter LED-to-guide spacing. A total-internal-reflection design plus moulded extractors gives a uniform lit signature without post-machining.
ideaData.materialGrade: Optical-grade polycarbonate (moulded-in prism extractors)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the secondary diamond-cutting operation and its fixture; moulds extraction prisms in-tool. PC tolerates higher LED hot-spot temperatures, easing packaging. Fewer LEDs (8 vs 11) reduce SMT placements and driver current. The optical tool requires precision prism EDM but is a one-time cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Moulded-in PC light pipe deletes diamond machining and cuts LED count via better coupling.

ideaData.riskNotes: PC yellows under UV more than PMMA, so it needs a UV-stabilised grade and validation to avoid colour shift on a lit signature. Moulded-prism uniformity vs diamond-cut must meet appearance/photometry; tool prism quality is critical. LED de-rating at higher temperature verified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Porsche Taycan PC light-guide DRL, 2021; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R7 (DRL) photometry and colorimetry; R48 installation

title: Rear lamp LED count reduction via secondary optics redesign
description: Redesign rear-lamp reflector cavities with a microlens collimating film to cut tail/stop LED count from 24 to 16 while meeting ECE R7 intensity, lowering LED BOM and driver current 30%. Saves ~£2.6/vehicle and 2.4W. Fewer solder joints raise first-pass yield. Benchmarks BYD's optimised rear-lamp optics on Seal.

ideaData.title: Rear lamp LED count reduction via secondary optics redesign
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rear lamp uses 24 LEDs across tail/stop functions in conventional reflector cavities. The change redesigns the reflector optics with a microlens collimating film (MLA) to raise on-axis intensity and uniformity, cutting tail/stop LED count from 24 to 16 while meeting ECE R7 intensity, and lowering driver current ~30%. Better optical efficiency means fewer emitters deliver the same candela. Saves ~£2.6/veh and ~2.4 W; fewer solder joints raise SMT first-pass yield. The microlens film is laminated over the exit aperture, redistributing flux without extra LEDs.
ideaData.materialGrade: Microlens-array collimating film over reflector optics
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: LED count 24->16 reduces SMT placements, solder joints and driver current ~30%; first-pass yield improves. Adds a microlens-film lamination step over the aperture. Same lamp housing; the optical-efficiency gain offsets fewer emitters. Lower thermal load eases board design.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Microlens optics deliver equal intensity with 33% fewer LEDs, cutting BOM and current.

ideaData.riskNotes: The microlens film must hold uniformity and avoid hot-spots/visible pixelation; validate ECE R7 intensity, distribution and homogeneity. Film adhesion/yellowing over temperature and UV verified. Reduced redundancy means a single-LED failure is more visible, so failure-mode lighting is checked.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD Seal optimised rear-lamp optics, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R7 tail/stop photometric intensity and distribution

title: Headlamp leveling stepper delete via static beam mapping
description: Remove the dynamic auto-leveling stepper motor and ride-height sensor on matrix variants by using pixel-level vertical cutoff control in software, deleting two motors, one sensor and a 4-wire feed per car. Saves ~£6/vehicle and frees ECU PWM channels. Software cutoff meets ECE R48 static aim. Benchmarks Audi's software-leveled Matrix headlamp.

ideaData.title: Headlamp leveling stepper delete via static beam mapping
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline matrix-variant headlamps include a dynamic auto-leveling stepper motor and a ride-height sensor with a 4-wire feed per car, mechanically tilting the module to hold the cutoff. On addressable matrix headlamps the change deletes the stepper and ride-height sensor, using pixel-level vertical cutoff control in software to keep the beam aimed as load changes, deleting two motors, one sensor and the 4-wire feed. The matrix already addresses rows of LEDs, so vertical aim is shifted electronically. Saves ~£6/veh and frees ECU PWM channels. Software cutoff meets ECE R48 static-aim requirements; basic levelling is retained electronically.
ideaData.materialGrade: Software pixel-cutoff control (no leveling stepper)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes two leveling steppers, the ride-height sensor and a 4-wire feed per car; ECU PWM channels freed. No mechanical leveling mechanism to assemble or aim at EOL, so software calibration replaces it. Fewer moving parts in the lamp lower warranty and assembly time.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Pixel-level software cutoff removes the leveling stepper and ride-height sensor.

ideaData.riskNotes: Regulators require automatic levelling for high-output sources; the software cutoff must be type-approved as equivalent under ECE R48 and not reduce range when laden. Dynamic vs static-only levelling acceptance varies by market, so verify. Vertical pixel resolution must give fine-enough aim steps.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi software-leveled Matrix headlamp, 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R48 automatic headlamp levelling; software cutoff must meet static-aim/levelling equivalence

title: Full lighting system controller centralisation to zonal node
description: Move headlamp and rear-lamp control logic out of four in-lamp ECUs into the front and rear zonal controllers, leaving only LED drivers in the lamps. Deletes 4 microcontrollers, their CAN transceivers and bootloaders, saving ~£9.3/vehicle and cutting lamp warranty returns. Lamps drop to LIN slaves. Benchmarks Xpeng's centralised lighting control on G9.

ideaData.title: Full lighting system controller centralisation to zonal node
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline lighting has four in-lamp ECUs (microcontroller + CAN transceiver + bootloader per headlamp/rear lamp) running control logic. The change moves headlamp and rear-lamp control logic into the front and rear zonal controllers, leaving only LED drivers in the lamps as LIN slaves. This deletes four microcontrollers, their CAN transceivers and bootloaders, saving ~£9.3/veh and cutting lamp warranty returns (electronics out of the harsh, hot, sealed lamp). Lamps drop to dumb LIN slaves driven by the zonal node. Centralised logic also enables OTA updates and shared diagnostics. Functional safety (matrix glare) is handled in the zonal controller.
ideaData.materialGrade: Zonal-hosted lighting control; lamps as LIN slaves (driver-only)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes 4 lamp MCUs, CAN transceivers and bootloaders; lamps become LIN-slave driver boards. Lamp BOM and PCB area shrink; warranty falls as electronics leave the hot sealed lamp. Software/control consolidates into existing zonal controllers (no new ECU). Simpler EOL flashing (zonal, OTA-capable).

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Centralising lamp logic into zonal controllers deletes four lamp ECUs and cuts warranty.

ideaData.riskNotes: Matrix glare-free control is safety-relevant (ASIL); moving it to the zonal controller requires functional-safety re-allocation and adequate compute/latency. LIN bandwidth for high-channel-count matrix may constrain, so verify timing or use a higher-rate link. Lamp/zonal interface standardisation needed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng G9 centralised lighting control, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R48/R123 glare-free control as ISO 26262 ASIL-B function in zonal controller

title: DRL and turn signal combined on shared multiplexed LED string
description: Combine white DRL and amber turn into RGBW LEDs on a single string driven by one channel with time-multiplexing, deleting a separate amber LED row and its driver lane. Saves ~£2.2/vehicle and reduces lamp depth 4mm. Meets ECE colorimetry on switchover. Benchmarks Hongqi's combined DRL/indicator signature on H9.

ideaData.title: DRL and turn signal combined on shared multiplexed LED string
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline front lighting uses a separate white DRL LED string and an amber turn-signal string, each with its own driver lane. The change uses RGBW LEDs on a single string driven by one channel with time-multiplexing, switching the same emitters between white DRL and amber turn, deleting the separate amber row and its driver lane. ECE colorimetry is met at switchover by driving the amber sub-die. Saves ~£2.2/veh and reduces lamp depth ~4 mm (one optical row instead of two). The shared string is sequenced so DRL dims when the indicator activates, complying with phase/intensity rules.
ideaData.materialGrade: RGBW LED string (shared DRL/turn), single-channel driver
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the separate amber LED row and its driver lane; one optical row reduces lamp depth ~4 mm. A single-channel driver and string lower BOM and board area. Sequencing handled in firmware. Fewer emitters and one optic simplify lamp assembly.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: An RGBW time-multiplexed string combines DRL and turn signal on one channel.

ideaData.riskNotes: ECE requires DRL to switch off or dim when the adjacent indicator operates and colour boundaries to be clean, so validate colorimetry, switch timing and no perceived colour bleed. RGBW binning and colour stability over temperature controlled. A single-string failure affects two functions; failure modes assessed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Hongqi H9 combined DRL/indicator signature, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R7 (DRL/turn) colorimetry and DRL-dim-on-indicator requirement

title: Headlamp housing from PC to filled PA6 for bracket integration
description: Switch headlamp housing from PC to 30% glass-filled PA6, raising stiffness so the three aiming brackets and a separate heat shield mould in directly, deleting four metal inserts and one assembly step. Saves ~£2.7/vehicle and 90g. Higher HDT tolerates LED hot-spots. Benchmarks Volvo's integrated headlamp housing on EX90.

ideaData.title: Headlamp housing from PC to filled PA6 for bracket integration
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline headlamp housing is polycarbonate with three separate metal aiming brackets and a discrete heat shield assembled in. The change switches the housing to PA6-GF30 (30% glass-filled polyamide-6, ~9 GPa flex modulus, higher HDT), whose stiffness lets the three aiming brackets and the heat shield mould directly into the housing, deleting four metal inserts and one assembly step. The higher heat-distortion temperature of GF-PA6 tolerates LED hot-spots better than PC. Saves ~£2.7/veh and ~90 g. Glass reinforcement provides the dimensional stability for aiming datums previously needing metal. The housing is non-visible (behind the lens), so PA6's surface is acceptable.
ideaData.materialGrade: PA6-GF30 (30% glass-filled polyamide-6) housing
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Moulds 3 aiming brackets + heat shield into the housing, deleting 4 metal inserts and one assembly operation. Higher HDT handles LED hot-spots; glass fill gives aiming-datum stability. Slightly heavier than PC but offset by deleted inserts (~90 g net saving). Tooling adds moulded-in bracket detail.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A GF-PA6 housing integrates aiming brackets and heat shield, deleting metal inserts.

ideaData.riskNotes: PA6 absorbs moisture, shifting dimensions, so aiming-datum stability across humidity must be validated; consider PA6-GF or PPA if margin is tight. Moulded-in brackets need stiffness/creep verification at lamp temperature. Adhesion of lens bonding to PA6 vs PC checked.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Volvo EX90 integrated headlamp housing, 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R48 aim stability; housing dimensional stability supports beam-aim compliance

title: Rear fog and reverse merged into single multifunction LED
description: Replace separate rear-fog and reverse modules with one dual-intensity white/red multifunction LED cluster in the bumper, deleting one reflector cavity, one driver lane and a connector. Saves ~£1.6/vehicle and one harness branch. Software arbitrates ECE-legal functions. Benchmarks AITO's consolidated rear signature on M7.

ideaData.title: Rear fog and reverse merged into single multifunction LED
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline rear has separate rear-fog and reverse modules, each with its own reflector cavity, driver lane and connector. The change merges them into one dual-intensity multifunction LED cluster in the bumper that produces high-intensity red (fog) and white (reverse) from a shared module, deleting one reflector cavity, one driver lane and a connector. Software arbitrates the ECE-legal functions (mutually exclusive use). Saves ~£1.6/veh and one harness branch. A bi-colour or dual-string emitter with shared optics covers both functions; intensity and colour switch under control. Consolidation cuts lamp depth and part count.
ideaData.materialGrade: Multifunction red/white LED cluster (shared optic)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes one reflector cavity, one driver lane and a connector; one module replaces two. One harness branch removed. Software arbitration of fog/reverse. Fewer cavities reduce lamp tooling and assembly; a shared optic simplifies alignment.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Merging rear-fog and reverse into one cluster deletes a cavity, driver lane and connector.

ideaData.riskNotes: ECE separation/positioning rules for rear-fog vs reverse and colour boundaries must be met, so validate photometry, colorimetry and that the functions cannot illuminate non-compliantly together. Red/white crosstalk through the shared optic controlled. A single-module failure affects two functions; arbitration logic verified.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AITO M7 consolidated rear signature, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R7 (reverse white / rear-fog red) photometry, colour and positioning rules

title: Matrix LED module count reduction via wider pixel pitch optics
description: Reduce matrix high-beam from 84 to 48 pixels using projection optics with finer angular resolution, holding glare-free performance while cutting LED and driver-channel count 43%. Saves ~£10/vehicle in emitters and silicon and 6W. One driver board removed. Benchmarks Audi's pixel-optimised Matrix on A6 e-tron.

ideaData.title: Matrix LED module count reduction via wider pixel pitch optics
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline matrix high-beam uses 84 individually addressable pixels with their drivers. The change reduces the matrix to 48 pixels by adopting projection optics with finer angular resolution per pixel, holding glare-free performance while cutting LED and driver-channel count 43%. Higher-quality projection optics map fewer, better-aimed pixels to the same angular coverage. Saves ~£10/veh in emitters and matrix-driver silicon and ~6 W; one driver board is removed. Glare-free masking around oncoming traffic is preserved by the sharper per-pixel boundaries. The trade is more precise (costlier) optics for far fewer (costlier) pixels and channels, net positive.
ideaData.materialGrade: 48-pixel matrix module with high-resolution projection optics
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Pixel count 84->48 cuts LED emitters and matrix-driver channels ~43%; one driver board removed. Optics upgraded to higher angular resolution (one-time tooling). Fewer channels lower board area, current and ~6 W. Simpler driver thermal design.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Better optics let 48 pixels match 84-pixel glare-free coverage, cutting emitters and drivers.

ideaData.riskNotes: A coarser pixel grid risks glare-control granularity, so validate ECE R123 glare-free transitions and dark-zone sharpness with the new optics. Optical-alignment tolerance is tighter with fewer, larger pixels. Edge-of-beam scatter and stray light checked; type-approval re-run.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Audi A6 e-tron pixel-optimised Matrix, 2024; benchmarked

evidenceSources.title: Matrix LED optics vs pixel-count trade

ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R123 (AFS) glare-free high-beam transition and dark-zone requirements

title: Full headlamp system carryover with software-defined signatures
description: Standardise one hardware headlamp system across trims and differentiate DRL/welcome signatures purely in firmware, deleting three lamp variants and their tooling. Saves ~£8.5/vehicle blended plus £0.77M tool avoidance and simplifies aftersales to one part number. Addressable LED matrix enables per-trim animations. Benchmarks Zeekr's software-defined lighting strategy.

ideaData.title: Full headlamp system carryover with software-defined signatures
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline carries three hardware headlamp variants across trims, each with its own tooling. The change standardises one hardware headlamp system across all trims and differentiates DRL/welcome signatures and animations purely in firmware on an addressable LED matrix, deleting the three lamp variants and their tooling. One part number simplifies aftersales. Saves ~£8.5/veh blended (volume aggregation on one design) plus ~£0.77M tool avoidance. Per-trim look becomes a software feature (animations, signature shapes) on common hardware. The addressable matrix gives the design freedom previously achieved with different optics, removing variant tooling.
ideaData.materialGrade: Common addressable-matrix headlamp HW; firmware-defined signatures
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: One headlamp hardware design replaces three trim variants; their tooling is avoided (~£0.77M). A single part number across trims cuts SKUs, line-side complexity and aftersales stock. Differentiation is a firmware load at EOL/OTA, no hardware change. Volume aggregation lowers unit cost.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: One hardware lamp differentiated in firmware deletes trim-specific variants and tooling.

ideaData.riskNotes: Common hardware must satisfy the highest-trim feature set (potential cost-up on base trim). Firmware-defined signatures must each pass ECE R7/R48 (no animation during driving DRL). Feature-gating/licensing and OTA signature management add software governance. Styling buy-in for one shared form.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr software-defined lighting strategy, 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: UN ECE R7/R48; DRL/welcome animations must be static/compliant while driving

title: Aluminium body harness replacing copper on high-gauge runs
description: Convert main floor and roof harness branches above 2.5mm² from copper to aluminium with ultrasonic-welded Cu transition terminals, cutting ~2.1kg conductor mass and ~£12/vehicle at current Cu prices. Crimp creep mitigated by laser-welded ferrules. Targets 35% of harness copper. Benchmarks Mercedes' aluminium high-current harness on EQS.

ideaData.title: Aluminium body harness replacing copper on high-gauge runs
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline body harness uses copper conductors throughout, including main floor and roof branches above 2.5 mm2. The change converts those high-gauge runs to aluminium (EN AW-1370/8030-class) with ultrasonic-welded copper transition terminals, cutting ~2.1 kg conductor mass and ~£12/veh at current copper prices. Aluminium has ~60% of copper's conductivity, so cross-section is up-sized one to two gauges, but mass and cost still fall on the heavy runs. Crimp creep and galvanic corrosion at the Al-Cu joint are mitigated by laser-welded ferrules and sealed transition terminals. Targets ~35% of harness copper (the thick, long runs where Al pays off).
ideaData.materialGrade: EN AW-8030/1370 aluminium conductor, Cu transition terminals
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Converts >2.5 mm2 floor/roof runs to aluminium with ultrasonic-weld/laser-weld Cu transition terminals; ~2.1 kg lighter. Up-sized cross-section but lower cost/mass. New Al-termination process and joint inspection added. Targets ~35% of copper content on heavy runs; thin signal wires stay copper.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Aluminium on heavy harness runs cuts conductor cost and mass versus copper.

ideaData.riskNotes: Al-Cu joints risk galvanic corrosion and crimp creep/relaxation causing resistive hot-spots; mitigate with ultrasonic/laser-welded sealed terminals and current-derating. Aluminium fatigue under vibration validated. Termination process control (weld quality) is the key reliability gate; copper-price sensitivity on the business case.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes EQS aluminium high-current harness, 2021; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 6722 / LV112 conductor & termination standards for automotive aluminium wiring

title: Zonal E/E architecture replacing domain controllers
description: Migrate from seven domain ECUs to four zonal controllers plus a central compute, shortening harness by ~340m and ~9kg per vehicle while deleting ~20 ECUs through function consolidation. Cuts ~£41/vehicle in silicon, connectors and copper. Power and data ride short zonal stubs. Benchmarks Xpeng's zonal X-EEA 3.0 architecture on G6.

ideaData.title: Zonal E/E architecture replacing domain controllers
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline E/E uses seven domain ECUs (powertrain, chassis, body, ADAS, infotainment, etc.) with long point-to-point harnesses. The change migrates to four zonal controllers plus a central compute, where each zone aggregates local I/O and power and talks to central compute over high-speed automotive Ethernet, shortening harness by ~340 m and ~9 kg/veh and deleting ~20 ECUs via function consolidation into software. Cuts ~£41/veh in silicon, connectors and copper. Power and data ride short zonal stubs to nearby loads instead of long runs to a central node. A service-oriented architecture moves functions to software, enabling OTA.
ideaData.materialGrade: 4 zonal controllers + central compute, automotive Ethernet backbone
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes ~20 ECUs and ~340 m harness (~9 kg); zonal stubs replace long point-to-point runs. Far fewer connectors and splices ease automated assembly. Central compute + zonal nodes standardise hardware; functions become software. Major architecture re-platform with new harness topology and EOL flashing flow.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Zonal architecture consolidates ECUs and shortens harness, cutting silicon, copper and connectors.

ideaData.riskNotes: Major re-platform: functional-safety (ISO 26262) and cybersecurity (ISO 21434) re-allocation across zonal/central compute, fail-operational power and network redundancy, and software-integration risk. Time-sensitive networking (TSN) latency for control functions validated. High NRE and supplier-ecosystem dependency.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Xpeng X-EEA 3.0 zonal architecture, G6 2023; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 functional safety & ISO/SAE 21434 cybersecurity for centralised compute; UNECE R155/R156

title: FFC flat-cable replacing round harness in door modules
description: Replace round multi-core door harness with laminated FFC flat cable terminating in ZIF connectors, deleting the rubber boot grommet, tape wrap and 60% of door-zone copper mass. Saves ~£6/vehicle and 280g per door, easing flush-handle and speaker routing. Benchmarks Nio's flat-cable door architecture on ET7.

ideaData.title: FFC flat-cable replacing round harness in door modules
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline door harness is a round multi-core bundle with a rubber boot grommet at the hinge and tape wrap. The change replaces it with a laminated FFC (flexible flat cable) terminating in ZIF (zero-insertion-force) connectors, deleting the rubber boot grommet, tape wrap and ~60% of door-zone copper mass. The flat profile flexes flat at the hinge and packages thin behind trim, easing flush-handle and speaker routing. Saves ~£6/veh and ~280 g per door. FFC's defined conductor geometry improves repeatability and automated assembly; the hinge flex life is met by a rated flat-cable construction. ZIF terminations cut crimp count.
ideaData.materialGrade: Laminated FFC flat cable (Cu foil), ZIF connectors
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces round harness + grommet + tape with FFC + ZIF; ~280 g/door lighter and ~60% less door copper. Deletes grommet and tape-wrap operations. The flat profile eases routing behind trim and automated lay-in. ZIF terminations reduce crimping. New flat-cable handling/jig at the door line.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: FFC flat cable cuts door copper, grommet and tape while easing thin routing.

ideaData.riskNotes: Door-hinge flex fatigue is the main risk, so validate flat-cable bend life (>=100k cycles), water sealing without the boot grommet, and current capacity of foil conductors. ZIF connector retention under vibration verified. Repairability differs from round harness; service procedure defined.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio ET7 flat-cable door architecture, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 6722 cable & water-ingress (IP) sealing at door pass-through

title: Ring busbar power distribution replacing star fuse boxes
description: Replace two large star-topology fuse boxes and their thick feeders with a laser-welded aluminium ring busbar feeding zonal controllers, deleting ~40 blade fuses, two PDU housings and 4.5kg of feeder copper. Solid-state eFuses replace blades. Saves ~£22/vehicle. Benchmarks BMW Neue Klasse ring-busbar power topology.

ideaData.title: Ring busbar power distribution replacing star fuse boxes
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline power distribution uses two large star-topology fuse boxes (PDUs) with thick feeder cables radiating to loads. The change replaces them with a laser-welded aluminium ring busbar that feeds the zonal controllers, deleting ~40 blade fuses, two PDU housings and ~4.5 kg of feeder copper; solid-state eFuses in the zonal nodes replace blade fuses. The ring topology gives redundant power paths and lets each zone draw locally, cutting long feeder runs. Saves ~£22/veh. The aluminium busbar (laser-welded joints) carries high current at low mass; eFuses add diagnostics and programmable trip, deleting mechanical fuses and their service.
ideaData.materialGrade: Laser-welded aluminium ring busbar; solid-state eFuses
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces 2 star PDUs + thick feeders with one Al ring busbar; deletes ~40 blade fuses, 2 PDU housings and ~4.5 kg feeder copper. eFuses in zonal nodes replace fuse banks. Busbar laser-welding and mounting is a new process; far fewer feeder routings and connectors ease assembly.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: An aluminium ring busbar plus eFuses replaces star PDUs and heavy copper feeders.

ideaData.riskNotes: Ring redundancy and fault isolation (eFuse coordination, fault-current sharing) must be designed and validated; busbar thermal rise and laser-weld joint integrity under vibration verified. Loss of mechanical fuses changes the service/over-current culture; aluminium-busbar corrosion protection required.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BMW Neue Klasse ring-busbar power topology, 2025; benchmarked

evidenceSources.title: Ring busbar vs star PDU study

ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 8820 (fuse) supersession by eFuse; ISO 26262 for power-path fault management

title: Solid-state eFuse replacing blade fuse and relay banks
description: Replace 28 blade fuses and 9 electromechanical relays with high-side smart eFuse switches in the zonal PDU, deleting the relay carrier, fuse holders and manual fuse-pull service. Saves ~£7.6/vehicle, enables current diagnostics and removes a mechanical wear item. Programmable trip curves cut over-spec wiring. Benchmarks Zeekr's eFuse PDU on 001.

ideaData.title: Solid-state eFuse replacing blade fuse and relay banks
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline power switching uses 28 blade fuses and 9 electromechanical relays on a relay/fuse carrier. The change replaces them with high-side solid-state eFuse switches in the zonal PDU, deleting the relay carrier, fuse holders and the manual fuse-pull service. Smart eFuses provide programmable trip curves, current diagnostics and load control in one device per channel, eliminating a mechanical wear item (relays) and blow-once fuses. Saves ~£7.6/veh. Programmable trip lets wiring be sized to actual load rather than next-fuse-up, trimming conductor gauge. Solid-state switching also enables fine load management and predictive diagnostics over the bus.
ideaData.materialGrade: High-side smart eFuse switches (zonal PDU)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes 28 blade fuses, 9 relays, the relay carrier and fuse holders; manual fuse service removed. eFuses integrate switch + protect + diagnostics per channel. Programmable trip allows thinner wire gauges. Fewer mechanical parts ease PDU assembly and improve reliability (no relay wear).

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Smart eFuses replace fuse and relay banks, adding diagnostics and deleting a wear item.

ideaData.riskNotes: eFuse thermal dissipation and short-circuit withstand must match blade-fuse let-through; validate trip coordination, junction temperature and inrush handling for capacitive/motor loads. Quiescent current and fail-safe behaviour verified. Higher unit cost offset by deleted relays/fuses and thinner wire.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Zeekr 001 eFuse PDU, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 8820 fuse equivalence; ISO 26262 protection coordination for solid-state fusing

title: 48V zonal low-current distribution replacing 12V feeders
description: Move body and thermal loads to a 48V backbone, cutting conductor cross-section fourfold for equal power and saving ~5kg copper and ~£16/vehicle. Local 48-to-12V converters feed legacy loads. Lower I²R loss frees 220W. Thinner gauges ease routing. Benchmarks Tesla-style 48V topology mirrored by Zeekr's next-gen 48V architecture.

ideaData.title: 48V zonal low-current distribution replacing 12V feeders
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline body and thermal loads run on a 12 V feeder network. The change moves these loads to a 48 V backbone; for equal power, current drops fourfold, so conductor cross-section is cut ~4x, saving ~5 kg copper and ~£16/veh. Local 48-to-12 V DC-DC converters feed legacy 12 V loads. Lower I2R loss frees ~220 W. Thinner gauges ease routing and lower mass. The 48 V system pairs naturally with zonal architecture (eFuses, high-power body loads). Conductor sizing, connector ratings and arc-mitigation are set for 48 V; legacy 12 V islands remain behind converters during transition.
ideaData.materialGrade: 48 V copper backbone (reduced gauge) + 48->12 V DC-DC
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: A 48 V backbone cuts conductor cross-section ~4x for equal power, ~5 kg lighter; thinner gauges ease routing. Adds local 48->12 V converters for legacy loads. Connector and fuse ratings change to 48 V class. Lower I2R loss (~220 W) eases thermal. Pairs with zonal/eFuse rollout.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: 48 V cuts current 4x for equal power, slashing copper cross-section and mass.

ideaData.riskNotes: 48 V arc/short risk and connector de-rating require new safety practices (arc-mitigation, touch limits below 60 V SELV). DC-DC converter cost/efficiency offsets some copper saving, so optimise placement. Transition islands (mixed 12/48 V) add complexity; a load-migration plan is needed.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Tesla-style 48 V topology mirrored by Zeekr next-gen 48 V, 2024; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: LV148 / 48 V standards; SELV <60 V touch-safety; ISO 6722 conductor rating

title: Central compute consolidating ADAS and infotainment SoCs
description: Fuse separate ADAS and IVI controllers into one central compute with partitioned SoCs sharing power, cooling and housing, deleting one ECU enclosure, its connectors and a 1Gb inter-ECU link. Saves ~£26/vehicle and 1.2kg, cutting two high-speed harnesses. Benchmarks Nio's Adam/central-compute consolidation on ET7.

ideaData.title: Central compute consolidating ADAS and infotainment SoCs
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline uses separate ADAS and infotainment (IVI) controllers, each with its own enclosure, power, cooling and a 1 Gb inter-ECU link. The change fuses them into one central compute with partitioned SoCs (hypervisor-isolated ADAS and IVI domains) sharing power, cooling and housing, deleting one ECU enclosure, its connectors and the inter-ECU link, and cutting two high-speed harnesses. Saves ~£26/veh and ~1.2 kg. A common thermal solution and PMIC serve both SoCs; mixed-criticality isolation keeps ADAS (ASIL) partitioned from IVI (QM). Consolidation removes the cross-link latency and shared compute scales features in software.
ideaData.materialGrade: Central compute, hypervisor-partitioned ADAS+IVI SoCs
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes one ECU enclosure, its connectors and a 1 Gb inter-ECU link; two high-speed harnesses removed (~1.2 kg). Shared power, cooling and housing for both SoCs. One assembly/EOL flash instead of two. Thermal and PMIC consolidation; partitioning handled in software/hypervisor.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Fusing ADAS and IVI into one central compute deletes an enclosure, link and harnesses.

ideaData.riskNotes: Mixed-criticality isolation (ASIL ADAS vs QM IVI) on shared silicon needs a proven hypervisor and freedom-from-interference (ISO 26262); thermal headroom for the combined load and single-point-failure mitigation are critical. The cybersecurity attack surface is larger; supplier lock-in to one SoC platform.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Nio Adam central-compute consolidation, ET7 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 freedom-from-interference for mixed-criticality; ISO/SAE 21434 cybersecurity

title: CAN-FD backbone replacing dual-CAN plus gateway bridging
description: Collapse two classic-CAN buses and a bridging gateway into one CAN-FD backbone running 5Mbit, deleting the gateway ECU, its transceivers and a harness segment. Saves ~£6.8/vehicle and removes a latency hop. Fewer nodes lower termination-resistor count. Benchmarks AVATR's CAN-FD backbone on the 11.

ideaData.title: CAN-FD backbone replacing dual-CAN plus gateway bridging
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline networking uses two classic-CAN (500 kbit) buses bridged by a gateway ECU. The change collapses them into one CAN-FD backbone running 5 Mbit, deleting the gateway ECU, its transceivers and a harness segment. CAN-FD's higher bandwidth and larger payload absorb the combined traffic on one bus, removing the bridging latency hop. Fewer separate buses lower the termination-resistor count. Saves ~£6.8/veh. Node addressing and bus-load budget are re-balanced for the merged segment; legacy classic-CAN nodes either migrate to CAN-FD or sit behind a partial-networking transceiver. Removing the gateway simplifies the topology and the diagnostics path.
ideaData.materialGrade: CAN-FD backbone (5 Mbit), no gateway
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes the gateway ECU, its transceivers and a harness segment; one CAN-FD bus replaces two classic buses + bridge. Fewer termination resistors. Bus-load re-budgeted for merged traffic. Simpler topology lowers connectors and EOL diagnostic routing.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A CAN-FD backbone absorbs two buses, deleting the bridging gateway and a harness segment.

ideaData.riskNotes: The merged bus-load must stay within the timing/latency budget for safety messages, so validate worst-case CAN-FD bus utilisation and arbitration. Mixed classic/CAN-FD node coexistence requires care (partial networking). Loss of gateway segmentation affects cybersecurity zoning; compensate at endpoints.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: AVATR 11 CAN-FD backbone, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 11898 CAN-FD; ISO/SAE 21434 network-segmentation considerations

title: Inline connector reduction via harness branch consolidation
description: Re-architect mid-floor harness to delete 14 inline splice connectors by routing through a single zonal junction, removing terminals, seals and a recurring connector-corrosion failure mode. Saves ~£4.3/vehicle in connector BOM and assembly clip time. Fewer mating cycles raise reliability. Benchmarks Li Auto's consolidated junction harness on L9.

ideaData.title: Inline connector reduction via harness branch consolidation
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline mid-floor harness uses 14 inline splice connectors to branch power and signals. The change re-architects the routing through a single zonal junction so those 14 inline splices are deleted, removing terminals, seals and a recurring connector-corrosion failure mode. Branches join at the zonal node instead of in-line in the harness. Saves ~£4.3/veh in connector BOM and assembly clip time; fewer mating cycles raise reliability. The zonal junction provides a controlled, sealed branching point versus distributed inline connectors prone to moisture and fretting. Harness build simplifies to fewer sub-assemblies feeding one node.
ideaData.materialGrade: Zonal junction branching (deletes inline splice connectors)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Deletes 14 inline splice connectors, their terminals and seals; branches consolidate at one zonal junction. Fewer mating operations and clips cut assembly time. Removes a connector-corrosion warranty mode. Harness simplifies to fewer feeds into the junction; EOL test points reduced.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Routing through one zonal junction deletes 14 inline splices and a corrosion failure mode.

ideaData.riskNotes: Concentrating branches at one junction raises single-point exposure, so validate junction current/thermal capacity and sealing (IP67). Harness flexibility/serviceability vs distributed splices considered. Re-routing must keep circuit lengths within the voltage-drop budget.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Li Auto L9 consolidated junction harness, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 20653 (IP) sealing at junction; connector corrosion per ISO 16750 environmental

title: Optical Ethernet backbone replacing shielded copper for cameras
description: Replace shielded copper coax camera links with a POF optical backbone for 8MP sensors, deleting heavy shielding, ferrites and EMC filtering while cutting 1.3kg and ~£9.3/vehicle. Immune to EMI, easing routing near inverters. Single fibre carries multiple streams. Benchmarks Mercedes' optical-backbone roadmap for high-bandwidth sensors.

ideaData.title: Optical Ethernet backbone replacing shielded copper for cameras
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline 8 MP camera links use shielded copper coax with ferrites and EMC filtering. The change replaces them with a POF (plastic optical fibre) optical backbone, deleting the heavy shielding, ferrites and EMC filtering while cutting ~1.3 kg and ~£9.3/veh. Optical links are immune to EMI, easing routing near inverters and high-current cables, and a single fibre can carry multiple multiplexed streams. POF transceivers convert at each endpoint. Saves copper and shielding mass and improves signal integrity. The optical channel removes ground-loop and emission concerns inherent to high-bandwidth copper coax, simplifying EMC validation.
ideaData.materialGrade: Plastic optical fibre (POF) backbone, optical transceivers
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces shielded coax + ferrites + EMC filters with POF + optical transceivers; ~1.3 kg lighter. EMI immunity eases routing near inverters and simplifies EMC validation. One fibre multiplexes streams, reducing cable count. New optical-connector handling and cleanliness control at assembly.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: Optical POF links delete heavy shielding/ferrites and ease EMC for high-bandwidth cameras.

ideaData.riskNotes: POF temperature range, bend-radius and connector cleanliness/contamination are reliability risks, so validate over -40 to +105C and vibration. Optical-transceiver cost partly offsets savings; latency/jitter for ADAS verified. Repairability and field-splice procedures differ from copper.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Mercedes optical-backbone roadmap for high-bandwidth sensors, 2024; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: CISPR 25 EMC (optical immunity); ISO 21111 automotive Ethernet/optical considerations

title: Battery sense harness to integrated flex busbar CSC
description: Replace discrete cell-sense wires and rigid busbars with a laser-welded flex-PCB cell-supervision circuit integrating voltage taps, temperature pads and busbars, deleting ~90 sense leads and their connectors per pack. Saves ~£14/vehicle and 0.7kg. Benchmarks BYD's integrated flex CSC on Blade pack.

ideaData.title: Battery sense harness to integrated flex busbar CSC
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline battery cell monitoring uses discrete cell-sense wires and rigid busbars with separate connectors. The change replaces them with a laser-welded flex-PCB cell-supervision circuit (CSC) integrating voltage taps, temperature pads and busbars on one flexible laminate, deleting ~90 sense leads and their connectors per pack. The flex bonds/laser-welds directly to cell terminals, combining interconnect and sensing. Saves ~£14/veh and ~0.7 kg. The integrated flex removes hand-routed sense harness and crimps, improving reliability and assembly automation. Voltage/temperature acquisition is integrated, feeding the BMS via fewer connectors; busbar and sense functions are co-located on the laminate.
ideaData.materialGrade: Laser-welded flex-PCB CSC (integrated busbar + sense)
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Replaces ~90 discrete sense leads, connectors and rigid busbars with one flex-PCB CSC laser-welded to cells; ~0.7 kg lighter. Combines busbar + voltage/temp sensing on one laminate, enabling automated module assembly. Deletes hand-routed sense harness and crimps; fewer BMS connectors.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: An integrated flex CSC merges busbars and cell sensing, deleting ~90 sense leads per pack.

ideaData.riskNotes: Laser-weld joint quality to cell terminals and flex fatigue under cell breathing/vibration are critical, so validate weld pull-strength, thermal cycling and isolation. Flex repair is module-level (not single wire). Voltage-sense accuracy and temperature-pad contact verified for BMS safety (ASIL).
ideaData.benchmarkReference: BYD Blade pack integrated flex CSC, 2022; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: ISO 26262 ASIL battery voltage/temperature sensing; UN ECE R100 battery safety

title: Roof zonal controller absorbing body-control functions
description: Add a compact roof zonal controller to absorb lighting, sunroof, mirror and antenna functions previously on a central BCM with long feeders, shortening interior harness ~70m and ~2.3kg. Local LIN stubs replace point-to-point runs. Saves ~£13/vehicle. Benchmarks Maextro's zonal interior architecture on the S800.

ideaData.title: Roof zonal controller absorbing body-control functions
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline interior body functions (lighting, sunroof, mirror, antenna) run on a central BCM with long point-to-point feeders. The change adds a compact roof zonal controller that absorbs these functions locally, shortening interior harness ~70 m and ~2.3 kg. Local LIN stubs from the roof node replace long point-to-point runs to the BCM. Saves ~£13/veh. The roof node aggregates nearby loads (dome lights, sunroof motor, mirror, shark-fin antenna) and communicates upstream over the backbone, removing the longest interior copper runs. Placing the controller near its loads is the core zonal principle, trading a small node for large harness savings.
ideaData.materialGrade: Roof zonal controller + local LIN stubs
ideaData.manufacturingImpact: Adds one roof zonal controller; deletes long point-to-point feeders for roof loads, ~70 m / ~2.3 kg harness removed. Local LIN stubs simplify routing and headliner build. Fewer long runs to the BCM ease assembly; one node carries multiple roof functions, reducing connectors at the BCM.

costSavingPotential.qualitative: A roof zonal node absorbs roof functions, deleting the longest interior feeders.

ideaData.riskNotes: The added node cost must stay below the harness savings; roof-zone thermal (sun load) and EMC near the antenna validated. Functional reallocation from the BCM needs software integration and a fail-safe for safety-relevant loads. Headliner-integrated controller serviceability considered.
ideaData.benchmarkReference: Maextro S800 zonal interior architecture, 2024; benchmarked
ideaData.regulatoryContext: CISPR 25 EMC near antenna; ISO 26262 for any safety-relevant reallocated function

title: Full vehicle harness redesign to flat ribbon spine
description: Replace the conventional branched round harness with a flat laminated ribbon spine carrying power and LIN to zonal taps, deleting tape wrap, conduit and ~30% of connector cavities while enabling automated lay-flat assembly. Cuts ~£25/vehicle and 6kg. Benchmarks Luxeed's flat-harness spine on the R7.

ideaData.title: Full vehicle harness redesign to flat ribbon spine
ideaData.technicalDescription: Baseline is a conventional branched round harness with tape wrap, conduit and many connector cavities.
